Comment: As a social worker working as a practitioner my career pathway is stunted.  I cannot progress to Advanced Mental Health Practitioner because I am not allowed to undertake the Non- Medical Prescriber training as I am not a Nurse.  I could do the Approved Mental Health Practitioner Training, but this would be precluded as it is not needed for the service I work in.  I have done what management training is available but this does not progress my career.  I did previously raise this concern with [name removed] a couple of years ago but nothing has changed at all.  Staff hours are taken up with far too many unnecessary meetings that reduce clinical activity and increase waiting times.  Managers are nurses who have been promoted without any business acumen and often without management training.  Induction processes are sadly lacking and new staff are thrown in at the deep end and often leave soon after.  The level of staff resigning due to poor management is excessive and exit interviews should be more radically adhered to and action taken to improve working environments and work pressures.  Increased staffing isn't always the answer.  Mersey care sadly has a policy that if your face fits and you say yes to everything rather than question things you do not progress the level of incestuous progression has always been rife since I first joined the organisation in [date removed].  I moved over to[location removed] which was far more fair and progressive but now Mersey care have taken over I see the same issues creeping in here.  I have recently moved to a newly developing team and so far so good the other staff and managers have a positive outlook on patient care as a priority and I will be happy to stay here to support its development until my retirement.

Career progression is limited because it is not offered to all, just those that tick the right boxes or do certain roles.  Changing your role or seeking training whilst supporting a family is extremely difficult.
Changes to management structure are risky, dangerous and creating a closed culture.  It's very concerning.
Communicate more and recognise concerns because this trust is bleeding good staff.
Currently happy in my role.

From my own experience and speaking to colleagues, where staff have experienced a lack of respect and civility in the workplace it seems to be most common when interacting with medical staff and many people still find this difficult to challenge due to the power difference.  Whilst hopefully a minority, I think this is something that needs addressing in this staff group.
Healthier food options at Hollins Park please, salad is not often available, cooked meals are fine but too heavy for lunchtime, need lighter options with plenty of fresh salad available.
I am generally happy with the organisation and the team I work in.  However following the Southport attack and the Riots, I did not feel supported by my team.
I am on a fixed term contract which is why I am looking for other roles.

I do not feel that the surveys accomplish anything solid.  We still do not have the correct working environment or the proper tools to do the job as effectively as we could.  So called flexible working does not appear to be that flexible and is causing more work place stress and complications for staff.

I enjoy my job and I have a brilliant manager.  I think the organisation I work for works very hard to get things right and is good at listening and responding.  I would definitely recommend this Trust as a place to work and to personally develop.

I enjoy working within my team, I feel appreciated most of the time and have no concerns with the direct team.  At times senior management make changes that seem impractical and will not listen to the concerns of the staff, at least initially.

I experience the Trust to not be doing enough to promote understanding around Neurodiversity and invisible disabilities to some extent in its service users and families, but significantly so in its workforce.  I have been and can be the receiver of discrimination born out of ignorance rather than maliciousness.  Nonetheless, this is still experienced as shaming/humiliating.  I believe the Trust also needs to still work bottom up concerning this and also the issue of racism experienced by staff.  In my opinion the Trust does nothing about the abuse received by its staff despite posters to the contrary (zero tolerance).

I feel I get respect from my immediate peers however I feel that staff that there is a huge lack of respect from staff that are line managed.  It can make managers often feel disrespected they will often voice that they will go to unions or FTSU and tend to this as a threat.
I feel that management (*including wider management) are more focussed on targets and outcomes rather than wellbeing of staff and patients.

I feel that some KPIs are unachievable, unfair and take away from patient care, for example access rates.  I also feel the new 15% annual leave KPI affects having a healthy work life balance when annual leave is not granted.

I feel that the Trust has worked hard to define the culture at a strategic level.  I feel that there are pockets of great practice and culture and others where there are blocks and the message struggles to penetrate these blocks.  This often corresponds with strength of leadership.  I am confident that the Trust strategy and values will be successful in improving the culture of the organisation at every level.  I'm proud to work for Mersey Care and manage a team.  I work hard to pass on the consistent message of supporting health and wellbeing and investment and recognition of my staff.  I feel supported by my managers to do this.  I work across lots of teams and see differences.  As an organisation which has undergone significant org change, we've come a long way and I have hope for the future.

I feel the majority of staff around me work very hard.  I feel staff go above and beyond.  I feel working with staff.  Staff can be overworked due to shortages of staff members.  Its a friendly place and it makes a difference when you come to work.  I work in many establishments across the Trust.  I feel the warmth and welcoming of staff makes it a nice place to be.  It is a very challenges role in Mental Health and I feel the majority of staff do this role because they care and are compassionate.
I feel very lucky working together with amazing professionals.  I feel totally supported and value by my line managers.

I generally enjoy my job and value my work.  The most challenging part of work is targets which are often difficult to meet due to the nature in which information is collated.  For example, a client who drops out of treatment following 2 or 3 sessions impacts on therapists overall recovery rate.  I have also noticed an increase in more challenging and complex clients over the past 12 months without any changes to caseload numbers or having extra time to treat this client group.  I would recommend my role to others however feel with a few changes it could be an even better place to work.
I have a fantastic and very supportive team and I feel extremely supported within my role.

I have been filling in this questionnaire almost every year now for the last decade or so, and most of the key issues that concerned me have not changed during that time.  It makes me feel like it is pointless filling in the questionnaire, and I find the pressure from the trust to fill it in, accompanied by the assertion that my opinion matters, to be very frustrating, because I don't think this is really the case.  I don't believe that the trust has a considered approach to the work that it does from a psychological point of view, which seems like a missed opportunity from a mental health trust.  I think this is reflected in the make up of the work force, the way that estates and facilities are used, and mirrored in the problems with recruitment and retention.  I don't think that the trust has access to enough psychiatric bed space, which means that people are put at risk on a regular basis and admissions are refused, or delayed due to this reason.  I believe the reasons for this are broadly financial and that this is the often the most paramount concern for the trust.  This is also the case in terms of the working conditions for the staff that, over the years, have been pushed to working in a call centre type environments, which I don't believe is arranged because it will help us provide outstanding care as a service, but to save money for the trust..

I have worked In healthcare for many, many years; Mersey-Care is the first organisation (and line-manager) I've worked with which fully understands chaplaincy/spiritual care; It has taken steps to ensure we have sufficient spiritual/pastoral care staff across our trust - this enables quality holistic care for our patients, staff and carers.  The very fact that my line-manager (and director) understands chaplaincy/spiritual care adds greatly to the feeling of being valued within my trust.
I love working for Mersey Care - I value the work I do within the Chaplaincy Team and believe we make a real difference.

I think Mersey Care and the NHS as a whole is a fantastic employer.  The pay could be better but that is a wider systemic issue.  I'm allowed to work flexible/condensed hours which has helped with childcare through the years which has benefited my entire family.
I would like to continue WFH and many of my colleagues value WFH.

Immediate teams that I work with are great but sometimes the bigger teams at large will make decisions that will affect other staff members lower down but not themselves, so it feels that they don't always make the best decisions as they might not care as much as it doesn't impact them and they don't get us involved for our views.  There seem to be a lot of managers that nobody is sure what they do and not enough on the 'shop floor'.

Its been very difficult over the last couple of years trying to adapt to constant ongoing changes to our service.  We have recently increased the amount of managers we have as part of transformational change, however this is impacting with having more work and less time to adjust to changes to how we work.  This has also increased the lack of communication with the amount of layers of managers to go through before getting a response and clear guidance if at all.  Quite a lot of new colleagues to the service are inexperienced and struggle trying to become familiar and learn in their new roll.  This impacts on the staff who are trying to do business as usual with no clear guidance support or answers to concerns they have.  There also seems to be a lack of following NICE guidance, we are being told its just guidance.  I cope with this by following guidance from my professional body to adhere to code of practice.  Most staff members just want to get on with their job and not have to constantly have to change how they do this, there is a lack of feeling stable with this constant change.
Just feel lost.

Lots of changes in the organisation over the last year or 2 have made the job more difficult and creates burn out.  Changes are based around meeting targets and are not in the best interest of the patients.  This is wrong and demoralising.

Managers can often contradict each others narratives on practice and policy.  For example, KPI 's etc.  Difficulties within team environment and actions have not been followed up.  Professional capacity which results in no protected time for admin and poses risk for incidents.  Professionals in lower bands feel they are not acknowledged and decisions are always made by leadership.  Managers are not visible in the office and rarely engage with the team when passing etc.  Managers close office door and this often feels they are shut away from team.  Team canvas not been updated considering more than half of staff are new to service.

MCFT is the best NHS trust I have worked for and it led by strong Exec Team who I have faith/trust has the best vision, plans, workstreams set for MCFT boroughs, families and services.  I am excited re: new much needed changes and developments, in particular MCFT Trusts commitment to supporting the most vulnerable complex families in our communities.

Mersey Care have tried to re-brand themselves after years of having a poor reputation.  However, they have failed to address concerns raised about bullying and harassment by a[job title removed] within my team.  We are losing staff and morale is low.  It remains to be seen whether Mersey Care will live up to its values.
Merseycare when it comes down to it is more interested in its own reputation and survival than it is in users.  I hope this will change with the new chief executive.

My Organisation since organisational change has become a big divide, and very fragmented, the teams don't work together any more and a lot of staff are unaware of what is happening within their own organisation due to this divide and non sharing of information.
My team were very supportive after a period of sick leave which I really appreciated.
NHS need better pay and benefits.  Staff will continue to leave and seek private roles if this does not change.
None, thank you.
Not enough rooms to complete my work which has a detrimental impact on waiting times.  Organisation is driven by KPIs and targets and this is felt by staff members, with little regard to burnout.

PACE was apparently revised this year but I thought it was just as bad as ever.  The domains aren't explained enough and I'm well educated.  We have a hard time thinking straight we're so busy.  The PACE could be so much more simple/straightforward.  My immediate team and managers are great, they're supportive and nice people.  The organisation is to big though and I feel we're very disconnected from senior management.  I feel they don't know what happens at the grassroot level.  There's too much admin to do and Rio is so awful.  I've worked on better systems so I know they exist.

Recently received an email saying I would be sent an appointment for MMR vaccine as occ health didn't have records of my vaccination/immunity status.  There was no discussion before hand and no attempt to find out what my vaccination status was.  I replied back with all the information required to evidence why I did not need MMR vaccine but then received an email which I presume has also been sent to my line manager, which I felt was quite threatening in tone.  This is not acceptable.
Request for more working hours should be constructively considered when requested by current staff.

Senior management within the NHS Talking therapies team leadership approach has been extremely poor with regards to their communication towards the team during several meetings this year.  There is a top down do as I say approach which is damaging morale and leading to widespread distrust.  This type of leadership style will result in staff leaving and it is no surprise that there are significant staff vacancies.  I do not believe that their communication aligns with the trust values in the few times they have been present in staff meetings.  There is a tunnel vision approach to achieving 50% recovery.  I would remind leadership that reliable improvement scores make a substantial difference to the day to day lives of many of our clients.

Senior Social Work leadership has caused a rift between nursing colleagues and social care service.  Lack of trust from SW managers towards nursing care staff.  Too much emphasis on using Local Authority IT systems rather than NHS systems.  Duplicating SW tasks, duplicating meetings, taking social workers away from direct patient care.
So many more expectations / training to complete e.g. higher risk management expectations, rather than refer to secondary for their input.

Social Work is undervalued within this Organisation and we are a vital cog in the discharge wheel.  We lead and guide our health colleagues and the team do often feel their work is unrecognised.  There is no ability for team leaders to progress to Band 8A level other than moving to a different area of work outside social work, this has been sanctioned by senior managers.  It is often sole destroying to witness the increase in operational management when SW are left behind regardless of the heavy workload which is wrapped around with Legislation and law.
Some of the comments couldn't say as I have just returned back after [timeframe removed] off [condition removed].
Some of the issues that get in the way of me feeling more able to complete my role/duties are down to not having more training on the use of RIO.
Staff services are poor in this trust - recruitment, payroll, HR.

Staffing is an issue in the MSU at Rowan View.  As a social worker, I am often concerned both about the care afforded to patients due to low staffing levels and also the welfare of the ward staff (NAs and nurses).  Because of staffing issues there can sometimes either be not enough staff to safely run the ward and there can be breaches in policies (i.e. back to back observations etc).  Furthermore, there are sometimes no or few regular staff on a shift and this can then feel quite unsafe and patients report feeling unsafe when there's few regular or not enough staff.  Patients' visits can end up cancelled preventing patients' right to family life and they can be prevented from going on leave which impacts them moving on.  It just feels there is no solution to this in sight and that things will continue on this way which is not good in terms of staff retention, staff sickness levels, and overall staff and patient morale.
Stating my age / gender / job title does not make this survey anonymous.
Targets appear to have become a priority over everything else which has a negative impact on staff and patient's.
The availability of clinical space significantly impacts on my working week and ability to see service users.  There are not enough clinical rooms to see service users in.
The main issues at present is low pay for the responsibility held, skill level used, and qualifications obtained.

The payroll and ESR within Mersey Care are one of the worst many of us have had to deal with.  I have been fighting for the last 18 months over annual leave, pay and contract errors.  The teams are unwilling to listen and have tried to out right break employment law, only once the union and the head of department got involved was my voice listened to.  I am not the only member of staff even within my team to have such problems.  It is a huge problem for recruitment and retention within Mersey Care and it needs looking into ASAP.

The service no longer is no longer a joy to work in.  It's no longer about people, it's about numbers and statistics.  Too much is expected of us in terms of information gathering and responsibility, this takes over doing the actual job.  I feel like a case manager and not a therapist.  We do make a difference to people's lives but unless their outcome measures show certain numbers then it doesn't count!  I came into the profession to help people, now I consistently feeling that I am just managing risk and seeing people that are our service is not suitable for.  Yet we are still expected to get results!

The team at a ward level are fantastic- very enthusiastic about providing high quality care, and regularly go above and beyond for patients.  I find starting improvement projects very difficult, and although there is often intellectual appetite for positive change, it often doesn't get actioned due to staffing or time constraints.  I find it difficult to carry a improvement project through to routine implementation, and important changes often get shelved.  Staffing levels can often be below minimum in all teams, affecting the pace and quality of work, and staff frequently report to me they are feeling run down and tired.  Staff often end up ill due to overwork.  The pharmacy department are great at supporting staff to undertake paid learning opportunities, and provide lots of funding and supported time in work to undertake this.  I feel there are opportunities for growth and advancement within the department, and feel its a place I can develop my career.

The team I work for are lovely and support each other well.  However with recent changes, requested administrative tasks make you repeat your work in several different formats, leading to unnecessary and excessive work loads and it is ineffective use of practitioners time.  Our team managers are also lovely and very supportive.  Whilst the trust advocate being able to report issues upwards, often it feels very much like a top-down process, in which changes occur in our service decided by management who aren't part of our team and with no discussion on the matter first.  When we report concerns around any of these changes, our service managers take this seriously but we get the sense that not much changes when raised higher.

The Trust is good at looking after patients but not so good at looking after staff.  So many people are leaving the team because they are being micromanaged.  At times it's like being back at school.  There's little opportunity for agile or flexible working.  It feels like managers are happy to have miserable, unhappy staff who can't wait to leave the NHS.  We have poor facilities, buildings fit to burst as so many people are crammed in, no wonder people are disillusioned.  You get more from happy staff!

The wider trust seems to have lots of process issues.  For example being told a department cannot do something despite there being instructions on how to do it on the department's own website.  There does not seem to be considerations for parents of young children, or parents whose children have additional complex needs, in regards to carers leave or ad hoc flexible working.  There seems to be an increase in duplications of recording, and unnecessary processes, in comparison to a few years ago.

There are challenges within psychology recruitment that put additional pressure on those in post (despite best efforts of local psychology managers).  There are limited senior roles in certain psychology specialties therefore we lose staff to other Trusts who have better career progression opportunities.

There has become a closed culture where problems are not addressed, objections are not listened to, concerns are ignored, and standardising procedures for QRV or CQC are favoured.  There is a nursing culture and not a multidisciplinary culture where opinions of professionals other than senior nurse leaders are ignored.  The drive for economy over quality care is also dangerous and is ostracizing staff who are concerned about their own welfare and that of patients.  There is a new drive of unilateral decision making which is harming the workplace, and no real forum to talk or be listened to and responded to in a meaningful way.  The workplace, despite all of the things in this survey which make for positive reading, is becoming unsafe.

There is a different between my experience of my team and service that I work in compared to the trust as an organisation.  I have found the trust to be largely unhelpful, defensive in practice, far removed from patient care, wellbeing and also staff wellbeing, poor approach to toil (encouraging staff to work unpaid hours) poor system development, poor management systems, poor escalation process, processes across services.  My sense is that the trust cannot cope with the size of itself and has become process for the sake of process at times, without clear rationale or consultation.  I have never heard that we can't do things or have to do things because that's just the way trust does it so much in another trust I've worked in.  I've worked in other trusts in my career and this is by far the worst.

There is a real issue within Mersey Care with a lack of clinical space to meet with service-users.  This has been going on for a number of years but has gotten significantly worse since staff have returned more fully to bases following COVID.  Places service-users are being seen are often completely inappropriate and untherapeutic and does not indicate that we, as an organisation, value our service-users.  Also our frontline staff also have to work in inappropriate environments that are noisy, stressful and impersonal.  Hot desking in large areas that have poor equipment is not understanding of the stress of working with mental health in secondary services.  Staff often need a quiet space to think, but this is not provided due to poorly designed admin spaces.  I am hopeful the Trust will prioritise this in the coming years as I am aware it regularly comes up in mega-conversations etc. - across the whole of the Trust but continues to be a problem.
Things seem to be improving.  Better managers.  Better culture.
This organisation is understaffed and overworked.  Staff are being told they can apply for incentives such as flexible working but the structure is not there to support this.
This organisation states that it has a just and learning culture but this is not reflected in its behaviour.

Unrelenting and unrealistic targets are driven from community division leaders who don't seem to understand or demonstrate the needs of the population that the team is serving.  Data driven dogma reduces clinical quality, affects clinical decision making - leading to local team managers feeling under pressure to push staff to do more with less time - due to needing to attend to patients with more complexity alongside staffing shortages.  Over last 12 months I have experienced pressures of "see more people quickly" - leading to messages of "discharge quickly" and watering down clinical offer.  Working at speed, offering reduced /less effective clinical intervention and discharging quickly are the elements that appear to be valued by senior community division managers with the organisation.  Some of the changes around safety/risk planning seem to be cumbersome and serve a "tick box" without any care being shown to patients or staff - but is carried under the guise of improving quality..  No exit interviews for those staff who move departments within the organisation misses vital information as to why someone may be leaving, no patterns/themes are investigate.  I'm aware of 8-9 senior staff who have left one IAPT service, myself included, due to bullying behaviour from a senior staff member - but people are too fearful of losing their jobs to raise concerns.  Its felt those senior staff are simply delivering what more senior managers in the service want regarding targets.
What is the point of commenting as nothing will happen/change.

Whilst the trust are advertising flexible working and trying hard to promote it as attractable for staff, managers remain to have a problem with allowing flexible working and making it very hard for colleagues to have a work life balance.  Even when giving reasons they are not accurate representations to reject, and even though there maybe appeal processes this would make daily life difficult and uncomfortable so its often seen as not worth it.  There should be more career progression opportunities within trust for psychology and recognition that real job responsibilities often superseded the banding so business cases for up banding for recognition and respect of job should be considered more openly.

Working in the NHS since the pandemic has become extremely challenging.  After [timeframe removed] of service I have never known things to be as difficult as they are for myself and others right now.  I feel that the impact of the pandemic on staff (and the public in general) has been grossly underestimated.  Wellbeing of staff in the NHS has been given a higher profile which is good, but in reality it doesn't have the impact it should.  NHS England, individual Trusts and senior managers can still be preoccupied with statistical outcomes, putting pressure on staff, and therefore cancelling out the wellbeing agendas they seem to be trying to promote.  Burn out in the NHS is a major concern in my opinion and one that needs to be addressed effectively going forward if systems, services and individuals are going to survive into the future.

Working within a psychological department that operates within a wider team, I do feel that we are not fully valued by the Trust, as illustrated by the severe lack of resources, especially room space.  Having regular, protected clinical space is the bare minimum we would expect in order to do our jobs, however across the Trust, we are continuously having to work around other professions, particularly medics, and are expected to be flexible and adaptive, often losing clinical space due to medical staff rotation or increased demand from elsewhere.  Many are the times we lose protected space and have to rearrange or cancel clinical appointments, or not book in assessments or even refuse taking trainee psychologists.  This is not sustainable and is a strong, significant barrier to us being able to implement care for service users and supervision for staff.  I appreciate the challenges the Trust faces in lowering costs and finding savings, however this is a problem that is persistent and pervasive.  This has a significant and strong impact on staff morale and ultimately does result in many psychology staff feeling undervalued and overlooked.
Workload is ridiculous and it is rarely acknowledged because everyone is experiencing it, so it is now the norm- but it is unsustainable.
You could do with separating line managers to more senior management in some of these questions as the answers may be quite different.

A big source of dissatisfaction within my role (Assistant Psychologist) is the poor pay and the lack of options for progression.  There are few options for me to progress which I will be likely to get on to (i.e. the doctorate), which combined with the poor pay and challenging nature of the work, is not a good mix for my mental health.
A four day week would be help with health and well-being allowing more family time and reduce burnout caused by stress and illness.  Offering longer shifts in certain services instead of usual 9-5.

All my negative comments are the higher management, don't feel apricated, they don't support our team if it wasn't for my colleges in work I would of left years ago, I got diagnosed with [conditions removed] on [date removed].  And heard from no one.

Although I think my trust is a good one to work for and has patients interest at the forefront.  I fine having outside SLA.  Do not have the patients best interest and do not go above and beyond, certainly not value for money.
Any answers given about management are for previous manager.  Only met new manager once as they are new to the team.

As an STR worker on the Recovery team I notice we have a lack of occupational therapists in the trust.  Wouldn't it be an idea for STR workers to have the opportunity to become qualified as occupational therapists even if this involves night school college attendance.
At present I am happy in my job.

At times of reorganisation within the OT sector I am working in I feel that there is no communication, as assistants we are given mixed information or none at all.  Also having worked here for [timeframe removed], I have spent 50% of my working time covering for nursing assistants, so have only actually worked in my OTA position for 50%, extremely unhappy about this.  Creating a very bullying atmosphere from nursing staff and totally unappreciated, and not given full breaks or always given the worst jobs to give their staff a break, often unable to take a lunch break due to being in observations often in seclusion, while ward nursing staff are able to eat while being on a lower level of observation.  At moment having contract changes and we are not being consulted or being given the opportunity to have a meeting to discuss the changes, miscommunication between staff, information being given to certain staff, then other staff being told that information was incorrect, leading to a very stressful working environment.

Brilliant team to work with and amazing colleagues and manager who all care about there job and service users.  MC appears to large to look after colleagues and sort out any concerns affectively due to lack of funding in NHS and social, this has massively impacted on everyone's job role making each task a lot more difficult - trusts/government looking at short term fixes, preventative measures are no longer looked at due to time and money which will cost more money long term.
Building feel old and dark.  Electric car charging use to be free but now is no longer free and outsourced to private company which chargers fee now.
Bullies get looked after.
Bullying and clique culture.  Often ignored.
Can the Trust review their skilled worker visa policy to support staff on Band3 that require visa sponsorship?  Such positive decision will make the Trust to retain staff.

Communication between teams could improve.  Managers should take on board concerns and feedback appropriately.  I feel we should also be included in decisions made that directly affect the staff, managers have made lots of changes recently without considering staffs feelings or opinions.  Feel as though staff wellbeing is not thought about at all, feels as though we are all just numbers and not human beings.

Community support workers should be paid at band 4 due to working alone, supporting with transitioning challenging individuals from hospitals and prisons into the community.  Working with other services.  We do not work on the ward and have nurses to support us and other professionals.

Continually under staffed with lips service support from senior management.  Unsafe staffing levels.  Site deliberately staffed short at weekends.  Staff ignored when highlighted.  Staff pressured to input inaccurate information on patient activities (iPad is commonly known as the lie pad).  Our patients have little or nothing to encourage them out of bed.

Disappointed that Mersey care has lost the tender in regards to addiction services.  I will be losing my job in [date removed] as I'm currently funded on a fixed term contract after having give up my substantial post to join addiction services.  I hopefully will be able to take another role within merseycare if not I will become unemployed, which is a shame.

District nursing is for housebound patients but staff think it would be helpful for my organisation to employ doctors for staff to seek medical advice when unwell as they may not get to see or have available appointments the same day.

Earlier this year l asked if l could retire and return as l had given[timeframe removed] of service to the NHS.  This was refused on the grounds of needing staff to cover the hours that l worked.  This was particularly irritating to me because of a number of staff in the team who have been allowed to retire and return.  These are all trained staff who been awarded the consideration of retiring and returning.  l feel l have not been awarded the same consideration as the trained staff in the team.  Very ageist decision as far as l am concerned.  General staff feel under pressure and under valued by managerial staff particularly the grade 8 staff who are neither use nor ornament to the real workers.
Enjoy my role within the trust.  Merseycare have helped me develop and now working as a band 4 nursing associate.  Hoping to further develop to a registered mental health nurse.
Equal pay or at least more fair across MSU.

Everyone should work together for the patients and have an effective communication, I have found management don't often communicate with staff on the ward and not update staff on decisions made for the ward and the patients.
Feel Mersey Care managers not supportive of staff on wards.  Feel like a number rather than a valued member of staff.

Feel under valued, under paid and under appreciated in our roles.  We all need to be taught the basics BSL which has been asked numerous times throughout the years especially COVID times as we were wearing masks and removing them to speak, its common courtesy.  There are a lot of flaws in this system.

First response team is a great team to work for.  We all share a passion to provide perfect care and zero suicide goal.  Our team is friendly, encouraging and supportive it feels like we are one big family.  Managers help you to gain new skills and help with trying to climb your career ladder with support and encouragement.
For a primarily mental heath organisation.  Your attitude towards staff with mental health problems is very poor.

For future admissions, management need look more at diagnosis and risk.  To have a balanced patient mix as management look at skill mix for staff on duty.  Maybe this would lessen assaults and aggression to both staff and fellow patients.
Forever grateful for my consistent support/understanding from my Team Leader while navigating my [family member and details removed].

Higher management need to understand and support us nurses more, we don't get dinner breaks ever as there's to much work put on us, as they say you us take your breaks its physically impossible.  Some days eat on go or don't eat at all, take work home with us, as there never enough staff are patient lists always high.  If mistakes are made high management come down on us, not understanding how hard day in day out we work.  The pressure is on us every day.  Its physically and mentally draining, they wonder why so much long term sick!  .  Things need to change for everyone's well being.  I love my job, but the stress of it is getting worse not better, exhausted all the time.
Higher managers do not care about pressures on staff.
I am [details removed] soon I would just like to say Mersey care have been very supportive over the years and I have had lots of opportunities to develop my skills.

I am a working as a Nursing Assistant.  I have a passion in caring people who need support and care, I have find the place for that in Mersey care NHS Foundation Trust.  I came from another country to be a part in health sector in UK, for that I need a sponsorship to stay in UK.  Currently my visa expires in a few months, if Merseycare provide me a sponsorship, I will put my whole career to Merseycare trust and I need to grow up with this trust, I like to become a nurse in future so I will be with this trust in my entire life.  So can you help me to get a sponsorship in this trust.  I am confident that with your support, my qualifications and passion for healthcare will be a great contribution to the Trust.  I am enthusiastic about the opportunity to work in Merseycare NHS Foundation Trust.
I am currently a Band 3 support worker and feel that a lot of the work that I do, should be Band 4 because of the level of responsibility.

I am dismayed at how the changes within my organisation make it it clear that they do not care about its employees.  I have never worked in a company that does not care about a employees mental well being in particular.  Senior managers of my organisation have no clue about what happens on the ground nor do they wish to.  Its all about meeting their individual targets regardless of what measures are required to achieve them.
I am happy at work.

I am happy in my role I feel very supported by my immediate manager and the team I work with.  I believe there are too many management roles within the NHS, and since the transformation, clinical pathways are more confusing than ever.  Systems such as booking courses are overly complicated for some staff.  Systems should be user friendly.  In an IAPT service, whilst we are working with measures that we all agree are not fit for purpose, but will not change, has the potential to impact on staff morale and make attracting new staff difficult.
I am proud to work in this team!

I am quite happy with my job, however being a band [number removed] for the last [timeframe removed] has made me feel like we don't matter.  We as a team have been fighting for a band [number removed] for a number of years and I feel that the managers have done everything in their power to stop this.  It is appalling that this has been allowed.  It has made me feel that we don't matter.

I am shocked at the low numbers of staff we are expected to care for patients with....  On the rare occasions we are on numbers we then are expected to send staff to more acute wards....  All patients should have adequate staffing to provide the correct level of care...Night coordinators don't seem to recognise this.  Staffing is available but it's all about saving money fewer staff lower wage bills.  Too much money funnelled on growing the business and not enough on paying sufficient staff to care for patients.  And if you really want to know how staff feel....  Visit some wards you will find out in no time....
I believe that Mersey Care should be trying and helping progress it's own employees more and reward them with more opportunity for those who wish to move up the ladder and progress professionally.
I cannot fault the support I have received from my workplace over the last 2 years.
I cannot say how I feel for fear of being identified and punished.

I continue to enjoy working within my team and my line manager is approachable, caring, a team player and after all the earlier managers I have had during my career, she is the best one.  I work for the best team and I hope to see my days out as a support worker for the people I support.

I decided to leave my current job because my employer do not offer healthcare work visa to Nursing Assistants, the occupation group which I fall under.  This would not allow me to renew my Right to Work when it expires in few month time and I would not be eligible to work.  My job security was not guaranteed.

I don't feel that qualified nurses are recognised in my Dept.  I have unqualified people on a band above me and my line Manager has informed me there is no scope to progress to a higher band despite being a qualified [job title removed].
I don't have confidence that this survey will make a difference or bring about changes to ensure a supportive healthy place to work.

I don't think we get the support from management they just talk down to us never support there staff.  We need new computers in the team to do our job as staff waiting round for staff to come of computers.  Home visits there is no support we keep telling management that there is areas that we need parking permit and they don't listen so then some staff have had parking fine due to parking at pts house.  We have a clinic in Liverpool rope walk and due to parking staff get in to work an hour early to get a parking bay.

I enjoy my job role as a health care assistant within[location removed].  My team leader is extremely supportive, encouraging approachable.  I feel my role [details removed] is rewarding fulfilling and you realise end of life matters.
I enjoy working in my current post has nursing assistant for Merseycare, and would recommend friends to work for the trust.
I feel as if we are not valued an respected the moral with staff members is low our team leaders and Band 5 are approachable it is the management on a higher level.
I feel completely undervalued and over stretched at work.  We have managers who are more concerned about kpi's than staff welfare.
I feel fully supported within my team.

I feel like a valued member of my team.  I feel confident that I could raise any concerns with them and they would be managed accordingly.  My team is friendly and approachable and I would 100% recommend the team.  As a team, we have high standards and go above and beyond for our clients.

I feel most of the time not supported enough by my managers in band 8 and above, we do not have face to face meeting even though we have safety huddle we do not get our chance to speak about our issues, and also even when we speak about the problems they will not get sorted.
I feel over worked feel under appreciated under valued and think the pay in disgraceful.
I feel that although we voice that we are over loaded they don't listen and we have voiced this so many times.

I feel that changing the hours within the District Nursing service has stretched the day staff leaving us short staffed with the same workload.  The 5-8 shift, there are more staff available with often less call outs.  Continuity of care is difficult to achieve as staff are expected to pick up work from other teams as they are now short staffed in the day.  Morale is at an all time low within the teams.  Sickness within the teams is increasing.  Its just so sad that an amazing well lead District Nursing service is now struggling.
I feel there should be a better system/strategy to show recognition for others.

I feel this survey is generic and does not represent the percentage of the workforce that work in Merseycare.  It is a liberal tick box exercise that does not meet the work force population within Merseycare, maybe allow additional comments in each section to get true feedback and not the percentages outcome that you will get from a tick box exercise.

I feel unsafe due to staffing levels, I feel staff that sometimes bank do not comply either trust policies and often disregard or ignore patients and staff that are not of the same ethnicity.  I feel threats off higher management moving me to other wards if I speak up regarding any concerns as this has happened to 4 colleagues I worked with who raised issues.  I feel wards are unsafe due to lack of boundaries and staff assaults increasing dramatically within last 12 months, and that staff are expected to accept this with no support or consequence.

I feel valued within my team, we are actively encouraged to have daily catch up meetings and informal supervision, discuss any concerns or problems.  My concerns are with senior members of the board who look into Emis diaries regarding (quantity)how many visits?  Rather than quality.

I have a lot of family problems at the moment and feel that my managers have been very understanding of these problems and have let me take family members to appointments as and when needed and have let me make this time up as and when I am able to.  I appreciate this a lot.
I have always enjoyed my job and love working in a team.  I feel important in my job role and my colleagues are really happy with my work too.

I have been in my job in community Phlebotomy for many years under North West Boroughs.  Since we were taken over by Merseycare the job is not what it was, we are on minimum wage and considering we are one of the busiest services we are being put under pressure on a daily basis, I consider myself as a hard worker but this is stressing me out, I've tried to explain mistakes will happen if we are put under more pressure.  Knowsley Phlebotomy do a great Job but I'm so thinking of walking away.  Nobody listens to us and it is so frustrating.  Someone in this organisation needs to listen to our concerns instead of just expecting us to do as we are told, Feeling bullied to be honest and as an adult this does not feel great.

I have been made to feel so welcome since starting with my team earlier this year and I see every day how hard everyone works to make sure that patient care is the top priority.  I feel valued, trusted and respected here and I am grateful that I have a really good work/life balance which was not the case in my previous job outside the NHS.  There are lots of opportunities for me to go into different career pathways and progress and I know that my team wouldn't hesitate in supporting me with this should I need it.

I have been with Mersey Care for [timeframe removed].  The atmosphere in the team is toxic, and has been a long time before I started.  I have experienced bullying due to the fact that I am a recovering [condition removed], and some members of staff see me as a threat as I offer something different to our service users.  I have reported this to my managers, and freedom to speak, and so far nothing has been done.

I have completed my survey truthfully and honestly and this is how I feel at this present moment.  I am suffering from anxiety and depression due to not being listened to and not being treated like a valued member of staff.

I have completed this survey reflecting on my department as I feel valued by the Dental directorate but not with Mersey Care.  I have had to use my own annual leave to see my daughter through cancer and chemo treatment which I think is disgusting.  I applied myself for the long service award and I'm still waiting 12 months on!

I have had [number removed] incident and [number removed] very serious operation in the [timeframe removed] I've been working in the phlebotomist department and in these occasions I feel I was not supported by this organisation what so ever, I find we are not supported in any way.

I have had to fight to have reasonable adjustments made due to a [condition removed].  Management are not understanding, staff are leaving the trust due to having their flexible working agreements refused.

I have just moved to another ward because I was unhappy with the OT department I worked for.  The OT band 7 was on maternity  The band 4 is good friends with the band 7 so all decisions and communications did not involve me.  A few days before I left, the band 4 OT practitioner assistant introduced me to the new OT that was starting.  I knew nothing of this.  Dreadful department, unless you are in the click.
I have really enjoyed my first year in the trust working within my team.

I have recently been part of organisational change where my department have been redeployed: it has been an anxiety provoking experience to say the least.  It would have helped greatly to be treated in a more empathic manner which would have cost nothing.  I understand that decisions are made at a higher level and there is sometimes nothing that is going to be done to change the outcome but the staff have been put through the maximum amount of anxiety, everything was left to the last possible moment, the redeployment process did not appear clear to anyone.  My department was a group of highly motivated, hard working people who know our own value to our clients - this process appears to be have been designed to destroy this.

I have recently been redeployed [details removed] and whilst happy within the new role and support from the team the entire process to be redeployed or supported in any real manner was extremely distressing further impacting on my mental and physical health.  It also resulted in me going from a band [number removed] to a band [number removed] resulting in [details removed].  During [details removed] there was minimal support from HR or my direct manager even though I was required to contact them fortnightly throughout this period.  There was no proactivity from my manager in finding out information from HR or anything that could be considered supportive.  Agreement for redeployment was only made after I had a direct conversation with a HR manager who was more supportive.  Despite this there was still minimal information given on processes and I attended several suitability interviews where the job had changed significantly from how it had been advertised meaning I was unable to take them.  Despite seeing occupational health on several occasions recommendations were ignored and no discussion took place on how they could be accommodated or why they had not been agreed by either HR or my direct manager.  I feel that effectively my direct manager and their management team were effectively trying to manage me out.  All negative comments on this questionnaire are related to my previous role and management within that role.  The culture in the previous office was a highly negative experience, inappropriate sexual conversations, lack of care towards service users, workers using private WhatsApp groups to discuss other staff negatively were routine.  When another member of staff completed a freedom to speak up no changes were implemented and she was seen as difficult.  If I did bring concerns to my manager again nothing was done rendering it pointless.  Prior to [details removed] I felt undervalued, deskilled and burnt out, when I was asked about contributing factors [details removed] in stage meetings these were dismissed.  In a previous [details removed] my manager lied on several occasions, they also told me in an initial stage meeting over the telephone that they was reverting my agreed reduction in hours to full time, therefore making it more difficult to [details removed] as I was then faced with having to challenge this with the union whilst [details removed].  The support I have received in my new role however from the team has been exceptional.
I have worked for my organisation for many years, I am going to retire and return part time to my current roll which I love.

I have worked in the NHS for many years and in the main I have enjoyed my work and found it rewarding.  However in the past year or so I feel that I am discriminated against because of my age and no longer feel as valued as I once was.  This saddens me greatly.  I was considering retiring and returning to work on a part time basis.  However I think I will retire with my dignity intact.

I previously worked for North West boroughs before being taken over by Merseycare NHS foundation.  I find Mersey care lack very little empathy for the pressures that staff are under.  The recruitment process is poor and takes over 6 moths for anyone to complete which sees staff getting work elsewhere so this leads to continuous staff shortages and staff being moved around the trust constantly.  Qualified are often left on their own to manage a ward with no qualified support and very rarely getting breaks.  While the ward manager is supportive there is lack of support from Operational managers, gone are the days where they would spend time on the ward and offer support during difficult times.  Very often team are told of what they have not done but never positive praise for all they good they have done under difficult circumstances which leads to poor moral.
I really think the sickness policy needs to be reviewed as it is a huge drain on the NHS.

I really wish to work in Mersey Care but the only problem facing is the organisation doesn't provide visa sponsorship for nursing assistant band 3 role.  If we get visa many people stay within the organisation and will help to develop the goal that organisation looking forward to.
I recommend us getting our birthday off as a thanks to staff and to help with staff wellbeing.

I think I'm is a great place to work.  A huge emphasis is placed on supporting colleagues with development and health and wellbeing.  The effect of which can be seen in the culture and behaviours in all we do and what we achieve as a relatively small organisation.
I think the Freedom to Speak is essential for me feeling comfortable in my role and how much the organisation values it makes me feel confident and supported.  Thank you for this being in place.

I think this organisation is too focused on paperwork and emails, constant changes to things that work well then fail to do so when removed.  Staff moral is low, no flexibility for staff to stay, if you don't fit the rules they won't alter them to encourage staff to stay.  Management has lost interest in staff as individuals and you are just a number.  Too many management and none clinical managers who don't understand what it is like to work on the floor and cannot pitch in to help when the staffing is poor.  Not enough focus on patients rather than paperwork.  Poor communication, website isn't clear enough to allow patients to make a clear decision what we offer at each Walk in site.  Poor sickness policies where long term illnesses are staged the same as fit people, many staff leaving constantly due to the pressures.

I think woman going through perimenopause menopause.  Need help and support in the work place.  And they need to be listened to.  I have had the worst [timeframe removed] of my life with being perimenopause.  I got moved hospitals I have really struggled, mentally because of the changes.  I just wanted to stay where I was with what I'm comfortable with.  Going to a new hospital environment has really affected me.  I told management how I felt and was not listened to.

I thoroughly enjoy my role as a Nursing Associate and am very grateful to the organisation for providing me with this opportunity.  However, I feel that there is a lack of understanding among some staff, particularly nursing assistants, regarding the requirements of my role, which involves working alongside nurses during shifts.  Additionally, being counted in staffing numbers rather than being supernumerary, like a student, often limits my learning opportunities, especially given the high acuity and staffing challenges we face on the ward.
I used to really love my job, working with children and families, but at present I feel its lots of documentation and lots of referrals.

I will be retired this year I hope I will be given a nice plaque after many years service and I would like a retirement party on the ward but I do not think Mersey Care will stretch that far but I can always live in hope.
I wish the trust will take the pay scale seriously so staff can afford a better living condition as its really a struggle with cost of living at the moment as NHS Staff.

I wish we could be on an equitable wage.  Band 3 for person is different to the next.  We all have to have some clinical knowledge and do reflective practice and training based on clinical knowledge yet we are not granted the lead payment that ward staff get.  I feel discriminated against.
I work at [name removed] Team, it is very well managed and staff morale is high.

I Work for Kirkby CMHT, at Northwood house I have worked for the NHS for [timeframe removed], and I have never felt so isolated ever I enjoy my actual Job when I am allowed to do it, , I have always taken pride in going above and beyond, but unfortunately since recent unexperienced newly qualified staff staff being made up to quickly and not having the experience of community for at least Six Months, we are not giving the best quality of care, and if your face don't fit, well you have not got a voice, and feel isolated, myself and at least 4 other, long term staff who were happy in our job and gave 110 percent to our job, have been put down and not heard, as we are not included in any decisions I have seen strong staff in tears, and that includes myself.  I have seen many changes over the years, some for the best and some not so it isn't that I'm not adverse of change for the best, in my working career, I have never worked in such an unhappy team, and I can go back to any team I have worked in.
I work within a hard working and committed team who are supportive and we can all be open and honest with each other, personal concerns stay confidential within our team.
I worked in my job for many years and never felt as low as this, Merseycare are changing things and not for the best.

I would just like to say I have based my answers on my whole experience within the passed few years.  However we have recently got a new team manager and they are great, they are limited to what they can do and change within a team that is already constructed and has a culture.  I feel strongly that there is some work carried out from the higher management to help the team bond as I have never worked with a team like it.  Its actually quite sad!
I would like to see the patients admitted to us to be actually physically well enough to go through rehabilitation.  I also think safe staffing numbers need to be addressed.
I'm currently moved from a acute ward to a fantastic physical health ward in [number removed] months where I now feel valued and supported and a great environment and I am now enjoying going to work.

If you raise a worry no matter how you do it you just keep going round and round until you just give up.  Issues take ages to even be looked at.  A lot is expected from people even when there are other things that need to be done.

It is very challenging at the moment as my staff are going through a consultation review, and it is very challenging, my staff our very unhappy about the new change in Job Description, and as much as I support them, the moral is very low, we do try and reassure them, but since we have merged with other services from Liverpool and Sefton, it has been a bit of a nightmare, as staff are unhappy about working across the Merseycare footprint.
It would be nice to support and increase pay bands when a band 4 hits the top of the banding as trusts are now assessing workers on individuals experience.
It wouldn't matter what I wrote Merseycare bosses stick together.

Its a shame this survey is just a tick box exercise you are probably missing loads of important things that could improve the service, like waiting lists, all over the trust o/t salt, physios, nursing need more staff.  Ask people these questions you will see.  Hard working people are feeling a lot of pressure.  Less agency more inhouse bank staff needed.  And why will the nursing bank not let a domestic with N.V.Q qualifications and working on a word, join the nursing bank.  But an agency staff with no experience who may not even have good English skills can.  There is something majorly wrong there.  Best care, well wake up and take people on who care.  And cut the [obscenity removed] if someone doesn't quite fit the criteria train them.  We see good staff being overlooked because they are pigeon holed or don't know the right people or process.  On call community is used as a bank and historically has been this is not best care either, recruitment is needed to cover these shifts not to be filled with someone because they are on call and have to work the next day not good for service users or staff or any of the teams.  Its a hard one for management to navigate I know and these surveys are good but let people write more not just tick a box.  Mandatory courses fund a better system no good on a computer, nothing like face to face, just a paper exercise and paying lip service to learning, again not best care, my opinion.  Otherwise happy at merseycare, worked for the NHS on and off for nearly [timeframe removed].
Just too high expectations for number of patients to work with.

Least restrictive practice has lead to more staff being physically assaulted.  Less boundaries for patients.  Less respect to nursing staff.  Zero tolerance of abuse to staff doesn't work.  Patients are not held accountable as deemed to not have capacity.  Staff are used as punch bags.
Love my job.

Low staffing levels are impacting every single aspect of our jobs and our patients care.  I've just looked at Mersey Care's vacancies and there are no Band 5 nurse vacancies yet there are never enough registered nurses.

Managers are only interested in out doing each other with team performance %.  As though its a game to win at the expense of the staff.  Example PACE, when the window opened to complete it we were all told to complete it asap via emails, text messages and verbally because our manager wanted to look the best for getting it done first.  As a result it was rushed without the opportunity to actually think about personal development by just writing anything down, sometimes just a full stop to be able to move on to the next question.  This continues with supervision, E learning and any training even when in date, just to look good better than the other teams.
Many challenges with systems, duplication of reporting on multiple systems that don't communicate.  Challenges with core functions e.g. getting timely responses from HR.

Mersey care do not have enough staff on wards and e.g. healthcare assistants which means staff are being moved constantly against their will to wards that they are unfamiliar with.  This leads to staff feeling anxious about coming into work and creates an unsafe environment with both staff and service users because when staff are moved to another ward they are unaware of risks/triggers for service users as well as being unaware of the environment and risks surrounding this.
Mersey care have been supportive to me throughout my employment here, I am enjoying working for Mersey care.

Mersey care is a trust that is only focused on meeting commissioned targets, the organisation does not value it's staff and there appears to be too many managers of B8 Who have no knowledge or skills of the staff they are managing and enforce working practices and SOPS that hamper the trained staff on completing there roles effectively.  Too much focus on supervision sessions and monitoring staff activity, rather then supporting staff to complete there roles or employing more staff to support services to work more effectively.  Mersey care does not care about staff, only about giving an outward appearance of being all singing and dancing without any merit.

Merseycare are an uncaring organisation.  It has become more of a business rather than a patient focused NHS.  Merseycare want staff over worked and pushed beyond their boundaries.  Staff moral is very low, staff have become very demotivated and looking for alternative jobs.

Merseycare do not care about the staff which is evident with the sickness policy.  This policy is disgusting for staff who work tirelessly for the patients in all weathers and all environments (community staff).  Staff are penalised for being sick and have to adhere to strict stages and the fear of losing your job, who is never sick in 12 months?  This causes more stress and anxiety with staff.  Community staff are just expected to use their own cars and although we get mileage back its not the same amount, the miles are reduced to what we actually travel and they expect staff to have copious amounts of equipment in their cars.  They do not pay for wear and tear on the vehicles either.  Staff morale is at an all time low.  I believe Mersey don't care need to focus on the services/buildings/staff they have before buying anymore that cannot be run/staff.

Merseycare does not cover the area where I live, I would consider moving as the services they offer are far better than my local organisation.  Compared to the trust I previously worked at, Merseycare is as well balanced for its employees as its patients.
More personal professional development courses to be offered for Band [number removed] health care workers.
More staff are needed to do our jobs effectively.  We are not robots and burnout is rife amongst the trust.  You can see this in the sickness rates and staff turnover.
More staff would be number one but most of the time we just have to get on with it.

My current team are amalgamating with other teams at present, it is a period of unsettled feelings and also whether funding will be approved for another year, I am concerned about placements I would be offered if funding was not approved.  Presently I still feel like we are working in as individual teams rather than a team as a whole.

My greatest sadness is that there is an absence of kindness, empathy and team working within the organisation.  I consider myself to be in a lucky position, I have a line manager to whom I can speak if I need to, however, there are other managers within our service who do not display these fundamental qualities.  Staff are clearly often torn between work and personal responsibilities; there seems to be little interest from our service in supporting their staff with all of their responsibilities and work is expected to take priority.  How can Mersey care's core business be in the caring field supporting patients with their mental health when they seem to have little interest in these aspects of their staff.  Some of our managers are just plain rude to their staff, I'm not sure how or why this is acceptable and tolerated.  In my opinion, there needs to be a full reset, boundaries between staff and patients are blurred and this needs addressing.  I'm not sure how we can expect the patients to adhere to rules when some of the staff are often unable to.

My last organisation valued their employees.  I don't feel we are valued as much as we were.  Especially staff at the new medium secure unit who work hard and are often understaffed.  The risks can be just as high as high secure yet high secure and low secure have had an increase on their lead and medium secure haven't had a rise in over 18 years.  Not the shop floor management but above don't have the staffs best interest at heart, they don't treat the staff well considering all that the staff do.  The moral is very low and the turnaround in staff leaving is astonishing.  If you looked at the numbers if all those who have left since Rowan View opened you would be asking why and what can we be doing better to keep staff.

My Service (Occupational Health) has gone through service remodeling since February 2024.  My role was no longer part of the new model.  The way we were informed of this in a huge group was appalling and felt humiliating and devaluing.  Since then there has been a complete lack of any empathy or value for me as a person going through this process of losing my job.  I am currently on redeployment and again information/communication has been very limited during this time.  I am now on trial for a post but feel very negative about the organisation and the way I and the rest of my team have been treated.  I think some learning needs to be taken from this.  As a valued member of staff who has committed to my role for [timeframe removed] as a Counsellor/EMDR Therapist and made a difference to many colleagues I have felt humiliated and devalued personally and within my profession.  I have never experienced this in all my[timeframe removed] within the NHS, this had led to me losing faith in the organisation as a whole.  The experience I have had over these last [timeframe removed] have caused extreme work related stress which hasn't been managed by higher management or HR in an appropriate way.
My team and manager are fantastic- if it wasn't from outside pressures of other teams/levels of safeguarding I would not consider moving job roles.
No communication, emails and queries unanswered.  Lack of support.  Lots of negativity.

No opportunity for progression when on family friendly hours for childcare/family life.  I suffer from severe [condition removed] attacks, sometimes feeling physically unwell and unable to carry out tasks (both in work and at home).  I feel like there isn't enough support from the organisation as a whole for conditions such as this, and this can lead to more stress and worsening of [condition removed] attacks and frequency.
No visa sponsorship is the reason why I am leaving the Trust.  So sad to leave.
None for now.
None.  I'm grateful to Mersey care for seconding me to do my RNDA training and I am looking forward to give my best to the TRUST and the clients by keeping all Mersey care values alive.
Nothing further to add.

Our ward has been understaffed over the past few weeks, leaving staff having to do back to back observation, sometimes without a break, I feel it is leaving the ward unsafe and staff at risk.  I feel that it is unfair that staff on high and medium secure are on more than staff on low secure wards, especially when our patient go out daily on leave and have more opportunities of secreting contraband or weapons onto the ward and coming back from their leave under the influence which makes our staff more at risk.

Over worked short staffed under valued and community district nursing needs investment and a revamp for it to carry on being fit for purpose in the future to carry on looking after a more complex ageing community, and staff working with out getting dinner breaks and proper rest periods.

Overall happy although I do believe the trust should focus on letting patients beloved to their own areas and closer to their families.  We see a lot of out of area patients who struggle as their families can't travel to see them.  It is detrimental to their recovery.
Overall.  Really like working for my team and the wider Mersey Care.  I have liked working in my team from day 1, which was 7 years ago.

People get promoted beyond their level of gross incompetence and training, then try to bully lower graded staff to make themselves look good.  Suggest managers/matrons go on training courses in leadership and staff management before being allowed to apply for higher paid roles, if they fail they don't get promoted.  That way you get a happier work force with managers/matrons that can do thy job the right way.  Look at the previous roles and qualifications other staff have and utilise those skills for the better of the trust.  Some staff at ward/department.  Level have held rank in the military or other organisations why not use those skills and knowledge.
Phlebotomy seem to be forgotten about as not even listed on the job roles and often feel ignored and undervalued.

Please can the sickness policy be reviewed, it does not promote being off ill when needed.  When on a stage, you feel forced to come into work if you are unwell.  Could the days of sickness not be calculated over your entire employed time with the Trust, not over the last year?  It can be very stressful knowing if your unwell you will be escalated to the next stage, when you are genuinely unwell.

Podiatry are sometimes not considered as a high profile department.  I think this is very sad.  The team work together and support each other.  The culture of care towards each other and our patients is very evident.  I [details removed] soon and will miss everyone.
Questions about managers have been answered in relation to my direct manager/line manager not senior management i.e. band 8 manager as most of my negative comments are in relation to these managers.

Really, really disappointed that the decision was made not to offer the secure care division any places on the latest Band [number removed] top up course, and then the further decision not to offer another course until March 2026, leaving myself and other Nurse Associates stranded.  International nursing recruitment drives are great, I fully recognise the need for a diverse workforce to work with a diverse patient group, but can the budget for those not be balanced with the funding to promote staff from within who have worked hard to come through Bands 2, 3, and 4 with the trust, want to continue progressing, but suddenly there is no funding for places because all the money has been spent on international recruitment?

Reorganising staff support was one of the worst things you could have done as a trust for staff wellbeing and more people will leave if you aren't willing to look after your own workers.  Mersey Care cares more about PR than how we are realistically managing on the wards.  I have worked at [number removed] other trusts and never experienced this level of corporation disconnectedness to its workers.
Sick of working understaffed on a regular basis working in a very potentially dangerous environment even when complaining to management it just gets brushed under the carpet,.

Some service users commented they can't sleep because staff talking too loud in night.  Some staff don't want to clear stained laundry accumulated in floor, or they are not interested to clean dining hall, kitchen etc.  Especially at night.  Or they don't want take used cups/dishes from patients rooms.  Most of student nurses don't want to clean kitchen or take laundry.  Expats like me shall be respected a bit better way.   85% service users day they are given wrong food choices.  Most of the nurses and some care staffs sit most times in ward offices, my suggestion is they've to interact with patients, especially who are upset.  Service users who came mentally ill are going out their illness doubles or multiplied.  Patients need novice ideas or activities to relieve boredom being in wards or hospitals.  Arrange a facility to go church on Sunday Mass for those who are interested.  More sessions of counselling are appreciated for patients.  If a staff feel not supported where and whom I can delt with?  All the best.
Sometimes get frustrated when I attend work very rarely off don't feel get recognition for good/excellent attendance.  Team members who let the team down by continually not being work.

Sometimes patient rights come before staff welfare making the environment unsafe, which at time results in staff being assaulted and being signed off work being injured at work.  Senior managers and doctors making decisions on patients behaviour and their risks without spending time with the patients on the ward and observing.  This upsets staff as they are the ones who witness to patients behaviour daily and then feel that their input and fears are not counted for.

Staff are empathetic, approachable and helpful at EDS.  Historically work have been kind and attentive when I've had [condition removed] struggles in the past.  They've also directed me to appropriate support when I've needed it.  I've struggled with the lack of opportunities for career progression outside of psychological backgrounds.  I don't really feel encouraged to develop my skills or improve at what I do - and I'd like to.  I'd like to be able to contribute more, understand my role in more detail and be equipped to do my job better.  I'd also appreciate more training, rather than simply relying on my instincts in the room.

Staff do not feel supported when things go wrong in the workplace, workplace restrictions suspensions this is why staff decide to leave the Trust and cannot attract new staff, due to the poor reputation.
Staffing is a major issue on the ward I work on.  Often there can be 1:1 observations for 4 patients, with 6 staff being allocated.  This leaves the ward unsafe to work in.

Staffing issues, always short staffed as the work load increase's, unrealistic pay, inequality regards retention payments for staff, pay in line with retail or hospitality however Mersey Care have outstanding values and training opportunities.
Talking no longer feels like a mode of communication, too much email and computer time which is not read or analysed by senior staff.

Talking Therapies really needs to hire more therapists as the waiting lists are far too long and staff are always leaving which puts more pressure on existing staff.  I think the only way to do this would be to look at increasing the pay banding particularly for psychological wellbeing practitioners as we are very underpaid therefore not many people stay in the job role for long.

Thank for the survey.  It was helpful.  For the question section of stating if a staff would leave or stay in the Organisation, I think there should a follow up question that would ask why the staff is planning of leaving the organisation.

Thank you for allowing a recent away day with our team.  We had a really good day and it helped me appreciate my colleagues and line managers efforts in providing a thoughtful, low cost activity that we could all take part in and enjoy time together away from the work environment.
Thank you.

The current incident reporting system Radar in my opinion is not fit for purpose for community services and is related to impatient care so we are faced with a lot of irrelevant questions and is confusing for community staff to complete it they actually manage to complete form!  This issue has been raised with our managers.
