We have recently been asked to complete a work plan which is basically time in motion and we were shown previous results led by therapies in a hospital setting that cannot relate to community services.  I personally felt this only added to stress levels in myself in that I was under performing.  Clearly you would be able to spend more face to face time with patients in a ward setting than in the community.  We were also given the breakdown for banding i.e. band 4 and 5 in a hospital setting would have 95% face to face time as they have no other clinical responsibilities i.e. supervision of other staff.  In reality a band 4 and 5 in the community in our team is not allocated only north, south or central localities but visits all, so this would incur increased travel time.  I felt, and aware other team members felt same, doing a presentation that was completed in a hospital setting was not a true representation for community services and I felt under unfair pressure due to this.  Our work in community is very different, we are not supplying equipment urgent for discharge but working in the home setting which is very different in respect to increased time for the patients we see, waiting for adaptations from housing associations in some instances.  Can take as long as 6 months and as a result these patients remain on our caseloads.
The management has no care for staff welfare and this is very painful.
The morale of the team is so low because of band 8a.  They disregard people's needs and holds grudges.

The negative feedback I have provided (i.e. last 12 months) applies to when I worked for Rowan View.  I have since moved to a different team within Mersey Care and now feel very happy in my new role with both work/ personal life satisfaction.  Working at Rowan View took a significant toll on my wellbeing due to negative attitudes and consistent disrespect from both managers and and workers on the wards.  Staffing at Rowan View is poor and, due to this, staff were constantly on observations and unable to facilitate therapeutic activities for patients to aid their recovery.  It is a real shame as Rowan View has so much potential.

The only time I feel drained in my job is when I work with another team 3 months a year.  I love the job but travel to work 9 hours driving in 3 days.  But I understand this is part of my job and it is only 3 months out of the year.

The organisation seems to have a priority of acquiring new services and expanding its foothold on services without due care in how to improve the services they currently have or the effect it has on the staff.  Staff seem bottom of the list on priorities for a number of years with executives unaware of how their decisions affect frontline staff.  Mersey doesn't care is a byword for how staff have to referred to the organisation for many years and known by staff from other trusts.  I have worked for the trust before foundation trust status and left due to the poor communication from managers and executives who also did not take staff concerns seriously, I returned due to limited opportunities in my chosen field without moving out of area.  Whilst I have seen improvements in staff opportunities to advance their careers with apprenticeships, poor senior leadership, poor recruitment, staff shortages and unrealistic expectations are putting staff and service users at risk.

The organisation should address systemic issues such as racism.  There appears to be a lack of representation within executive roles within the NHS.  Issues such as racism are not addressed in a way which makes staff or service users feel safe within the NHS.  A good example of this is the recent racist riots - how were staff/service users supported and where was the direction from the top to guide individual service responses.  Issues of racism continue to exist following the riots and the NHS should be developing a service where staff and service users feel safe.  It should also look at solutions for people on waiting lists, consider how the pressure put on services to see more people can impact patient care i.e. if there is one rigid model or treatment pathway can this be patient-centred?  There should also be more work done to ensure that recommendations are followed up and followed through with at a service level - research should be utilised to better outcomes for staff and service users.

The pay is poor for the level of education and experience needed (an undergrad degree, a masters and 3 years clinical experience for a band 4 job).  PCs and printers need to be updated so we can do our jobs to the best of our abilities.  Onboarding into this role took 5 months!

The progression opportunities within the trust Psychology professions without core professions are very limited as is the access to external training.  It would be great if the trust implemented more apprenticeship opportunities to widen access to the psychology professions which are so sought after within teams and there appears to be a consistent shortage.

The service is poorly run and staff concerns are not addressed effectively.  Burn out is normalised and staff expected to just put up with it as they are told that that is how the role is.  Senior management do not care about staff wellbeing and repeatedly make decisions about service changes that put further strain on staff wellbeing without even consulting them.  Changes to the service are being made frequently which confuses staff and mean that there is never any consistency as to what we are meant to do.  Staff are expected to work beyond their remit and their training and beyond the limits of their accreditation.  Staff are told that they should work outside of their remit and essentially not record it on the system so that the service can still maintain accreditation.  Staff morale is very low and burn out is very high and the service is doing nothing about it because they care more about the targets than the staff who are burning themselves out trying to reach them.

The sick leave policy feels punitive, especially giving people on stage one who may be unwell reduced sick leave for the following year.  This causes a lot of stress and makes it difficult for people to manage their health, forcing people to work even when they are not well enough to do so.  This is particularly problematic for people with long term health conditions/ other disabilities and makes it harder for people with disabilities to remain in work.

There are very few opportunities for career progression within the role as an assistant psychologist.  Many assistant psychologists are aspiring to get onto clinical training and become clinical psychologists, however, many courses desire research experience, can the trust not arrange research opportunities for assistants within the trust and support them in publishing and disseminating said research?  Additionally, some APs may not wish to pursue a doctorate, can the trust not provide pathways for assistant psychologists to train and become psychotherapists, there are a few paid internships around the country, can the trust not do the same?  Many courses offering CBT training could require assistant psychologists to complete KSAs (Knowledge, skills and Attitudes portfolios), the trust could provide training places or support assistants in getting into training for CBT, DBT etc and then support them to have roles within the trust as trained psychotherapists who have relationships within teams and with the trust.  Furthermore, many assistant posts within the trust are band 4 pay, current bps guidelines would suggest that they should be paid band 5.  Many assistants may wish to stay in the trust if they were paid fairly and/ or had opportunities to undertake supported research projects or were supported by the trust to complete training in psychotherapy or even to develop specialist skills in other areas.
There is a culture of bullying within the organisation amongst the lower grade staff that leads to high absence and turnover of staff.

There is a difficult member of staff within our department (Occupational Therapy) and when multiple members of staff have raised concerns or complaints about this staff member nothing has been done about it.  I have been personally targeted by this staff member - a member of management has witnessed and knows they are targeting me however they have done nothing about it and allowed it to carry on happening.  Despite knowing my mental health has been significantly impacted by this colleague the manager for our department has called me unprofessional and given me no support.  Went to freedom to speak up over the issue and my manager has not spoke to me since.  This colleague has caused problems within our department for over a year and a half, causing poor mental health in colleagues as well as colleagues leaving and looking for other jobs.

There is currently no way for me to progress through the NHS in this role (Current band [number and Job title removed]).  Opportunities for career progression would be great as would enable me to stay within my service or the NHS and also provide the opportunity to enhance the service I currently provide to patients.

There seemed to be no one that makes decisions always has to be passed up to someone else, there is never solutions for problems that arise, the safety on the wards e.g. security, inexperienced staff, not knowing what to do on the ward agency or bank, lack of staff on the wards and in other departments e.g. OT.  Not feeling valued as a team or respect for others about our job, job role.
This form took a very long time to complete.

This is the worst trust I have ever worked for in my [timeframe removed] of service within the NHS.  They have no regard for staff health/well being, they have continually lied to us face to face.  As soon as I can find another job I will leave.

This organisation views people by their bed status and whether they "qualify" to be here rather than how to best support patients.  From what I have witnessed, staff on the wards do their absolute best and are the most compassionate people I know, however it is the constant pressure from the matrons caring more about beds that make patient care inadequate at times.  There have been several discharges that I have witnessed that I believe were unsafe and this not been down to the nurses but the pressure from the higher ups (matrons etc.  ).  My line manager is fantastic and supportive, however the people who are higher than them have no compassion or empathy towards their staff.  Due to my role being a training role, they know that we are completely replaceable and do not care whatsoever about our mental health or wellbeing.  I have even tried raising concerns to the Associate Director of Psychological Professions for that to go nowhere, again proving that the organisation and the higher ups do not care.  I have recently been off work for over [timeframe removed] with work related stress, to come back into work and be told that I am not allowed to say that it is work related, instead I have to have a meeting with HR to decide this.  The organisation then wonders why people are not wanting to stay and leaving instead, I would love to know how a meeting with HR is supposed to be unbiased and supportive when they are the main people who will make sure to cover and protect the organisation.  This meeting with HR also isn't mandatory, I have had to request it and then fill in additional paperwork to try and defend my case that this is work related.  The irony is not lost on me or I imagine others, that if we report to be stressed at work, we're gaslit into being told that it is not and they will add more stress onto us to ensure we don't report it formally.  How the organisation views sickness and the stages is ableist, although there is such thing as a HR27, this does not mean that your sickness due to your chronic condition is acceptable, it is still down to the managers discretion and they can choose to ignore it.  As someone who has worked in the NHS since 2020, I find it ironic how little Mersey Care care about their staff and boast how they're one of the most profitable organisations when there are staffing crises everywhere because of their bad reputation.
To become a hospital again, and not a YMCA or housing crisis unit.
Toxic culture in management needs serious reform.
Under paid, undervalued, management trying to dodge giving us back pay for a role which has changed drastically over a long time.
Very good survey.
Very recently under a new team leader and have positive hopes for a better team now.
Very unsettling at moment in work.  Getting moved more frequently.  Feel there are a lot of cliques on ward and favouritism shown regarding moving and shifts with certain staff members.

We as a service are often short staffed and very often have staff taken to cover other wards.  This results in staffing pressures and unrealistic support targets.  Which lead to a knock on effect to our patients and their mental health when they have restrictions ward activities and leave both Section 17 and grounds leave.
We have far to many meetings and mandatory training.

We have not a clue about how to deal with the transgender issue to make it safe for all service users.  The management and organisation gives the strong impression that they don't care who is occupying a bed.
We no longer work in set groups but work flexi-shifts which I find is detrimental to good team work and patient care.
What happened to the word team.  Teams are meant to work together and be proud of what they do.
When I pressed yes for have I seen any near misses in the last month Patients have come in with wrong blood forms from GPs.

When this asks about managers I do not mean my line manager, my line manager is fantastic, we have been taken over by Mersey Care, since this takeover we have lost most of our staff, we have lots of staff sickness, and the rest of us are looking for other jobs, out of Mersey Care.  Mersey Care are horrendous to work for, they do not care what you are saying how you are feeling, they burn you out.  Far too many managers, it angers me so much, no budget for pay rises especially low paid, however pay these managers who do not even know your job to know how to help, yet they sit day in and day out on teams meetings discussing how to get more work out of you.  Filling out this survey will not do a single thing either!  Maybe you will all go on a teams meeting.
Why is the Trust not giving the opportunity to staff that desire to stay working?  The policy on not sponsoring from Band 3 is mental torture.
Would like to see required staffing levels on wards.  When staffing levels low high acuity on ward dangerous for staff feel other patients not getting time with them.  This needs to be consistent.
You never get an opinion the ward manager is so rude and disrespectful its always " if I say jump you ask how high " awful no respect and always short staffed.
A place of work to hot desk from. Working from home all week can be lonely.  Its sometimes nice to be in a full office grabbing a coffee and seeing a smiling face.

Admin staff are being forced to work in the office full time.  Admin are told it doesn't work from home without explanation, it is not true and many admin staff can do their entire job remotely clinical staff are treated better and are allowed to work remotely when they can.  Admin staff are being treated unfairly and do not get the respect they deserve.

Although this is a great organisation to work for, I do feel many staff abuse the good nature and support of Mersey care as it is a 'Mental Health Advocate'.  I feel HR and decision making on staff is too lenient when it comes to sickness, absence and performance.  Other organisations have trial periods for staff to 'prove' they can do what they confirmed at interview.  Here, as long as you pass the interview, even if poor at your job, you have a job for life.  I work with staff who are on decent pay but play the fool or blame mental health on their performance which is accepted.  They are allowed to continue on low input when others have to work harder to accommodate them and I feel management have their hands tied as HR and Occupational Health support the individual more instead of appreciating the impact on the department/service that the staff member works for.  The organisation and NHS is struggling to provide a great service for patients as we are too busy dealing with problematic staff.  Absence and sickness is too lenient, staff are staged but don't seem to care as staging leads to nothing.  In other organisations the processes in place made you think twice about how bad your record would look having too much sickness and absence and equally you felt bad letting your colleagues down.  Mersey care allow poor attendees and performers to move freely from one department to another with no responsibility.  I feel we are very lucky with the privileges we have working for this organisation as I have worked for some terrible and strict employers and it angers me that with the state the NHS is in, that we have staff who think the organisation owes them a better life and spend more time not working than working and complain.  They have either never worked for another organisation, or forgotten how lucky we are.
Another tick box exercise.

As a manager I struggle to manage my team with the policies that are in place, I feel that these policies allow my team to abuse them especially the sickness policies - the number of people within my team who make a miraculous recovery at the 6 month period is amazing - coincidentally this is the point that sick pay drops to half pay.

As an admin assistant I feel totally indispensable at the moment.  Events have occurred which although they are being looked into I feel this always comes down to cost rather than staff safety.  Far too many managers who seem to give conflicting replies to queries and admin not being replaced making work much more stressful.  Expected to cover a wide variety of duties in different venues so trying to split myself in too many different directions.  I am totally disenchanted with my role at the moment and can't see this improving any time soon.

As the NHS continues to transform digitally to inform our health outcomes for our patients and clinical services.  It is disappointing to see the staff survey does not reflect this in its data collection.  No real acknowledgement or data capture of long term health conditions and neurodiversity.  This will impact upon perceptions, use and engagement with the process.

Band 2 staff that are not just doing (what is described in their role for Band 2), so doing extra in their roles and responsibility's should be on a Band 3.  Getting information from hospitals, having to collect sickle cell results and booking sickle blood tests etc should surely be on a Band 3 as this is more intense but vital for the team and especially the patient.
Banding needs to be sorted out for Band 2, they've taken the pay scale from Band 2's to give to the Band 8's.  There is no progression route where I am and even just for admin staff in general.
Brilliant trust to work for.
Bullying culture in patient safety team is still prevalent and needs to be addressed.
Can I point out that my team sits within a wider team.  My smaller team recognises my worth whereas I feel the wider team does not fully appreciate my role.

Communication could be improved, to ensure that strategic information is shared to allow dissemination across the wider team.  Information is often not shared in a timely manner i.e. when information requests have been emailed to some, and then not shared with the team/acted upon, which can then mean work / for example data requests.  FOI's have to be actioned in a unrealistic timeframes - creates pressure in the team.  Decision making can be slow which causes added work with chasing for responses and frustration within the team.  The team are often asked for.  Uncivil, unkind behaviours of [some] senior leaders in the organisation, are not challenged and are accepted causing upset, stress unnecessary work pressures to others.  Team is often subjected to confrontational and often aggressive behaviours by some staff side colleagues, which has seen little improvement despite escalation.  Some meetings with management could be more structured, action focused so they feel like an effective use of time and if meetings are not going ahead due to non attendance that this is shared in advance - again improved communication.

Currently I am clear on what my responsibilities are, although this can change sometimes.  I feel because my role is split, Senior Admin Support and Admin Team Leader, I cannot dedicate my time fully to both.  When applying and accepting my role, it was advertised as "Senior Administrator", and this is what is on my Employment Contract.  I do not feel it was clear that my role would be split.  My Job Description states no mention of managing staff within different teams or different localities.  I currently manage six staff across Liverpool and Sefton Place.

Decision makers need to listen to clinicians rather than just say ridiculous comments like patients can book their own appointments online as their [family member removed] can do it and they are [age removed]!
Desktop support team is neglected by management nothing is ever done to support us or when we raise concerns they seem incapable or don't care to other support or assistance.

Difficult survey to complete due to situation of admin staff being under an admin management hub and no longer under the management of the team they work for - this is causing frustration, resentment, stress, and feelings of being under valued.  Removal of admin staff from their clinical colleagues in services where they work together closely is causing admin staff to feel demotivated, disengaged and separated from their clinical counterparts and the collective ethos of the service.  It is also disorganised, inefficient, impractical and frustrating way to work.  Decisions and opinions are made externally without the input of the staff from the service either clinical or admin which has bearing on the operational running of the department but without any knowledge - leaving staff to pick up the pieces and seek out work around solutions which is time wasting and exhausting.  Admin staff have also now been denied the opportunities of flexible working and told must be office based 5 days a week regardless of job role - when seeking clarity over this have been told that it does not work and it is 'the directive from above' - which has caused feeling of resentment, undermining and under-valued.
Divide between Trust Wide Support Services and Clinical Divisions seem to be getting bigger.

Divisional staff are very supportive.  Staff from Trust Wide Support Services do not offer any support, they just ask for information constantly, expecting the divisions to drop everything to deal with any requests from themselves.  It does not feel like Trust Wide Support Services appreciate the work that is completed by the divisions.  Often when Trust Wide Support Services are liaising with staff they often speak to them in a derogatory manner, if they speak to divisional staff in this way, how do they speak to their own teams..  Teams within Trust Wide Services are also working from home the majority of the time and have no idea what it is like in the divisional services, this isn't a good look when dealing with staff who are in full time.

Due to the impact of working from home and people being reluctant to work from an office base, I feel like working relationships have suffered and that work is not always coordinated in a way to deliver a quality standard.  I also feel the focus can be based on just getting things done instead of ensuring things are done properly to give employees/new starters constructive feedback.

Far too much micromanagement within our organisation.  Too many non-patient facing managers view the world through data on a screen rather than viewing feelings, emotions and people as exactly what they are - people.  With families, responsibilities and life issues.  Help, don't hinder them or you will lose very good staff.  In fact you are losing very good staff.

Feel that service mergers/changes for example for services now covering wider catchment area there is reduced quality of service/more pressure on clinical staff/room for error e.g.  For cases whereby they have not seen the same member of staff more than once for follow-up reviews especially in urgent care community setting.  Overall, feel that there is uncertainty regarding roles/service changes which impacts on staff morale and wellbeing and has meant that staff have moved roles due to this causing more pressure on staff left within teams.
Feel very disheartened, demotivated, with little hope or faith that things will ever improve based on own personal experiences.
Feel very valued by my manager and feel part of wider team.  Don't believe I am valued I by the trust as a whole.

Flexible working is not available within our team unless that means wanting to reduce working days or hours.  Working from home is not an option unless you have an appointment it is not built into our working lives to help us have a better work life balance as mentioned in the flexible and hybrid working policy.
Flexible working needs to be improved if you care for somebody who has cancer.  Trust also needs to look after staff when returning from long term sick.

Flexible working when you have caring duties still seems frown upon and at times you can be made to feel uncomfortable.  I know the Trust has worked hard at implementing this, however there still appears to be taboo around this.

Following on from the large scale organisational change in 2022/2023 this past year 23/24 has been about consolidation, relationships have stabilised and grown, staff have had more respect for each other in their different roles and supported to do their jobs.  Flexible working is supported in the organisation.

For a mental health trust, Mersey Care does not seem to focus enough on the mental health of it's employees or educating staff the understanding of mental health conditions.  I have regularly been made to feel like a burden.  In my team/department, there is also not enough handover/support/training for new starters.

Frustration exists because of the poor Management at certain levels and failure of a consistent approach in accordance with the Trusts Policies and Procedures by all Mangers.  Staff are treated differently dependent on who there Manager is.  This is to the point were some staff get away with using the Absence Reporting line some can work from home others cant.  Some can go off sick and work from home others have to go off sick even though they have offered to work at home due to cold, COVID etc. where they can contribute work from home and not leave team depleted.  Inconsistent approach breeding discontentment.  HR need to under take more Audits and have more of a presence holding workshops etc. so that Managers are aware that staff can speak out.
Good people, poor leadership.  Better organisation would take away some of the stress.
Great organisation to work for.
Happy to come to work everyday.  We have a lovely Team and all get on well and support each other.
Happy with my job and management.

Having the opportunity to work from home especially for roles who are are not clinical and responsible for own workload.  More opportunities for admin staff in terms of training (even if not directly linked to role) and progression.  More of a dedicated and valued approach towards admin staff.

Health and wellbeing of colleagues seems not to be a priority for the trust.  The trust wants to be seen as forward thinking, and a leader in various topics, but the support from the most senior managers in the trust doesn't support that.  The biggest working demographic in our trust is people of menopause age, yet there isn't the support for colleagues experiencing menopause to be well in work.  'Service need' is why reasonable adjustments aren't made, but what this ultimately leads to is people going off sick because of menopause, which has a detrimental effect on the service and trust as a whole.  The trust wants to become a menopause friendly workplace, but it is far from that at the moment.  There should be mandatory training on menopause for line managers, which is promoted by senior leads within the trust.  There are a few teams within the trust who seem to be menopause friendly, but with a trust of +12K colleagues, a few teams is not enough.  There doesn't seem to be the willingness to support colleagues as a general rule, which is leading to not only more sickness absence within the trust, but people are leaving the trust as they are not supported.

How am I supposed to trust and believe in my organisation when my line managers' actions resulted in a serious suicide attempt by my daughter?  They did not think through the consequences or apologise.

How come this survey is supposed to be anonymous, yet I continually receive notifications to complete the survey?  It's a joke, the same as the Organisation as a whole.  Lowly Clerks are the lowest of the low.  I would never suggest anyone work for this organisation and I cannot wait to leave.
HR backing staff that shouldn't be here makes me feel less motivated in my role.  Clear breaches of conduct but ignored due to "Just and Learning Culture".
HR policies are not always followed in a timely manner leading to stress and confusion.

Huge concern that the stability and innovation of the last decade under outgoing CEO must be protected and developed.  We need the future management structure to be as broad in experience and as proactive.
Hybrid working is only allowed for senior managers, which is grossly prejudicing other staff especially admin staff when their role allows them to work hybrid.

I am [age removed] old, soon will be looking to go part time or step down to a less demanding roles.  My role has changed since I first joined this team, a good deal of the work I enjoyed most has shifted to another team.  I don't enjoy the role as much as I used to because of this.  I'm actively looking for something to hold my interest a little more.  Unfortunately not easy to change direction at my age, no doubt I will find something I usually do.  It is a good organisation to work for, I just need to find something a little more interesting to finish up my working days before I retire - I've been through a lot over the past [timeframe removed], especially around my [family member removed] getting [condition removed] - it has been hard and the ability to work from home and with more fixability has be of great benefit, especially when they needed looking after and being immobile after  an operation - please keep allowing a more flexible work environment for employees available as it can be a life saver.

I am becoming more frustrated with my role.  I am consistently asked to cover work colleagues who are not working core hours and take lots of time off, over and above normal limits.  This is all very secretive and as a team we cannot get to the bottom of this.  We have asked to meet with managers but this request has been ignored.  There is no support from managers and no chance to progress or move.  This is something as a group, that we are going to try and resolve.  When I joined Mersey Care it was a wonderful place to work, but year on year my view changing and Mersey Care is not a great place to work.  The Trust are losing good members of staff due to various issues which never seem to be resolved, but occur time and again.

I am disappointed with The Life Rooms and I have raised my concerns about staff turnover and long term work related sick leave.  I am a confident person; however, many other people won't challenge SLT on their actions.  Colleagues have shared with their reasons for sick leave and the reasons why they have resigned, and it is most often because they feel undervalued, and sometime bullied by senior managers.  Many of us feel we have been sold a false narrative about what our place within the wider community is.  There have been several incidents recently that could have been avoided or reduced risk if the senior members of the team had more experience working with community members with complex support needs.  One incident had a regular service user committing public indecency and sexual assault towards two staff members.  One staff member is off long term due to the trauma and the other has resigned partly because of this incident, and I suspect because of the inadequate treatment received afterwards.  The perpetrator had been reported on multiple occasions to management because of clear drug use in attendance, and this had been totally overlooked.  There are many other incidents that have been played down internally, however after recent health and safety review many staff now feel heard externally, and that the false narrative projected is opening the eyes of the rest of Mersey Care into the inner politics and divisions between teams.  We are told from day one that we don't manage risk and that it's as safe as working in a cafe at The Life Rooms.  This is something I have questioned and challenged since I began my role here.  I believe many SLT members are out of touch with the front line and don't realise how many cases of mental health, housing and financial crisis we support people with daily.  Their judgement that being nonclinical makes us a casual service is naive at best and disinterested at worst.  I feel that many managers lower down on the pay scale are disempowered and not respected for their knowledge and experience.  Being on a higher pay grade does not make a person more superior or knowledgeable.  I think some decisions on recruitment of those in very senior roles are based on academic achievement, and not direct transferable skills.  Some of the decisions made by people in senior positions are clearly demonstrating low experience and their lack of knowledge on managing unsafe behaviours of service users.  This is no longer shocking to me, as it's become part of our culture.  I have always been disturbed about hierarchal behaviours of people in senior roles, and regularly witness communication that is disrespectful and patronising.  Micromanagement is allowed leading to staff members feeling low and disempowered.  A lot of the time it is unnecessary scrutiny, I don't understand how people in high positions of authority have time to nit-pick minor details.  I have asked the question to several SLT members directly how they feel about being consistently short staffed because people leave or go on sick leave because of the culture.  We have lost a lot of excellent staff members, and this saddens me.  I was subject to bullying two years ago which knocked my confidence and put me in secondary mental health care as a patient for the first time in over a decade.  This prevented me for applying for jobs elsewhere.  After mental health intervention and a boost in confidence I will be moving on when a suitable role comes up.  I love The Life Rooms frontline, that is why some of my survey answers may seem contradictive.  My direct line manager is excellent along with the team I belong to.  The challenges I have at work are a lack of consistency, only negative feedback being given to staff and the detachment and separation from SLT.  I believe in the work we are supposed to deliver, so it saddens me that I want to leave.  I have never worked in an organisation that is so top heavy without any of our staff knowing exactly what goes on "upstairs".  This service needs full transparency of roles and actions all across the board.
I am happy in my work environment and with my colleagues who I choose to call a second family.
I am happy in my work place and my job, I like the people I work with.

I am happy within my role at Merseycare however I feel there needs to be a stronger more effective process when involving absence.  I find there is little or no support from HR in these matters and seems to be no consequence for staff who have extensive sickness.  I spend a lot of my time dealing with team members absence instead of using my time for more development of staff that seem to want to be here, give back and have strong team values.
I am really happy in my role and love the team I work in.  I enjoy having the flexibility of hybrid working, it is very valuable with juggling work and family life.
I am thinking of retire and return.  The last medical secretary was turned down to do this and has since left the Trust.  I will ask and see what the outcome is before making any decisions.  Thanks.

I am truly disappointed in the lack of support that the organisation has provided me over the last [timeframe removed] during some really trying times.  If it weren't for my immediate team providing support, for which I am ever grateful, I don't know how I would have got through everything.  It's such a shame to be treated like this when you put your all into everything that you need to do.

I am worried about constant change in the organisation and how long its taking to settle executive leadership and this is destabilising and unsettling for people and team.  I feel as though Chair and Non-Executive Directors are far too operational and that this creates unnecessary work and disruption to the organisation.  There are still many problems around HR services who can be very slow to respond and so often staff relationships and management becomes complicated and damaged.  I believe this service needs quite a lot of reform in order to get the balance right.  Managers need to be able to manage but many of them feel they will not be backed or supported by HR so they don't act without them.  HR then gets swamped with requests that they can't get through fast enough and so consequently the management situation gets worse.  This is often exploited by Staffside.  We need to make sure managers have trust and confidence in support services like HR and that the organisation will support them to make the right decision.  I do worry that we are in danger of throwing out the baby with the bathwater as we focus on getting the basics right.  There are significant innovations that have developed over the last decade and I am concerned that we are not valuing them sufficiently.  There's a danger that leadership knows the price of everything but the value of nothing, or at least the value of very little.  Without some of this freshness of perspective and innovation Mersey Care is really only a collection of quite average clinical services.  It takes much more to make an organisation outstanding.  I think there is quite a clinical arrogance about the organisation and that non-clinical opinions are not valued.  This is dangerous as it takes a wide range of skills and experiences to make an organisation safe and successful.  There's a danger that this will take some of the talent out of the organisation and again that may just reduce us to a collection of quite unremarkable clinical services.

I believe all phone calls should be recorded, or if not all phone calls, then definitely the teams that receive a high level of abusive/inappropriate calls.  SU's regularly record us.  Staff would feel more protected in their job roles and able to feel empowered to escalate these calls more quickly.  I feel staff are 'expected' to deal with these calls, as its past culture, managers are too busy to respond quickly due to already high levels of staff absence, which then results in ongoing calls, causing stress, anxiety and potential sick leave/leaving the job.  Staff in lower bandings/receptionists suffer the most and need more resilience training.

I believe the main problem we face at the moment is with high sickness absences.  This is monitored by line managers within the team accordingly but rarely any action taken once staff reach stage 3, this seems to be due to to delays/push back from HR.  I believe this is causing a lot of wasted resource, money and time and also causes a decrease of staff morale constantly having to cover sickness.

I come under admin staff and feel that we are expected to do to much for our current job role i.e. expected to cover a wide range of duties including your own job in different venues - which at the moment is impossible, due to lack of staff.  This makes me feel indispensable.  I feel that where health and safety for staff comes is always down to costing.  Staff should feel safe in their workplace and I do not at present.

I did love my job for a long time, but the way things are changing don't make things better.  Admin seem to be a forgotten group.  The rules for everyone else don't seem to apply to admin.  We are not allowed to work from home as there is a dip in service, but doctors/nurses/support worker etc. all do.  There has been no discussion about flexible working for admin.  The Trust carer leave policy is a farce.  [family member and condition removed], and has numerous appointments each week.  They can't drive themself and I need to take them.  I get no carer leave, and have to take annual leave.  I therefore have no down time.  All holidays are hospital appointments, all weekends are filled doing chores that they can't do, and all the Trust can offer is unpaid leave when my holidays run out.  I am the only wage earner in my house currently and reduced wages will make everything just that bit more difficult.  With regards to roles, too many people are now made to get involved in other peoples roles, which causes confusion, conflict and resentment.  People make decisions about work that falls in someone else's role without consulting the person who actually does the job.  Few days go by where work colleagues don't complain about knowing their place as someone has stepped on their toes.  Moral is low throughout most work groups.  Luckily the team I work in look after each other within the team as no-one likes to see their colleagues struggling.  There is quite a lot of covert bullying in the background.  Having reported poor practice and bullying in the past, and not receiving the support I should have because both experiences were with a pay grade higher than my own, I would never report this again.  You are left to deal with the fallout yourself, and if a doctor is involved, managers like to step back as doctors are hard to come by.  This makes you feel like you are too far down the ladder to matter and that you would be easier to replace, unlike the medical staff!  People do say that they appreciate your work, and that they would be lost without you - unfortunately actions say otherwise!
I do enjoy my role and coming to work every day, but feel the JD and PS are out of date and certainly do not reflect the role I do in the trust.

I do feel that if we are to hear more from the public at engagement meetings that the title: 'Your Voice, Your Change' needs to change to something as simple as: 'Have your say about the NHS'.  There does seem to be constant change/s which can be unsettling for staff.  Health prevention should be more actively promoted.

I do not believe service user experience is priority and management appear self-serving in only allocating resources to their specific areas of attention and importance without wider considerations.  Policies and procedures are not worth the paper they are written on when management do not follow them and their superiors do not enforce correct protocol.  Poor performance appears to be rewarded with promotions and pats on the back.  Staff safety both personal and psychological do not appear to be important.  Heavy reliance on low level staff to run the services whilst management are overwhelmed by paperwork and meetings.

I don't know how these surveys reach useful conclusions.  NHS needs to address ''most senior person knows best'' culture.  Insightful decisions are not made by people who have made themselves well informed about services.  People do not understand or value the roles of others.  Different teams could work much better together; hostility towards other teams is common.  Surveys ask about the manager immediately above, but the impact upon wellbeing and effectiveness in role is often influenced by other managers.

I feel admin should be tiered and paid according to the level you are on.  Not all admin do the same work or carry the same responsibilities, yet we are all paid a Band 3 salary.  Our admin team, for example, could be a Band 3 but at a higher tier salary than another admin team because of the nature of our work.  I feel we are underpaid for the responsibilities we carry.

I feel admin staff are treated differently than clinical staff in the NHS, for example flexible working and working from home!  Clinicians nursery nurses school nurses can all work from home, but admin in my team are not allowed and the explanation being other members of staff have been abusing it?  The times I have been allowed to work from home all my work gets done, so why not tackle the problem with the individuals who are abusing it?  I feel that's a big problem in the NHS and I have worked here for over [timeframe removed].
I feel appreciated by the clinical team that I work for, but feel that generally administration is undervalued and is often overlooked as a service group in the NHS.

I feel it would be beneficial if admin staff could be involved in decision making as we are the ones who lead on the processes.  We are currently the last ones to know and often are not informed, it is only be default we become aware of changes made to processes.  I also think we should have monthly/bi monthly meetings with Health Care Professionals to ascertain any problem areas or to contribute by suggestion of how to improve our service.

I feel our Trust has the right intent and vision in place however this seems to drift when it comes down to making it a reality.  I left a non-clinical community service within the Trust to move to another dept as it was exceptionally dysfunctional and this was being overlooked at exec level - there were a number of FTSUs from across staff teams, lots of data on why staff were leaving but this didn't make any impact into how this service was managed and senior managers are continuing to operate a toxic work culture unchallenged, this leads to a lack of trust in any positive initiatives or processes in place to support staff.  Intensions are honourable but implementation is sadly not quite there.

I feel that Admin staff should be given an opportunity to work from home.  I have had other roles in the NHS where it has been allowed and creates a better working environment and less people tend to stay off sick.  It seems people are only given this opportunity if they are unable to make it into work on the day, which is unfair to the rest of the staff who have made it into work.
I feel that as admin we are overlooked with being able to work from home.

I feel that at times staff behaviours are not dealt with, there is an unfair disparity between staff with and those without disabilities.  Staff with children are not always supported.  Communication is not always the best and the organisation feels at times like a dictatorship/done to rather than with organisation.

I feel that most things are theoretical within the Trust and what looks good on paper, isn't put into practice.  There is a huge disparity between how lower bands and admin staff are treated compared to qualified and band 5 and above.  MH division admin staff are not allowed to work at home, however, others divisions are and it is discriminatory.  I used to feel really proud of working for the NHS, but the culture seems to get worse all the time and staff are becoming less happy, but unable to be honest about it.  I think staff morale is at an all time low.

I feel that our immediate line managers should have more input and decision making in our roles within the individual needs of the teams as all teams work differently.  I think, as close knit teams, decisions should be made locally and not trust wide.

I feel that the rapid expansion and now huge size of the organisation has not been for the better.  It is difficult to know who does what in terms of roles and responsibilities, even with the various organograms.  I feel that because of this, wider communication is poor and there is a lot of duplication of work and wasted resources as people are not aware that a piece of work is already happening elsewhere in the organisation.

I feel that the Trust's Respect and Civility Policy favours the perpetrator and doesn't consider the feelings of the person on the receiving end, e.g. of bullying in the workplace.  I don't feel mediation is always the answer and the Trust should take appropriate disciplinary action in some cases.

I feel that there could be more support for staff with neuro divergent conditions especially around recruitment and selection process.  I do not feel that staff are aware of what adjustments can be requested for interview and there is not anything to request on the interview acceptance form as it is more related to physical limitations.  I do feel I was disadvantaged at a recent interview due to this as there was an unseen presentation which I could not fully complete due to my protected characteristics.  I believe that the service line in which I work has struggled as it has only recently had quality nursing posts added to SLT and an addition of an AHP lead would contribute to the team immensely.  Although the addition of Microsoft teams meetings have been a gamechanger and improved communication it also means that sometimes meetings can be back to back throughout the day with little chance to catch up on actions adding to workload pressures.  Throughout the division there seems to be some evidence that people are promoted into senior positions that do not have the necessary experience or knowledge for the roles they have taken on, this has led to a lack of robust leadership and decision making that impacts on teams.  Although I do enjoy my job and will always give 100% I also feel that the last 12 months have been difficult with sickness and vacancies impacting on my abilities to fulfil my role to the best of my abilities as I have been picking up work for absent colleagues.  I also feel that my contribution to the service line was not understood or acknowledged and as a result I feel there is nowhere further that I can progress my career and if anything has had a severe impact on my confidence.  I want to acknowledge that my colleagues in my team and the clinical teams are amazing and work tirelessly to ensure that the patients get the best experience from them and they flex on a regular basis if new pathways are introduced or piloted to ensure that patient can receive the right treatment in their own home.

I feel that there is no value placed on the administrative colleagues that support the teams I work in.  We have been subject to several admin reviews which have reduced staffing levels and increased workload.  A rethink to how the admin roles are banded is required.  Several colleagues are now working for minimum wage whilst performing duties that are complex in nature, others higher than a Band 2 are being expected to cover the workload of two people due to the reduction of staff following these admin reviews.  Whilst I appreciate there is not an infinite budget, I feel that a fair pay structure for the job a person does is important to ensure we feel valued.

I feel the NHS management culture is to listen with closed ears.  It is all lip service with no action following any discussions.  I quite often give suggestions on how to improve the service, this is always received with a closed book but then later on (maybe a few months later), my suggestion is put in force.  It is as if they don't like non-management making suggestions.  There is definitely a pecking order culture, even though in my former employment I was had a high profile managerial position!
I feel undervalued and by the organisation (not my immediate manager).  Staff are not always listened too.  Sometimes staff are given lip service.

I feel very disillusioned with the Trust at present, it feels too big with not enough corporate support to support services.  Estates issues are not always addressed in a timely manner.  HR support has improved since NWB joined although you can wait for days for advice which makes it difficult to manage staff or situations.  Policies do not match with some of the new systems we are using, too many systems in the management of staff.  Lack of communication from Commissioner's and contract updates are non existent and we are expected to deliver the same services on a contract that was awarded 10 years ago which is impossible within the current budget and the demand for services has increased dramatically with the patients becoming increasingly complex.
I feel very supported by my team and organisation, I feel this is due to the culture of my team and good management.
I find it difficult to identify protected time for improving my skills in the role, predominantly due to the limited team resources available that could free-up some time.

I find this a great organisation to work for with staff who treat each other respectfully.  The team I work in all follow the trust values and I feel the work we do is really valued in the wider system.
I have a good working relationship with my line manager.
I have a long term condition.
I have loved working with Merseycare so far.  Great organisation.

I have never felt so undervalued as a team member.  Other staff are able to work from home occasionally admin staff are not.  There is a 2 tier system within this organisation.  The lower the grade the less you are valued.

I have only been with NHS for nearly [timeframe removed] it's an incredible organisation to work for I love my job it is so rewarding Mersey care truly believe everyone matters and everyone counts that's from patients, staff all round and it's true.
I have really enjoyed working in Mersey Care, and all the support from my team and the trust has been amazing, and has allowed me to thrive within my role.

I have recently moved from another organisation and I can hands down say the commitment of this organisation to becoming anti-racist and creating a culture of openness and learning is exceptional.  I have been supported in achieving work life balance for the first time in my career in this job, and I feel I am trusted to perform in my role as well as balance my commitments with my young family.  This organisation has been good for my mental health and physical health and I am so relieved that the toxic culture that exists in some other NHS organisations doesn't feel present at Mersey Care.

I have scored my survey based on our current working dynamics.  We currently have half the team on long term sickness, my scores/selections reflect my current working environment with the current team members who are endlessly supportive.  This year has been particularly difficult for our team but we have got through it with the continued support of each other and our line manager.  We recently witnessed/experienced verbal aggression from a senior manager, escalated it as necessary, however have heard nothing since despite being told an apology would be issued.  This has been noted within my completion of this survey.
I have started a new role in the past week where the culture is much more positive than the last team I was a part of, this survey has been difficult to complete as a result.

I have thoroughly enjoyed working for The Life Rooms.  Staff and service users have been friendly and respectful, and the environment is welcoming.  My line manager has been extremely supportive and understanding of my individual needs, thy have made reasonable adjustments, including flexible working as needed.  I hope to continue working for this service.  The role could be improved by offering more secure job contracts (e.g. permanent or longer term contracts) but otherwise, I have no other suggestions for improvement.

I have worked for NHS for many years, but several years ago I was down banded which caused me great anxiety and stress leaving me feeling undervalued.  I did contemplate leaving the NHS but as I was of a certain age and enjoyed my role within the service I decided to stay until retirement.

I have worked for the NHS for many years.  I have had various roles over the years but now I work as a Receptionist for Knowsley Clinical Admin Team.  I have never seen so many staff who are so unhappy, feeling so unappreciated and have no flexibility or say towards their rota and ways of working.  Staff are constantly giving ideas to management to make our team run more efficiently and effectively for the service needs and for staff to have a healthy work/life balance but unfortunately management just say "No".  No explanation given, just "NO".  Management style is micromanagement, Bullying people, shouting at staff.  I have never seen this before or worked in these conditions before.  I look forward to retirement.

I have worked in Mersey Care since [number removed] without any problems.  I feel bullied and unsupported in my role by my line manager.  I have been successful in gaining employment in a different role and area of Mersey Care which I am starting shortly and feel will be happy again in this organisation.

I have, in the main, enjoyed working for the NHS.  I do think that more should be done within the organisation to deal properly with any members of staff who either cause others stress, bully people, speak in a disrespectful way, or don't pull their weight.  These people are in the minority, but seem to carry on regardless.
I hope this is anonymous.
I like this organisation.  [timeframe removed] in.  I feel great.  Thank you.
I love Mersey Care!

I love my job and enjoy the variety of tasks I undertake.  The most important aspect and positive impact for me has been working from home for most of my week; not having to commute on public transport (I don't drive) has freed up so much of my leisure time.  The only time I feel under stress is when my colleague is on annual leave and I'm having to try to keep on top of the workload on my own.

I love my job in terms of managing and supporting my staff members.  They are my priority and I have a responsibility to ensure they are happy, psychologically safe and have the suitable resources for their individual needs to assist them in completing their duties.

I now work for a great team with great managers, but the last team I worked for had one of the worst managers I have come across in many years of NHS service, I don't know how they are still employed by Merseycare, its a Joke.  So glad I changed role.

I really enjoy my job, but lately, it feels like most of my day is getting eaten up by red tape and processes.  It's a bit frustrating, to be honest.  Another thing I've noticed is that far too many people are still working from home (lack of engagement) and their absence is really noticeable around the office.  What's puzzling is that these folks still demand desk space within the organisation but never actually use it.  On the flip side, there are some people who are on-site but seem to have multiple desks.  For someone like me, it's becoming quite difficult to find a workspace.  Just thought I'd share my thoughts and see if there's anything we can do to make things smoother for everyone.
I really enjoy my job.

I strongly feel the option to work from home should be honoured and staff are not penalised for their choice.  It is incredibly frustrating traveling for over [timeframes removed] to attend meetings and the recipient does not turn up, you are then working alone in a cold room, continuing your regular work which you could have easily done at home and in result you are costing expenses, travel time, lost work time.

I think it is important to get the balance right from working from home and working in the office, I think it is beneficial to have 2/3 days in the office to build relationships and also getting the chance to talk face-to-face and not rely on emails to communicate.

I think it would be useful to capture if we are clinical or non clinical at the top of the survey and have more options for N/A.  The team question can be a bit hard to answer as its unclear if you are asking about the dept or the individual team.
I think Mersey Care is a great NHS trust to work for, in particular I have a very supportive manager and really appreciate the hard work that she puts in for her team.

I think some of the senior management in Mersey care are happy to sweep things under the rug.  I feel like my immediate manager supports me as much as they can but I feel like those in positions above them are not supportive.  Even when we've had challenging times and begged for help they've treated us like we don't matter.  It sometimes makes it hard to respect them.

I think the organisation is a great place to work.  However I think the lack of communication between concurrent running projects leads to duplication of work, or projects being worked on being irrelevant.  Regarding patient care I do think it is of high priority, but not highest, I feel like seeing patients within KPI is the number one priority.  Yes this absolutely is key to ensuring timely and efficient care across large cohorts of patients, but it would be disingenuous to say that the quality of care or staff wellbeing was the number one priority.
I think the staffing levels and the staff turn over needs a serious looking at.

I think there is more opportunity for flexible working for administrative staff.  Majority of other organisations allow this but it is not widely accepted in ours.  Some managers will approve this and understand, while others don't.  I have children at home and current caring responsibilities for [family member removed], I could easily work from home and provide same standard of work, (if not more work because you don't have office interruptions) as I do in office rather than take the day off for carers leave or unpaid if I had an emergency where I couldn't come into office.  Nearly all of the nurses I work with work from home on a regular basis and some of them have small children etc too and it is never questioned and they provide clinical, patient facing work.  I think this needs reviewing to truly make flexible working more accessible.
I wish to take MARS due to my age and my health is not that good.

I work for a wonderful team and organisation.  I feel super proud of the work we do..  I'd like to be honest about another team that I worked for briefly.  But, it's water under the bridge now and I would not like to revisit my time there.

I work for managers and have a different line manager so answered questions based on who it was directed to.  Also what lets the role down is the PC as there are numerous faults such as the printer driver going missing when you have been printing out all day.

I work in a central function, this Trust writes too many reports, this is a waste of managers time, the point should be about acting on the data not, just pulling and waiting multiple reports that show the same things leaving less time to do something about the what the data shows.
I would just like to add that some grandparents have caring / helping responsibilities also.

I would just like to say how disappointed I am with my higher admin management who are completely unsupportive.  I just feel really worthless and like I am just a number.  Colleagues in the team are leaving because of it so we are losing highly skilled brilliant staff who have been here a long time.  My immediate line manager is one of the best I have ever had and just the most lovely and caring person.  I just wish the 2 higher managers could be as amazing as her.

I would like to see the AFC review the Band 2 role of the DN Clerk/Admin within the Trust as this is role is paying £11.44 per hour which is the same as the minimum wage, this banding does not reflect the role in which as admin/ clerks work within the Trust and should be reviewed by both the Unions and the Trust Management.
I would like to work from home at all times.  I would also like to do long days in order to get work life balance for myself and because of my disability.  If I could afford to I would retire.

I would really appreciate the opportunity to do flexible working as I am being actively treated for breast cancer and I have sever osteoarthritis.  I have submitted a flexible working request and was told 'no' as the needs of the service come first.  Myself and 4 of my colleagues worked 8am to 6pm over 3 days which suited us all perfectly.  That was from the start of COVID right up to the 2024.  Now we have had our days of work and times of work changed by the manager as they say it is the needs of the service again but we are no better off and it makes for a very unhappy office.  I am not allowed flexible working, even taking into consideration my active treatment for my health condition.  However, there is a member of the team who is allowed flexible working.  Some days I am on my knees with fatigue, stress and the ongoing fight for flexible working.

I'm currently off on the sick with work related stress.  I thought I could speak freely with my manager and SLT about how their constant unrealistic demands and being told to reply to emails within 20mins, adding all of my tasks onto outlook calendar and having to justify my work on an hourly basis was causing my so much stress as I am neurodiverse and this is documented in my HR27 along with other conditions.  Since making them aware of this they continued to give me more unrealistic demands and constantly questioned my work and whereabouts whilst working alone.  I explained several times hoping they would understand and was told that this will never change.  I felt that I was doing my job fine but the level of security was horrendous.  They referred me to OH instead for feeling stressed, and they answered one of questions on the referral form saying is this job for me.  This made me feel so undervalued and really knocked my confidence, I am more than capable and have experience and qualifications but was never allowed to showcase my true potential within my role.  I feel so stressed and believe since I spoke up for myself that I am being pushed out.  I was given an action plan with no explanation, when asked they told me oh I thought it would help you since you have been on annual leave.  I can manage my own time but this has been taken away from me.  I made them aware that all of the above is micromanagement.   Since I have been away from that awful environment that I'm actually slowly starting to feel better and I made a decision to resign from my position.  I really love my job but with everything that has happened to me I can't go back as nothing with ever change and my health is more important.  Also at work I was subject to sexual assault, after the incident happened and when the police asked me if I wanted to make a statement, I was made to feel like I couldn't due to policies and procedures with Merseycare NHS.  Although I did make the choice to make a statement.  Also I hadn't received a return to work discussion and was told by my manager that we had, they lied and this made me feel like the sexual assault wasn't anything.  To me this was really a traumatic incident that happened to me and another member of staff and others that witnessed it.  No support and lack of empathy given.  The incident was played down and made out to other staff that it was nothing.  I am very much saddened that all of the above has made me feel that I cannot continue to remain there.  Staff are leaving on a monthly basis, alone there will be 5 staff leaving come the end of October.  This is ridiculous.  The service we provide is very much needed within the community.
I'm is a lovely place to work and has a really supportive culture.

I've had experience of working here and also as a patient receiving.  Overall for the most part as an employee it is quiet good and run smoothly.  However there are definitely improvements that need to be made in terms of supporting your employees.  Last year work kindly paid for a [condition removed] and it got confirmed that I do [condition removed], following this I was made aware by my manager I can apply for access to work to help with reasonable adjustments that have been recommended by the DWP.  [number removed] months later have passed and I have only received [details removed] recommended in my report which is disappointing.  The [number removed] that I have now only just received was delayed because IT were refusing to install it because of security measures or something from what I have been told despite my manager being told that she can go ahead and order the programmes.  Anyway the issue was resolved after a few months but I am still without some recommendations and I am fairly new into my diagnosis so I struggle to explain to people how my [condition removed] affects me and believe that I should at least be given a chance to try and access the recommendations suggested.  My manager is supportive and has been throughout the whole process but I just cant believe things take this long for certain decisions to be made.  I actually feel sorry for any future employees who go onto complete the process.  IT sayings there's things in place for [condition removed] like what tell me give me links to things be clear with instructions as advice.  My experience as a patient within Talking therapies has also been a journey.  Having waited over a year to access the therapy again I was told I was receiving my therapy through an extended service who now has a contract with the NHS to deliver therapy's to Mersey Care patients.  I was told only [number removed] sessions to which I couldn't believe (having previously had therapy from talk for around 3 months) you only build a relationship with the therapist after [number removed] weeks never mind getting [number removed] weeks of sessions in all honestly how can that be effective for anyone trying to help themselves learn new skills and coping mechanisms to help them cope with their mental health.  After my feedback I give I was then offered the full [number removed] sessions that I was told I should have.  I think an overview of a how the trust helps and supports the patients in talking therapies could do with a review and how certain triage decisions are made.  Its clear from my experience as a patient how much statistics are taken into account rather than patient care ( with the questionnaires that are sent out before each appointment) and to be fair also in my role I notice how the statistics are used.  It only makes patients feel like they are a number and that their care is not at the forefront and aim of the care they should be receiving.  There is so much good with how Mersey Care is ran in terms of being an employee and the benefits and security you can feel from it (e.g. sick pay, reasonable time for appts and flexible working) but there is also so much more they could do to help both employees and patients.

I've wanted to work for the Trust for a long time- and was delighted to get a job here.  The hospital is modern and a good environment to work in.  Occasionally I feel uncertain of the security of my role- or where I may be moved to.  And sometimes feel that there's an unnecessary feeling of competitive behaviour amongst.  Staff in my immediate team.
In relation to leadership, our direct team lead is very good the issues we encounter are above.

In the main office we had a jigsaw that was on out of sight and on a unused worktop.  The jigsaw was done by all members of staff when they had breaks in their heavy and demanding workloads and would often dip into the jigsaw and gave us all a very small gap and relieved a bit of stress to carry on with the day.  After a recent inspection it was ordered to be removed as a member of staff was supposedly sitting on a chair doing the jigsaw and looked unprofessional.  I can categorically deny that anyone was sitting there as the worktop is too high and no one has that much time!  Considering this a mental health trust it really doesn't ask how staff are and what can help throughout the day.

Initially following the merge in 2021 there was a lot of confusion regarding data systems.  Which systems to use?  How to access them?  And often there was 2 versions of everything with conflicting guidance.  There has been a gradual 'coming together' since then.  And certainly in the last 12 months it feels more and more like we are one organisation where Corporate teams are supporting one another.

Issues within the organisation come from poor leadership, management and communication from SLT.  SLT lack experience and people skills, they do not embrace Mersey Care values and have created a poor culture with lack of support to frontline staff through not understanding roles or ongoing challenges and risks.  Frontline staff are treated differently and don't feel valued.  Service provision and service user wellbeing is regarded more important than staff wellbeing, though managers who are not in SLT are very good and supportive but don't get the recognition they deserve.

It is mostly a good place to work, however due to the service being so busy, it leaves little time or support for any development/support/mentor opportunities to secure a higher position, which makes me want to move on and apply for other positions.

It was difficult to answer the personal development questions(PDR) as I think it is useful in nursing roles, but I am IT and I don't think it is very useful or ever will be in any form.  These surveys always ask about immediate manager but managers above that and structure of depts.
It would be helpful if hybrid working arrangements could once again be made available to staff as was the case during COVID.

Its about time NHS organisations where held accountable to the staff survey results.  A lot of the time the data is manipulated to a positive bias and there's never information about changes that need to be made that gets back to staff members.
Just and learning culture is very positive within the organisation and widely recognised.  Very sad to see the number of senior leaders leaving Mersey Care, worried what might change.

Just to expand on the section around pay etc, I have recently learnt there are same band roles in other teams that do not/may not have the same level of responsibility I have, this I feel is inequitable for my self and the admin team I am Team Leader for.  I would also like to elaborate that the admin team I manage are the same banding as other admin teams again with less responsibility and I don't believe the pay scales are accurate or fair and should be reviewed across all services.
Love my job, keeps me going and gives me purpose, love the team and would be lost without them.

Mersey Care is not a very nice or good company to work in.  I am not even sure its genuinely NHS or a private company who has paid the NHS for a contract to run a service.  I've come to understand Its very good at paying lip service to looking after people and making out it cares but its actions are very different.  You would have to work in it to truly understand what I mean and go through all the subtle and not so subtle behaviours of higher management and other colleagues and how you get treated.  We've been moved around so much and how now we've been all lumped together different departments all squashed in a small space the noise can be unbearable and make it impossible to concentrate on what you are doing.  The things you hear, the complaints the stress the anxiety the axe to grind the all too personal conversations the tone and upset in peoples voices not having a moments peace without someone talking no matter where you go - the building we are in is not fit for purpose and is very unpleasant to work in.  There's no safe space and there's is nowhere to go for a private conversation without risk being overheard there aren't enough rooms for private meetings and booking one has become are real trial.  I would like to see the person who decided that this was a good idea come and work where we do and suffer it day in and day out.  We must have 50 - 100 people walk past our desks every day, its sounds as if they are walking on a wooden floor an irritating creaking noise all the time.  Just think about that for a moment, every single day the noise of people walking up and down past where you work, conversations going on all around you complaining, hearing the sound of their voices the tone the harshness in the voice and then having to do your job at the same time dealing with patients how they can be complaining, upset.  And I think that's it - decisions being made and it not thought out how it will effect people - stick them all together save money on rent of all other buildings let them suffer and then have to leave and we will just get someone else.  This is only going to lead to low morale stress people going off sick - leading to poor patient service and complaints, I was told that higher management gave the okay for another department to use another departments desks if they needed to without asking that department!  The first they new about it was when it started happening - that could of lead to a row the consequences could of been ever lasting and the person who said it was okay would never of suffered for it other people would of.

Mersey Care needs to sort out who can work from home and who cant!  Some office managers are working from home but don't allow their office staff to work from home.  Some departments can work from home and yet other staff, who could easily benefit from working from home aren't allowed too.  Its unfair and causing a lot of disharmony with your staff.  You have a YouTube video out promoting flexible working and a manager has outright said that they work from home but yet your 'managers' decline these requests from staff.

More opportunities for staff to retire and return to the same job rather than having to work elsewhere.  Also all staff should have the opportunity to do this regardless of whether their contract is full time or not.

My belief is the the NHS still offers unappealing remuneration for the volume and complexity of the service users I support - when compared against jobs in for example, local councils, they come up a poor second.  Terms and conditions for example, annual leave, sickness pay and pension benefits in the local councils are very similar to the NHS offer and yet they offer their posts at around a grade higher than those offered by the NHS - and often with a great deal less responsibility.  One of the biggest drawbacks to working in the NHS for me is that it refuses to allow hybrid working.  So that even if I am just contacting service users by phone or having an admin day, I am still expected to present to an office - when all this could be achieved just as easily in my home space - without the stress and cost of an hour`s commute each way to and from my home.  Many council jobs and civil service jobs as well as other sectors offer hybrid working as a matter of course.
