The NHS however, is still stuck in a very 20th Century working model - which obstinately refuses to see the value to the service as well as employees of hybrid working as well as stubbornly refusing to accept that in lots of cases the revolution in information technology makes it as easy as just logging on and hitting a button!

My experience of working in the organisation has been fabulous.  Career and professional development has been encouraged supported and acted upon.  As an employee I find them perfectly fine.  As for providing a service that's another story all together.  We promote ourselves as an organisation operating in a Just and Learning Culture.  As a trained restorative clinician I fully understand the practice however I believe more than half in fact I would estimate 80 - 90% of staff have no idea of what a Just and Learning Culture is.

My HR27 is frequently not adhered to, I am told if I have annual leave I forfeit my working from home day (although this day is due to my disability). My work pace does not make reasonable adjustments re my disability, I am advised if I have dizziness episodes then I have to go off sick, instead of being able to work from home, as it is part of my disease.  I do not feel valued by my management team, despite the fact I can be left alone every week currently due to short staffing, on the day they suggested I work from home.  I waited months for a written HR27 to be put in place.  Our management are obstructive and unapproachable.  They denied us the right to go to panel to put our case for a band 3, so a grievance had to go in, which we won, but now they are dragging their heels about us going to panel.  This has been going on for years.

My immediate manager is fantastic [name removed] takes initiative to ensure the team they are working with are well both physically and mentally, using there time effectively to ensure we are all able to speak to them when we need, ensuring we are all okay and feel valued within the team.  [name removed] is the manager we all go to and the reason many SPOA staff are still working with the team.

My line manager is wonderful but their hands are tied by management with any issues we have.  Long term absences are not covered by Bank or Agency staff, even during annual leave!  My job has been made even harder during and after COVID with staff working from home, it added extra work because they were not in the office to deal with it themselves.  It is harder to contact staff via phone or email.  It can take several calls to different member's of staff in one organisation to make contact, if at all.  And it is the same with emails.  There was a proposal recently for staff to return to the office, but it is at managers discretion, so not much has changed.
My onboarding was very good, the opportunities are good, my manager is great, I am listened to and valued.

My only concern is that there is an alarming lack of respect between colleagues of the wider organisation (Team - Team basis) despite the fact that respect and civility is promoted everywhere in the trust.  There is also a prolific blame culture which really needs to be addressed, and the Trust really struggles with Pay Band related Ego and this is further reflected in how out of team/department colleagues treat each other.

My only worry at the moment is there is a chance our service might be coming to an end in [date removed], this is a big worry for me and adds stress to my life as I worry what the future may hold.  I also am a carer so work some days from home, I worry if I need to find a new job going forward I might not have the option to work from home and I don't know how I would be able to manage this.

My primary concern is that there doesn't seem to be any kind of forward planning to address issues that are or are likely to occur.  There is a tendency to let situations develop and deal with the fallout rather than having potential solutions in place.  Recruitment seems to take an interminable amount of time to resolve.  We have a post that was vacated 17 months ago and still has not been filled with a permanent appointment.

My Role is quite Unique to my Organisation however recently I feel that I've had to cut my hours as I feel that my Role is not wanted or needed any longer which has made me feel sad.  I would like to continue to work in the organisation but feel that due to my [condition removed] I cause problems because it feels like the few reasonable adjustments I need are to difficult to be made in the building I work in.  I have continued to try my best and make suggestions so I can continue to do the job I was so proud to get, however I feel that things way out of my control will not allow for this to happen. [name removed].

Nepotism is a culture here.  As is poor behaviour - bullying / micromanagement and obsession with assurance to tick meaningless boxes of nonsense - real issues are not dealt with.  We state we support flexible working - yet have very senior managers openly stating 'it is frowned upon here' and life is made as hard as possible for anyone who has a flexible arrangement.  I rarely get any supervision and have not had a PACE review for over 4 years.  My manager is too bust trying to please his own manager - and the limited time we every gt is wasted by him looking at his watch and phone continually.  This is not helpful or supportive.
NHS should focus on giving opportunity to individuals that are good, want to develop, and will make NHS and the patient happy.  Not focusing solely on the DEI project.
No employee recognition schemes with rewards / benefits.
Not enough staff to meet requirements.
Obviously its a survey for healthcare workers - I felt I needed more - "does not apply boxes" for my answers.
On the question about me leaving, it is not because of the organisation.  I have had the opportunity to travel to [location removed].
Organisation needs to look inwardly to assess what it has to be able to then work to replace or work to fix that which doesn't fit what it should represent to the public - top heavy structures.

Our organisation has a profile that promotes an excellent service although a large number of staff do not feel confident in the management structures of which there are too many again.  These managerial jobs every few years creep back into the NHS and take a huge resource of the finances but there is no effect they play a significant role to the care of patients or staff.
Our pay is crap.
Our team has been undergoing re- structure for a number of years.  It is now causing low morale and I believe it is cruel to keep people in suspense without clear objectives or job descriptions.
Overall, a great place to work.  The pay scales not so good.
People with disabilities are not treated reasonably or equitably and the Trust is enrolled as a Disability Friendly Trust.
Please allow flexible working e.g. work from home.

Please encourage people to stop gatekeeping their roles by not providing full information to fellow employees who they directly work with.  Please improve communication, people making changes to rosters etc, managers, and not informing people can be very detrimental.

Please stop introducing countless different systems to perform what one properly commissioned system should be able to do, for example, Allocate Optima (health roster), Empactis, Case Manager, ESR, Workforce portal, Employee Online, Expenses.  Life's busy and stressful enough without having to learn and remember all of these - which are only the HR-type systems!
Poor direction, no scope for development, no clear prospects, never had a formal chat/PACE etc with a manager about my options.
RJLC - is a farce.
RJLC is a travesty.

Sickness rules are not the same for everyone, lateness is not treated the same for everyone, some people make time up some people come and go as they please.  Holiday entitlement is not treated fairly, some staff work from home in emergency and some staff won't as they refuse to even though they have equipment at home.
Sort the pay structure the same as other NHS trusts.

Speaking from experience, I think when you are supporting an other ward, when that person is on long term sick, I think staff should be given an incentive.  Not quite sure how this would work, but I'm sure this could be facilitated.

Staff are so concerned about sickness stages, that they are working through the sickness or coming in and spreading it throughout the team.  Sickness stages need to be addressed, it is causing a lot of unnecessary anxiety and stress for staff.
Systems are not always clear to follow or take too long to action.
The amount of staff being on long term sick, particularly managers, is worrying.  Far too many people use sickness as an excuse to not do their jobs whilst others have to pick up the slack.
The boxes need to be changed into yes no boxes as will make answering some of the questions a lot better.

The culture within my organisation / service is negative and toxic.  Communication from senior staff members to the workforce is passive aggressive and combatable.  Staff sickness and staff turnover is a real concern for me as the service has extremely high levels of sickness due to work related stress.  This points to a negative culture that does not behave in line with Merseycare Values.  Senior Leadership team lack skills, knowledge and experience in management and effective communication.  Our service has no leadership or direction from the "SLT" instead the "SLT" are usually working from home or offsite.  Staff members used to play table tennis during our unpaid lunch break, however "SLT" removed the table tennis without communicating with staff.  This has damaged the relationship between the workforce and the "SLT".  It feels like we are constantly fire fighting with the diverse needs within our local communities that accesses our services as staff are not listened too and not provided opportunities to upskill themselves.  It appears to get promotions within our organisation you need to be friends with people in our "SLT".  This has had a profound impact on the organisation achieving its goal, actually I am not aware of any goals/objectives this year for my organisation.  During my two years working for this service it has been not a career that I am passionate about it has been more of just a job.

The HR department in our organization is not doing enough to address serious concerns regarding staff members who continually put patient care at risk.  While we are often reminded to follow existing policies, this approach fails to tackle the root issues at hand.  The HR team's excessive risk aversion limits their ability to effectively manage these critical situations, leaving employees frustrated and patients vulnerable.  It's essential that HR takes a more proactive stance to ensure a safe and supportive environment for both staff and patients.

The last 12 months have been challenging to deliver services the last government disinvested in the NHS or spent money incorrectly and we are now suffering the after effects of many years of depravation across cities communities and the NHS.
The NHS really needs to concentrate on what is should be doing - caring for patients - instead of being distracted by politics and social engineering.  And always remember - first do not harm.

The operating context, organisation, it's leadership and its culture has changed significantly in recent years.  The aims and the work to support staff and culture is extensive and needs to be prioritised and resourced.  This is an extremely complex and hands on agenda and the expertise and capacity is extremely limited whilst the risk, targets and implications are significant.

The organisation genuinely does try to be an exceptional place for staff to work and patients to receive care.  It is challenging to consistently translate this to practice in such a large organisation.  There are pockets of poor culture in key areas that can prevent/slow down progress and this is the area that I personally feel requires attention to truly achieve the strategic aspirations.

The organisation I work for does not value its admin staff.  There are few opportunities to progress.  Roles are longer retained, if someone leaves, their workload is disseminated between others.  The NHS banding system is archaic and a throw back to another era.  Generally everyone is seen as and feels like they are expendable.  Platitudes do not help pay the bills and being told of my importance to the running of my organisation becomes meaningless.  I'm really good at my job and have knowledge others don't.  This isn't reflected in how I am treated or paid.  I always feel like there is a hidden agenda from my managers.  I don't feel secure in my work and expect to be worked harder in the coming months.
The organisation is supposed to be a just and learning culture, but in the office where I work the line managers and service managers 100% do not follow this.

The organisation needs to identify and offer in-house training and a clear career progression for it's admin staff.  The general feeling amongst my colleagues is that although individual clinical staff do value our role and the support we provide to enable THEM to be more effective, the organisation needs to focus more on the development of quality admin staff to attract more young people into the role.  The cohort is currently split between older staff who are very experienced but close to retirement age and young staff who have fallen into the role without quality foundational training and procedural rigour prior to recruitment.  A "grow your own" approach with admin staff would greatly benefit the organisation and the wider NHS.

The organisation provides no support despite claiming to.  The only support I would rely on and feel protected by is the Union, which I have joined as I have no faith in being treated fairly by management and some colleagues.

The organisations and the NHS in general need to review the roles of those who are working in band 2 and band 3 positions.  The band 3 role I am currently in I'm doing work that should be banded higher and its unfair to be doing this work just scraping about the national minimum wage.  The minimum wage has rose greatly in the past few years year out wage has lagged behind meaning even with the 5.5% rise we are not much better off and expected to do hands on and technical work above our banding and not compensated.

The patients run the wards and there not a thing staff can do or say to stop it.  Doctors are under immense stress for zero suicide and patients know if they say they are feeling depressed and suicidal there is less chance they will be discharged.  Again seemingly playing the system.
The rate of pay does no reflect the job and what we do compared to others in the NHS in different boroughs.

The senior leadership teams need to better role model flexible working as it still seems to be frowned upon.  Senior leaders need to reduce the amount of time spent at V7 and get out into services on a weekly basis.
The team I work in is particularly supportive.  I think there is far more room to work flexibly in non clinical roles than clinical.

The team work with and the team I am part of are two different groups.  The team I work with are supportive, respectful and proficient.  The team I am part of are the total opposite and are the reason I want to leave the organisation as soon as possible to the point I would accept a low band than I am currently on.

The trust do not take Health and Wellbeing of their staff seriously.  Service need always comes before individual staff need, which I completely understand, but they need to stop pretending like that isn't the case.  Health and wellbeing is always a last priority.  Managers aren't trained properly and don't know how to support staff.

The Trust I work for is more interested in being a corporate entity than a trust it has all these policies in place that actually look good but do not work.  Community based teams are completely out of the loop PACE is a joke it has nothing to do with you its a tick box exercise.

The Trust is ambitious and well intentioned but sometimes over promises or over expects with the limited resources available.  Greater work could take place in the inclusion space - Inclusion should not leave others feeling excluded or cause further hurt.  And a greater focus on capturing learning and good practice - celebrating the achievements of all of our services.  We're so big now, maybe we could do a spotlight on different teams in the weekly communications to get to know what a team does and their achievements, one from each division?
The trust is too big is more concerned about how big it is rather than quality of service.
The Trust needs to invest into a new Trust reporting system.  The current (only been in place since April 2023) system is cumbersome, time consuming and not fit for reporting purposes.
The trust response to the aftermath of recent riots in Southport was caring and reassuring.  I am proud to work for a an organisation that is working hard to improve racism and encourage inclusivity.

There are too many layers of management.  There is an encouraged culture of bullying from managers.  I have had my hours cut by the management.  I feel so poor working for Mersey Care.  I use all my annual leave for medical appointments and sometimes my manager will not let me attend my medical appointments due to "service needs".  I have to request that my manager will let me go to the toilet, which is humiliating.  I had severe [condition removed] and requested to go home, I was told "get your [obscenity's removed] back to work".  The toxic environment between staff and management is insufferable.  Management just constantly boast about exotic holidays, what fancy car they are getting, while the staff at the bottom are lucky to have food and heating.  Mersey Care policies are only there to protect the Managers and make life awful for other staff.  Working for Mersey Care has had a negative impact on my physical and mental health.

There has been a poor approach to planned changes to Exec Directors.  Changes were announced, but these new roles have not been recruited for and no update on reasons for this has been provided.  This is causing difficulties in the effective completion of my role, as there are conflicts/tensions between affected managers and departments.  The existing "Your Brief" is ineffective.  The sessions are only 30 minutes in duration so updates are rushed and there isn't time for questions.

There is a complete lack of attention to efficiency.  It is common to hear "that's how we've always done it" with complete lack of interest in becoming more efficient and saving money.  Take the Agenda for Change process for example.  I estimate it has cost the NHS about £2000 for me to complete an AfC process.  There are much more efficient ways to deliver the same outcome and ultimately save money via efficient processes.  That is just one example, not specifically the AfC process.  However, this does need an overhaul like the MOD undertook recently to enable technology to help and assist with automation and ease of completing documents/submitting.

There is a continued decline in the functionality of Corporate functions.  More and more work is passed to clinical divisions by TWSS, I highlighted this on last years survey alongside many others and this was not evident in the results.  There is a drop in skills and knowledge within teams within TWSS and a reluctance to assist even when asked to.  The roll out of RADAR is a good example with Clinical Divisions ignored and not included in the consultation.  When ideas/requests for changes were made these were ignored by the Project Lead and Divisional reps were made to feel stupid and unable to make themselves heard.

There is a lot of ableism and discrimination happening across the Trust.  The time taken to invest in collecting awards as a Disability Confident Employer is pointless if we cannot get the basics right in looking after our staff.  As a large employer we need to remember 'patient centred care' this also applies to our staff who will also be our patients.  We seems to be giving advice and care to people as a patient and then ignoring it as an employer!  There needs to be 360 review held for managers, which staff complete anonymously as some staff do not have an outlet to seek support and managers are continuing in bad practice.  I would not use FTSU again as in the past this caused more problems.  There needs to be more robust systems in place for policies and ensuring they come through the staff networks as there is no consistency currently.  There is also an ablest practice with policies as the authors do not represent the subject matter.  There needs to be further training with HR on the Equality Act and Employment law for those with protected characteristics and a consistency on advice given as currently this is sporadic.
There is not fairness for everyone in this Trust.  There is still incivility and unfairness based on Race and protected characteristics.

There needs to be a review of band 2 posts in the organisation and the NHS as a whole as prior to the NHS pay increase they were just below the national minimum wage which should not be acceptable for any organisation.  There are a vast array of roles within this banding so for example house keeping/cleaning, reception teams to the other end of the scale teams that are assessing patient needs when referred into services and signposting if needed.  There appears to be a lot of band 2 roles that have a lot put on them compared to others and considering the level.  It would also seem that management are disinterested in reviewing this when highlighted which then leads to a high turnover of staff with no consistency.

There never appears to be any consultation with the people who do the job in a lot of new processes and practices put in place by upper management.  I use a procurement system which used to take me a couple of hours per month to process for ordering and payments, the Oracle system administrated by SBS replaced it.  I now spend 2 or 3 hours per week doing the same thing.  That to me isn't an efficient use of my time, plus the fact suppliers invoices go unpaid for weeks and we have been put on stop which effects the patients.  The IT systems and equipment needs an upgrading also.

Think we should be given the choice of working from home.  Pro are get more work done at home and less time travelling to office.  Sickness levels are down as you can just work from home if you have an off day when you just don't feel well.

This is a huge organisation growing continuously day - day frustrations for me in my role are the lack of training facilities the fight to secure rooms big enough to accommodate staff comfortably, when booking rooms early and advertising to staff the rooms have to be freed up to accommodate the Board of Directors and always last minute leaving no room to deliver training which is mandatory/role specific to this Trust which in turn reflects on the low compliance figures not all training can be delivered via an eLearning package.  Its time to build a Education Centre or when new Builds are in place provide adequate rooms to reduce the continuous battle.  Thank you.
This is a nice team to work for our higher management are supportive of work life balance.

This trust in writing promotes flexible working and Hybrid working, yet in mental health care division administration roles this is not the case and they do not allow this for any post regardless of peoples circumstances and role ability to be remote.  This has been an ongoing problem throughout the division and is frustrating when all other divisions promote this for work/life balance.

This trust would run a lot smoother if they weren't so top heavy.  Too many 8a's and above that don't have a clue what they are doing.  Most of the band 3/4/5's could run rings around them and they wouldn't have a clue, we could literally tie them in knots and they would be oblivious.  Over promoting the wrong people into the wrong jobs, it is pathetic.  These people aren't competent.
Too many processes that are in place to be followed that are not followed.  A top heavy organization with lot of promotions at this level with no process.

Tried to raise concerns about procurement and the lack of accountability from a third party company who are "ripping the NHS off" with how much they are charging for goods (even for items never delivered).  Is this a way to spend tax payers money - "jobs for the boys" in procurement.

We continue to tolerate individuals who are in their position due to their gift of the gab rather than operational or clinical merit, and this is frustrating to watch.  These individuals seem to then get away with recruiting individuals whose faces fit, rather than the most appropriate candidate.  What we tolerate, we promote.  It's a shame because there are some really hard working members of staff who don't get the same recognition.  Also we need to be unafraid to get rid of the coasters - we're not very good at performance managing individuals when they get beyond an 8A - and MARS feels like a reward for them!

We had an admin and clerical review a few years ago resulting in some admin staff choosing to leave, or accept a down banding to a lower grade.  Recruitment to vacant posts has been difficult in my office, leaving the remaining admin team shorthanded for long periods.  There has been longstanding discussion of changes to our duties, e.g. minute taking being done during the meeting by those involved so as to free up admin but this has largely not happened and we are expected to maintain the same or increased workload with fewer staff.  I feel that while admin are valued within their direct teams, they are not treated as a professional group, with their own skills and expertise being valued by the Trust as an organisation.  My direct manager does not know what my role entails, but apparently the Trust has decided I no longer need a medical support secretary as the office is not busy enough.  They have arrived at this decision using a metric / KPI method we no longer support, so I am at a loss to know how this has been achieved.  They may not be able to see a recorded result but I can assure you I am still doing the work.  Without sufficient staff, there is no capacity to cover annual leave or sickness and in addition to the ongoing lack of staff, I have ended up supporting a colleague during their sick leave for [timeframe removed] recently, doing as much as I could of their role as well as my own and ending up with a backlog in both roles as a result.  This has been the proverbial last straw for me so I have decided to retire as soon as possible.  Policy seems to be not to recruit until the post holder has left so there is no opportunity to do an effective handover - another example of the Trust not appreciating admin skills and knowledge.  I feel the Trust is missing out on colleagues with lots to offer the Trust, but many of the admin feel that the Trust regards the role as a necessary evil, that anyone who can type can do, no skills needed.  Recruitment of consultants and locum psychiatrists has also impacted my role.  We have had one post vacant for a substantive clinician since [date removed] and this year alone I have had one locum leave and another only available part time or remote only, which has had an effect on timely appointments.  There is no opportunity for progression in my current role, especially if I wanted to retain contact with service users, I would need to move into a more corporate role which does not interest me..  Finally, there is no scope for home working or flexible working for those of us with family responsibilities such as elderly [family member removed].  My line manager has been as supportive as they can be within the existing framework, but the current policies have also factored into my decision to retire as soon as possible as the Trust is insistent on staff being in the office when with a little flexibility with home working I could maintain effective working and lessen stress in trying to juggle care for an elderly [family member removed] with working responsibilities.  Our staff group has many of us in the same situation with no leeway around this.

When [family member removed] passed away in [details removed], the support I received from my co-workers was amazing but I felt very unsupported by the organisation as there is no bereavement leave.  I had to take this as sick leave and was used to trigger sickness levels.  Then when I had a gastro sickness, I was upped a level.  I find this very unfair and left me feeling undervalued as a member of the organisation.  If I had taken time off for [family member removed], I could understand, but this was [family member removed].  As an only child having to care for [family member removed] and organise a funeral all the time being aware that my absence would count against me.
When multiple parties are copied on email, the sender(s) should be conscious of whether everyone really needs to be copied and especially on courtesy replies or thankyous or tangential replies.

While the Trust genuinely strives to create a cohesive, long-term plan, there appears to be a lack of alignment in certain areas.  It often feels as though different departments pursue their initiatives independently, whether it's the procurement and implementation of new systems, internal projects, or collaborations with external partners.  This lack of cohesion can result in efforts that don't always align with the overarching strategic goals.  To truly create an integrated, effective approach, we could benefit from adopting a more holistic planning process.  This would involve bringing all key stakeholders into the conversation from the outset, ensuring that everyone has a clear understanding of priorities and how their work fits into the larger plan.  Currently, the absence of a unified prioritisation can lead to resource strain, causing individuals and teams to become overstretched as they work to meet disparate demands.  This strain not only increases the risk of errors but also impacts the well-being of our staff, who may experience stress-related health issues when navigating these pressures.  By fostering a more inclusive planning process and prioritising cohesion, we can better position ourselves to deliver exceptional results across the board, maintaining both the quality of our work and the well-being of our teams.
Whilst going through consultation.  I feel that not enough information is being given, causing anxiety and stress.

Whilst the trust is making in roads with regards IT.  Many systems used by various departments often don't communicate with each other such as roster, impacts, etc.  Health systems are fragmented and don't communicate to external systems at times.  Financial constraints are a big issue for staffing and finding the right staff is a challenge.  The quality of buildings is poor in some areas and the means to upgrade/renew is almost non existent.  Private providers are eating up huge amounts of public money for profit in areas of commissioned services, bank staff due to shortages, and undoubtedly repairs and maintenance is extortionate.  Don't feel I've been able to.  Progress as I'd like, found it difficult to understand aspects of job but don't feel I've been supported practically are times to rally progress to a role that I aspired to.  I think HR processes are restrictive for managers and way to to generous at times to staff.  There isn't even a requirement for individuals to show diagnosis of stated health conditions such as ADHD of that they have been referred or being assessed.  Staff can almost say anything they like and we are expected to accept this on face value and then manage the requests sometimes completely unacceptable and proportionate to the requirements of the service.  In other sectors or if it was myself I would be happy to evidence any condition I have in support of myself to gain the proper support from my employer.  These issues really need to be addressed for a better financial footing as well as being able to hire and manage people more effectively and stop allowing people to take advantage.

Working environment is poor, heating can't be controlled, windows not accessible/easy to open and close, roof leaking.  Would feel better if I could buy nutritious (able to get a few of my 5 day )in the canteen, but It's very difficult to, veg and salad portions often only coming with set meals.
Working for the Mersey Care Long COVID Service, we provide a robust and caring MDT approach to patient care.  All staff are equally valued as integral to the service provision.

Working from home is not allowed regardless of reason for request, although this worked well during COVID.  Some areas are still working from home.  Flexible working is not encouraged/allowed.  Different rules for managers who can work from home for any reason i.e. car in for MOT, waiting delivery of an item, tradesperson attending to do work, we would have to take annual leave.  General unfairness between bandings.
Working from home should be available for all staff.  Not enough promotion opportunities.
Would like the sickness policy to be reviewed.

Your specialist clinical services admin teams should be managed by the specialist clinical team leaders as they understand the requirements and complexities that the admin job role entails.  Centralised admin teams do not work as they do not understand the complex requirements that are needed for the specialist services.  Being managed from afar is not the way forward it brings so many problems i.e. sick leave, work relationships, guidance, support, work challenges, organisation of work loads team building the list continues.  We have never been able to attend admin team meetings as we can not leave the job for more than half an hour due to the work demands, this is not right.

Although the RJLC culture is important, I have found there is a challenge in holding people to account when performance or actions falls below the expected standards.  I think there is a fine balance to be struck still between RJLC and accountability.  I have found there is support for staff to prevent managers treating staff unfairly, however there is limited support for managers when the situation in reversed.

As a department it is a fact we are short staffed.  And have a exodus of clinicians this year.  While the rest of us perform at our AFC band it is demoralising to see that clinicians are brought in as band 7 AFC and doing band 5-6 work as it was the only way anyone will apply.  Within the profession we have a bad name of over working staff.  The staff that remain are loyal to each other and that is the main reason they are in work.  To have staff in tears daily on a team meeting and stating its too much, I cant cope, stop adding to my case load is heart breaking.  We have manager who is living in the past and how thing's worked where she used to work.  She needs to be in the moment and face with the issues here and now.  Managers saying they see patients in their unpaid lunch, isn't a solution and should be condoned.  As this is then a sign to lower staff members well if manager doing it I must do it as well.  If we need to see patients during unpaid lunch then logistically something requires addressing as just sticking a band aid over a issue doesn't resolve it.  We now see that advertised are positions higher than my pay grade where experience isn't necessary.  So leave they have just qualified from university.  No NHS experience, patient experiences or computer system skills and they are rewarded with a Band 6, a position unobtainable to me after [number removed] years service.  We have band 7's who just seem to spend time in meetings and admin.  In the past three years we have had managers who I personally feel haven't been equipped to do the job, very nice people but not management material.  I feel we are a department lost at sea, bobbing around on a ocean like a rudderless ship.  As AHP we have a duty to maintain our development with CPD.  As I am employed by the NHS this is a mutual arrangement where this essential training to maintain my professionalism is given by the trust.  Continually meeting after meeting and CPD session after CPD session has been cancelled last minute due to staff shortages.  Again this bring despondency to the team.  We get no down time or time to catch up with each other.  As clinicians we work autonomously so we look forward to these sessions.  Our commitment to patients?  We have patients adults and children in pain with acute conditions,  are we being open and honest and writing and telling them just how long they are going to have to wait to have the treatment they are waiting for?

As a whole I feel that we as a team are feeling generally burnt out and that morale is at an all time low- we are currently severely understaffed, operating on EMS level 3 for a long period now with an ever increasing workload which we are expected to complete within an unrealistic time frame.  This in turn is causing many staff to take long term sickness due to work related stress and this is further exacerbating the problem even with aid from other trusts etc.  This has been ongoing now for several months and does not seem to be getting any better and I am concerned about the further implications of this on the team in general.  I would however like to say a massive thank you to the team managers who are doing an excellent job of showing their support towards us as a team during these difficult times and especially towards myself- I am currently going through a lot of [details removed] issues and management have been incredibly supportive towards me with everything from checking in on me regularly to accommodating appointments which I have needed to attend and ensuring I know that they are only a phone call away if needed- in fact I would like to offer my most recent thanks to my team manager for taking time out of there day off to call me after I had emailed to express my levels of work related stress and how this has been affecting me as I really do feel this was above and beyond the call of duty and I would like this to be recognised higher up.  Thank you!

As I have only been in post for a [timeframe removed] I feel I cant make too many comments on this organisation.  But what I have seen up to now of my team and the organisation so far I cant complain.
Bullying and nepotism is rife.

Bullying culture evident at senior level.  Senior managers not upholding trust values.  Staff are not being valued or recognised for genuine hard work and the expertise and value they bring.  Some staff given unfair advantage due to who they know.

Clinical staff need to have the opportunity to work from home to complete admin tasks as much as the managing staff do.  Also mental health days should be brought in to stop people leaving the role within the first 2 years.

Considering the trust has a reported learning culture, can I suggest that bands 8 and above consider this as these people in the hierarchy need to wake up and pull their fingers out!  The only group of people learning are the clinicians at the sharp end?  Glad to be retiring within the next year as I find Mersey care absolutely embarrassing at board and higher levels - not a clue!

Continually bombarded with emails and information.  I feel that half the job is taking up with satisfying policies and procedures which leaves less time for clinical practice.  This is not always reflected when scrutinizing time allocation between clinical and no-clinical duties.  More time is spent using IT which at times connectivity is an issue and my eyes sight has deteriorated due to the amount of screen time now expected as part of carrying out my job role.

Don't feel valued by the organisation although we are working as a small cog in the bigger picture I feel under valued and don't feel praised for all my work and effort put into my team.  I don't feel supported to develop my career and not had the opportunity to do this.  Reasonable adjustments have been supported by DWP but no one has provided me with an office space to utilise these.  I have to continue to type I am unable to use the dragon software which was part of my reasonable adjustment package.

Due to management restructure and an obsessive focus on KPIs, staff at every grade are feeling more and more pressures on their time.  Band 7's are being forced to spend more time on non-clinical activities pushing pressure further down the line.  Staff morale is low, good staff are leaving due to this.  Staff also leaving due to no career progression opportunities as Band 7 roles which are being vacated are not being replaced.  It doesn't really feel like a nice place to work anymore.

Feel well supported by my line manager and in my immediate team.  However do not feel well supported by higher management that has been reduced so that they cover more teams than previously.  Can see that more is being put onto the Band 7 level of staff adding to their stress and reducing their clinical time with patients.  Band 7 opportunities are being lost as when a band 7 job becomes available it is being down graded to band 6.  This has already resulted in the loss of a fabulous Band 6 clinician that has left the organisation due to being told there will be no opportunity for her to progress.  Feel that the skills, expertise and loyalty of experienced band 6 staff is very much being taken for granted.  We are expected to the bulk of clinical visits but also more being asked of us as more demands made on Band 7 time.

From a reports perspective, i.e. rapid reviews/ radars/ documenting in prep for QRV/ documenting safety huddles etc., I feel there has been a huge increase in this admin type work which takes time and has resulted in reducing clinical time to see patients and support staff.  This needs to be considered in respect of staffing.  There are tasks such as documenting MCA/ BI and completing shared decision tools which take a great deal of time and is not reflected in out staffs timetabling.  Staff are mostly clinical with little admin time to complete such tasks.  This needs to be addressed.

Generally there is not enough resource to meet demand.  There is a very nursing/medical/psychological heavy focus from the organisation and allied health roles are not valued.  There have been recent developments in allied health but not a change of culture and attitudes, respect and value towards AHPs are very poor in some areas.
Haven't had any management 1-1 supervision in Sefton in [timeframe removed] with regard to caseload management or health and wellbeing.
Having my occupation listed as AHP other says it all really about how valued I feel my profession is within this organisation.
Hybrid model of working is life changing and provides a much better balance.  The option for people to decide where they work best and can output the most work is invaluable.
I am due to leave the organisation, I value the time I have worked here and hope to return in the future.

I am experiencing difficulties completing the RADAR system of reporting safety concerns.  The online form isn't fit for purpose.  The trust has become so large that forms are required to cover in patient, community, mental health, and physical health settings which makes forms such as RADAR very difficult to complete.  As a [details removed] I have concerns that errors /concerns aren't being recorded as staff when completing RADAR find the form isn't saved.
I am happy in my job.
I am happy to work in my team, inside the organisation.  Many thanks.
I am happy with in my current role.
I am very grateful to my colleagues, managers and other management of my organisation.  And I love my patients, most/all most all of them are wonderful.
I applied to reduce my hours , I have had to wait for over many months for the organisation to recruit another member of staff to allow me to finally reduce my working week from 4 days to 3.

I do my job, because I love to do it and I enjoy making a difference in people's life.  But the way that the health has been approached is fragmented and inefficient.  I would recommend to offer different opportunities to the patients, where in one place all the needs are sorted while the patient is visiting.  Also, trusts' education programs should promote holistic perspective to diagnose and treat, and when possible to provide with more physical contact and appreciation than given self-care responsibilities, to people that are living lives with isolation and minimal common sense..  Currently, the opportunities to see and talk with a person to receive help, are very limited and humans need humans to find the way throw their difficulties.

I don't feel the organisation listen to our team as a whole.  I feel they hear what they want to hear and don't resolve issues.  For example, since COVID we have been moved base 4 or maybe 5 times.  Number of desks gets smaller each time.  We are now in an office that is out of area to our patients so travel time to base we could have had clinical contact instead with patients.  As a band 5 I feel I would benefit from an office base that everyone feels they can travel to within work hours so that I have support to ask questions rather than over the phone.  Physio is a hands on job and I need hands on guidance not remote.  Also, I feel the office issue has affected our teams health and wellbeing.  We work remotely within community and it is difficult to set boundaries and we often work over our hours and through are lunch breaks.  The team are absolutely amazing and I feel we are not appreciated with the way we are treated, recently another change to our desk space without any consultation.  I feel our operational lead has a difficult job and is not very present because they are too busy with higher management issues.  All this leads to is poor staff retention and lack of job satisfaction and team with low morale and people will end up burning out.
I don't like the pay step in the bandings.  It infers that we haven't learnt a thing in a year and our experience not valued.  We should have our increments yearly for all grades.

I enjoy all aspects of my work apart from unnecessary paperwork which sometimes is felt like it is done for the sake of doing it.  The incident reporting system and processes are simplified and streamlined, it would make clinician's job much more efficient and result in effective service delivery.
I enjoy my job and look forward to coming to work.

I enjoy the clinical aspects of my role and feel this brings me job satisfaction.  This is the reason I am in the role and not looking for alternative employment.  There have been significant issues with staffing which have been poorly handled by senior management in recent years, with people being treated as numbers, feeling unsafe in their workplace and treated unfairly.  This has impacted on staff wellbeing, sickness rates, morale and has placed additional pressures on others to maintain safe and effective patient care.  These efforts go unrecognised and staff are left feeling undervalued.  Staff are often advised that they are 'over reporting' on incidents in attempts to escalate these concerns and any rare interactions with senior management feel like an interrogation process, rather than what should be an effort to understand and support the challenges we face.
I feel as though there is a strong team identity in my Trust.  However, often, it becomes challenging where the NHS is attempting to collaborate with other organisations - e.g.  The city council.

I feel like immediate members of staff in my team (e.g. team leaders and other clinicians) are doing everything we can to support one another.  The work load is getting heavier with more procedures/admin tasks to be done (radars/reporting), chasing things up with other organisations and patients becoming more complex.  We cannot recruit anyone, this is having an affect on us all.  Moods are low and people are fed up at times.

I feel more and more dismayed and disheartened with the state of the trust both for patients and staff.  It is run as a business and not as a caring environment for staff or patients.  The opportunity to progress for staff who are not full time is none existent, despite this being no fault of the person and having both the skills and experience to complete the role leading to progression and fully able to meet the needs of the service.  This also applies to training.  I have continually asked for training and have had absolutely no opportunities outside of eLearning, which I find of very limited benefit in improving my skills and knowledge.  I am not happy in my role, I am not happy working for this trust.  I feel both staff and patients are often 'done to' than 'with' despite the valid concerns and contributions that could be taken on board.  People get promoted because of who they know and not because of their genuine experience and skill.  The lack of adequate treatment for patients is diabolical.  The lack of beds is appalling.  I am entirely frustrated on behalf of myself, colleagues, patients and carers.

I feel that issues could be addressed/resolved if staff members are offered an exit interview, even if they move job internally.  It is difficult for staff living in Liverpool to not work for Mersey care due to the trust size.

I feel that the organisation's size makes joined up working and supporting service user involvement in decision making difficult.  Various parts of the organisation are working in different ways in terms of service user involvement.  In terms of secure services recruitment and retention of OTs needs looking into and supporting as the lack of these professionals has an impact on all aspects of positive discharge experiences.

I feel that there needs to be more equality in the management structures in the trust, there are staff line managing larger teams of 30+ staff while others managers within the same division are managing small teams of less than 10 staff.  In my role for the past 12 months at least 50% of that time I have had no operational manager in place.  This has meant that I have received very infrequent management supervision and support, been a new manager supporting over 30 staff I feel that this is not adequate making the role very difficult.

I feel there should be recognition for length of service.  Whilst immediate managers are supportive and show appreciation of experience, the trust should be able to award something to recognise those who have significant experience at the top of their bands, often performing above their bands due to their experience.  The trust needs to explore further opportunities for clinical specialist posts as currently you can only progress as an AHP past a B7 if you go into management/ non patient facing job role.

I feel upper management is lead too much by KPIs and productivity relating to number of patient visits rather than quality of visits.  This is not a true reflection of the work load we do on a daily basis which is complex, qualitative, and multi-faceted.
I feel valued by my team but not the organisation.
I feel well supported by my team interim manager and also by their ops manager.

I had to raise formal concerns about the service and colleagues clinical work around 12 months ago.  An action plan was written but I never had an outcome to this despite chasing it up.  There are ongoing issues with colleagues talking about each other, this is an issue from clinical leads down and has never in any way been successfully addressed.  I am [condition removed] and have unfortunately had a lot of [condition removed] related sickness.  My experience is there is little support or understanding of how [condition removed] related sickness should be treated or that somebody may not have a straight forward [condition removed] - I have had attempts to trigger maternity leave and told I would at face disciplinary for further [condition removed]  related sickness.  Clear irritation if I haven't been well enough to work and on my return senior staff won't ask how I am but will email about picking up more work etc.  I really feel that management would benefit from some training around how to support pregnant staff and particularly those who are experiencing [condition and details removed].  I had to ask repeatedly for [details removed] risk assessment.  Basic things like return to works (not even a phone discussion to see how I am) and updating my [details removed] risk assessment haven't been done.
I have completed this survey out of work time.
I have recently moved to a different job within the organisation so some questions were difficult to answer.

I much prefer the morals and values of my position in the NHS compared to my previous private employment.  I am not a band 2 however our band 2 admins work with duties befitting band 3 as regular day-to-day activities if someone goes above and beyond their job description this should be reflected in usable reward, they deserve a chance to progress and fight for their right to appropriate pay.  One banding system does not work across all departments, they are not all a booking service.  We hear about clinical patient-facing understaffing and under pay, but the administration side seem not to get the recognition.  We cannot function without them.

I think the Trust should do more to help with staff Psychological Wellbeing and Emotional Resilience due to having to deal with the general public and their traumas which they offload onto us.  Also I think the Trust's needs to do a LOT more work on Population Health and Prevention instead of fire fighting by frontline staff who are only getting burnt out.  Finally, with the rising level of anxiety and concern about Climate breakdown, 3/4 of the population have expressed feeling very worried about climate change and the direction humanity is headed, I would like to bring to your attention the effect this can have on us as Mersey Care staff and frontline healthcare professionals.  It is also on the rise in later life and within our hyper-individualistic, consumer-capitalist society, it can leave a lot of us feeling isolated, alienated, and helpless.  I have not seen any support (maybe I haven't looked hard enough) on the Mersey Care website, YourBrief emails or Magazine about the topic and was wondering if anyone in you are looking into this?  Earlier this year I attended a "Climate Cafe" / Listening Circle run by a member of The Climate Psychology Alliance (CPA).  They have registered and trained practitioners who have been holding safe spaces for people carrying the burden of being climate conscious, where it is often the only outlet people have to meet with likeminded individuals and hold a safe space for exploring how "honouring our emotional responses can create strength in a time of climate and ecological breakdown".  I met with the Psychologist afterwards who was more than happy to train others to be able to facilitate such safe spaces as they spend a lot of their time travelling between Liverpool and Manchester to deliver them.  They are beyond retirement age but continue to practice and volunteer for this cause but acknowledges they won't be able to continue for much longer and is keen to enable others to take up the baton to help with this work.  I'm sure if you did a survey of Mersey Care staff there would be quite a lot of people who are on the scale of "somewhat" to "very worried" as reported in the ONS study above.  I was wondering if this is something the Wellbeing Hub would be able to support?  I did email them this earlier in the year but didn't get any response.  Many thanks.
I wish that senior and operational managers were more active with staff worries/concerns re bullying at work from direct team leaders or other colleagues.
I work across two sites.  These answers were in relation to the Liverpool Community Learning Disability Team.  Many thanks.

I've been working for my service for the last [timeframe removed].  The strains to the service have become more and more visible/significant during this time.  Treatment waiting lists have grown significantly.  Staffing levels have dropped.  Treatments have become more complex due to waiting times.  Time constraints per patient have not altered over the years I have worked for the NHS, meaning I am currently working harder per patient appointment, now than I ever have done before.  I also have the burden of patient dissatisfaction due to waiting times.  The days can become tiresome with constant complaints and maxing out efforts at work constantly.  I don't know the answer to the above problems but I can not see myself continuing within the NHS as I get towards retirement, I don't believe I would be physically capable to do this job full time into retirement.

ICB and commissioning services is creating service gaps the patients are falling through and make it difficult to holistically care for patients and exacerbates long term health problems, mental health concerns and staff stress.

Immediate line and team managers are brilliant and support from them is excellent.  However, I feel higher management do not understand our role, what is needed, the demands of the service and the needs of our client group.  We do not always have adequate resources to do our job.  This makes us feel devalued and affect motivation and wellbeing.

Improvements needed to IT - computers that work, retain charge and internet access remotely,.  Improvements needed to HR and recruitment.  Need to respond to teams where demand is greater than capacity and investment in recruitment is needed.  Many contracts with previous CCGs ( now ICBs) are historic and no longer recognise the increase in demand and patient complexity.

In my role I am due to competing values within the organisational partnership and as a result of my dependence on my employment, I am obliged to witness and collude with moral, ethical and clinical challenges which significantly impact my personal wellbeing and are juxtaposed to my personal and professional values.
Lack support via senior leadership and lack of awareness of our role.  Senior management will request us to complete additional tasks with no additional time which is not realistic.
Long standing staffing issues have been acknowledged and processes are now being put in place to address.
Mersey Care NHS Foundation Trust makes me feel supported through various ways.

Middle management will not consider flexible working as there are not enough staff to in teams to provide the service.  The budget is more important than patients.  Staff are punished and penalised if there is a waiting list yet they are working hard and their pleas for more staff are ignored because of the budget.  There are gaps in service provision where the Commissioners have not considered Physiotherapy for Learning Disability pts in South Sefton, Merseyside.
My answers are in relation to other partners such as LA, not just MCFT.

My current line manager and team manager at the time of this survey are supportive when I raise work pressures and time constraints.  This varies between different team managers and line managers.  There has been a huge increase in level of need with Speech and Language amongst children and this is very difficult to manage without more staff and in fact being understaffed.

My flexible working application was rejected on the base that I don't have kids below 6 years old when was accepted for others who does have kids older then 6 years old - discrimination.  Rotation is not implemented, some people can work from home and some not - issue raised multiple times but not actioned.  Lack of appropriate desks and chairs - have to sit b broken table and armchair - MSK issues - raised with management multiple times - no action.  Management is dismissal and doesn't listen concerns and doesn't consult decisions with team members.

My manager who sits under the community Service in South Sefton does not know how to support  staff that has neuro-diverse brain; they have received some work place training and whilst initially, it was being met with understanding, it became apparent that management were not willing to meet accommodations in practice - it was all just written down in a piece of paper but never really supported.  I have a fantastic colleagues and our Trust is great but we have an unreliable line manager, where they change their statements, they don't do what they say they do, and they see feedback as a threat and take things personally.  In turn, the team morale is low in general and those who has neuro diverse brain were covertly discriminated.

My role requires me to do Bronze-on-call duties.  I do feel that this impacts on work home life balance and is not adequately remunerated.  This also impacts on stress levels as often required to work outside of comfort zone.  This is the only negative aspect of my role and is the main factor for considering alternative employment.

My team has recently joined with other services so will have a new management structure going forward.  The information provided is generally about my pervious small team.  Unsure yet it things will be as good in the future.  Hopefully they will.
Negative comments that have been made in this are in relation to my previous job at another NHS Trust.

NHS management needs to stop continuously attempting to increase the staff work load.  If demands on services are increasing then this should be managed by increasing staffing.  We are continuously being informed that managers are scrutinising your diaries and that we are expected to see a minimum number of patients every day regardless of patient complexity.  Without completing admin outside of work hours it is impossible to maintain a case load.  Support services are disappearing resulting in increasing numbers of complex patients with social problems or safeguarding issues being routinely referred to the service.  Management focus on waiting lists can never be resolved by increasing pressure on existing staff or without investing in new staff and retaining existing staff.  I have worked in the NHS for over [number removed] years and have never witnessed such a hollowed out and demoralised NHS as exists now.
Opportunity for home working would greatly improve my work-life balance.
Our team would be better able to support our patients if we had more permanent staff and did not have to rely on bank staff.
Pressure of workload, demands for managerial, clinical and professional duties can be difficult to manage some days.

PSIRF and all the associated paperwork is clogging up valuable clinical time which would be better served with patients.  Experienced clinicians are processing RADARs, undertaking reviews then reviewing reviews.  It is a cumbersome process involving a multitude of personnel who would be better served treating patients and being available in their departments.  Nobody is questioning the value of patient safety but recently it has become unmanageable and is having a detrimental effect on staff morale.
Questions were clearly worded and easy to understand.
Senior managers are out of touch about the needs of the patients - too focused on numbers.

Since we have had a new management structure, we now have a Operational Service Manager and Professional Lead whereas before we had a Clinical Lead who was my line manager and provided me with regular clinical supervision.  This means I do not have regular clinical supervision and my line manager is not from my own profession.  The suggested resolution to this is to do peer supervision which is not always adequate or appropriate.
Some of the questions are weird.

Staff are not encouraged to progress or develop things or take responsibility for issues that they are the expert on everything needs to be passed through SLT even though they have no expertise in the area.  There are also senior managers who get staff to do pieces of work and then pass the work off as their own with no acknowledgement of who actually did it this is unfair and in no way reflects a JLC culture.  Senior managers do not listen to anything they think they know things even though not in their field of expertise.

Staffing levels are the biggest problem we face.  Recruitment and retention of staff is extremely difficult / poor thus putting more pressure on existing staff.  The real concerns of staff are not being listened to, in Podiatry the eligibility criteria is at least 10 years out of date.  We should be operating a High Risk service only with the levels of staff we have available, yet low risk patients have easy access to NHS assessment / treatment when they should be directed to private practitioners.  Meaningful change is being delayed by transformation and IT systems remain unreliable and time-consuming.
Thank you NHS.

The amount of defensive practice happening now due to fear in clinicians is truly heart-breaking.  I have never in [timeframe removed] seen such anxiety amongst a staff group.  Too many box ticking exercises being placed onto Mersey care staff and not enough recognition for the documentation that covers all these box ticking areas/exercises.  This is taking up far too much clinical time.  There is too much recruitment to managerial positions and too many of these jobs are with people who have no or minimal clinical experience working in the NHS today, and it shows.  Whilst the main areas are ignored the real problems will sadly not be solved.  I will always remain proud to work in the NHS but feel sad that decision making processes that pave the way for the future can present as ill informed at times.

The department does not have enough staff for the demands of the service.  External training courses (once per year) have been cancelled due to low staffing levels/ service demands - Lost CPD.  Service not very flexible on leave for last minute school meetings.  Occupational health times very long but were still expected to work when we have our own health issues.  Too many people on home visits, staff are going to have long term health problems.  A lot would be able to get clinic.
The expected paperwork since the introduction of RiO has become unmanageable!  It means that I have less time to see service users and actually do my job.

The extreme shortage of occupational therapists in the trust has resulted in mobilising existing staff to cover areas of need.  This has then resulted in other occupational therapists leaving the trust, leaving an even bigger deficit and having further consequences on the remaining occupational therapists.  Although it has been declared as a risk; processes are still not in place to address this, e.g. approval for advertising for agency/locum occupational therapists.  Some of this is also hampered by long recruitment processes (4-6 months) subsequently delaying new starters, all of which continues to have a detrimental impact on the delivery of occupational therapy services.

The governance aspect of the job is stifling service development.  I recognise its importance, but so much of my time at work feels like it is taken up 'gathering evidence' - for QRV, CQC monitoring, PSIRF, surveys etc.  Constantly having to evidence that we are performing well ironically feels like it has a negative impact on team performance.  It so often takes me away from looking after patients /direct care and training for my team.  I wish there was a more suitable way to provide quality assurance without the masses of documentation.  I often have to spend time at home completing g this type of documentation.

The leadership team is in disarray, therefore the team comes across as dysfunctional.  No one knows who does what role and people are reluctant do undertake tasks or roles that were previously the responsibility of staff in 'higher' bands..  The team is also incredibly hierarchal..  Some experienced staff do not like being questioned and very defensive about being asked questions, rather than understanding that questions are not criticism, questions are a quest for understanding and learning.

The organisation is now too big, corporate functions including HR are not resourced to support clinical services fully and corporate functions lie with clinical managers who don't always have that skill set amongst everything they need to do.  Plus some corporate functions are still unknown to clinical operational services plus there is at times a cost attached to clinical services for these functions when teams are not always fully established or demand outweighs capacity in a lot of clinical services.

The restructuring of the SLT band 8a and above has had a detrimental effect on the workings of the team.  We have less band 8a time and the duties they performed have filtered down to the band 7s.  There is no appreciation of this from SLT and there is an underlying atmosphere that the Band 7s are not working hard enough; we are told repeatedly that our diaries are being scrutinised and monitored.  I took an instance of incivility from a member of the team to both the band 8a and 8b and no action has been taken, resulting in me feeling unsafe in work.  It was suggested to me, by my band 8a, to request carers leave to take my child for surgery.  After the request was made, I was told I would be able to take 4 hours and have to work the other 4 hours back as TOIL - something that I struggle to do.  If I knew this was the case I would've asked to change my NWD.  This does not make me feel valued by the organisation.
The service is stretched the stress this puts on staff is a lot.  Although I feel supported by my mangers, I can't see it improving without further investment in Allied Health Professional role's.

The sickness/absence policy is too lenient.  We have staff who repeatedly are on long term sick leave and remain in work.  When these staff are in leadership posts and several are off at a time it is difficult to function effectively.

The team I work is excellent - full of dedicated professionals and extremely supportive of one another.  The Trust, however, less so, with the impression that we need to 'tick boxes' over and above providing patient care.  The Trust aim of "perfect care" sets us up to fail - we are expected to deliver perfection, despite imperfect circumstances and situations.  The question regarding accessibility to healthy, nutritious food appears to be essentially asking us if we can prepare a healthy, nutritious packed lunch.  At our current work site we DO NOT have any access to healthy, nutritious food on site, unless we prepare it ourselves and bring it with us.
The trust just doesn't seem to care about employees.

The uncertainty of not knowing if the service will be recommissioned for 2025 is very frustrating and damaging to staff wellbeing and morale.  We provide a high class service to our patients and are passionate about the service and intervention we provide and this should be recognised with basic communication from commissioners regarding the service moving forwards.  Not only staff but patients are very anxious regarding this.

The very fact that dietitians are listed in 'other AHPs' sums up how under valued we are in this trust.  The majority of our patients suffer with obesity or nutritional issues and over the years it has only got worse.  There is not enough being done to support our patients with their food, weight and life-style issues.  In my opinion there is not enough staff support or holistic support in this are.  It has become a very frustrating field to work in and one of the reasons I am leaving.

There is a feeling from staff that senior leadership team do not fully respect AHP roles and the differences in ways of working from nursing teams.  There is a lack of staff engagement with changes driven top down without getting staff on board or listening to staff suggestions.  At times it feels like a blame culture - over scrutiny of caseloads and numbers rather than caring about quality of care or what staff are doing on visits/the complexity - numbers over quality.  No regard for complexity or lack of pathways and processes between teams to be more efficient in working.  Little feedback or appreciation shown towards therapy staff from the SLT within urgent care.  Immediate team and managers very supportive and care about quality of care and patient outcomes.

There is no occupational therapy clinical supervision / support structure.  If I have a particular problem, concern or query about a patient, specific to OT, there is no-one to turn to for help.  This isn't a local team issue, its an OT structure issue.  It would be good to have band 7 OTs who I can approach to ask for help.  Including doing the occasional joint visit in order to address any concerns.  The token group supervision / meeting is pointless as different boroughs offer different things and so any advice is not generalizable.

There is unrealistic expectations for the number of staff in comparison to the volume of work.  Within the organisation there is not a fair number of staff from one region to another when number of referrals to number of staff is compared.  This has been raised and nothing has changed.

There is way too much work to be completed in my contracted hours.  I work over my hours every day, every week.  Much of the excess is of no benefit to the patients and is about organisational bureaucracy.  I am bogged down by endless, pointless Trust-driven exercises.  This is not what I trained to do.

There needs to be parity in development and career progression opportunities for AHPs, it does not exist at this time.  We are not a staff led organisation but a nurse led organisation and this is not in any way derogatory towards nurses, they are the backbone of the organisation, but AHPs deserve an equal opportunity at being the frontrunners and work shoulder to shoulder with nurses.

This is a mental health trust that has acquired a large number of physical health services.  I feel the organisation focuses more on the mental health side.  They have built several new mental health buildings whilst they shunt the health staff from one worn out building to another.  We have been allocated smaller working spaces each and every time we have been moved.  My team in particular has been moved [number removed] times in the last [timeframe removed].  We used to be one large cohesive team but now we are all either working from home or locations that mean we cannot be together.  I am extremely unhappy.  The trust is scrutinising us on KPIs and the band 8s and above have no idea how complex our work is with extremely complex and challenging patients.  I am not really a [job title removed] anymore, I am a case manager and this is not what I joined the NHS for in the first place.  Our immediate band 8s are under such pressure.  One of them left their post after only [timeframe removed] stating that the role was impossible to do for one person.  They were replaced by another, who is saying the exact same thing.  How is that effective for the rest of the team?  .  Merseycare is too big of an organisation and before long something serious will happen with the physical health side of it because, largely due to the trust focusing on their mental health side.  And now job planning - basically a way of managers to tell us that we have to see x amount of patient in a day and x amount of new patients in a week.  This is not a caring organisation, this is a dictatorship!

This organisation has poor management and recruitment process;.  This organisation most of the time listen but has no actions or ignore suggestions from team leads regarding staffing and pressure on the workload.  Poor communication from Band 8's and above with clinical staff.  All services are understaffed.  Staff are leaving and no staff given to replace missing space.
This survey is quite time consuming to complete.

This Trust's priorities are no longer the care of its service users.  There are too many senior managers who are disconnected from the reality and pressures of providing a frontline NHS service.  Unrealistic expectations are placed on staff and morale has visibly and significantly declined over the past 2 years.  There is no acknowledgement of good practice.  This Trust is going to lose highly skilled, motivated and dedicated staff if it does not change its approaches, this will all be of its own doing.

Too many new mandatory courses being added which are either irrelevant to my role or long winded and time consuming which I find difficult to complete while doing a stressful job with 50% of our staff roles not filled.

Too much emphasis on KPI's.  Patient are not seen as people but rather figures by senior management.  Following a re-structure, the job seems to 'big' for the manager and we do not get adequate supervision.  Senior management do not understand what the 'teams and workers' do - they can be unrealistic when delegating.  Mersey care is a truly miserable place to work!
Too much paper work.
We acknowledge this every year, it feels disrespectful for our profession to be classed as 'other AHP' when some are listed.
We are and always have been understaffed.

We are encouraged to innovate and develop new ways of working, but the approval process is long and arduous, we have to submit to senior leadership team in place and then to CCAG and this slows down any new ways of working being launched.  Could there be a quicker process that is less daunting for staff at local level?  We would also like more support with obtaining patient feedback through patient stories and coproduction of services.  Service specs are outdated and need reviewed with opportunities for new service provision and funding opportunities.  There is a disconnect between Operational Leadership teams and those working in more patient safety and quality roles at times.  Lack of admin support also impacts on clinical time spent typing minutes, organising meetings, updating action planners, booking follow up appointments.  Clinical staff do a lot of admin and there is a lot of delegation of work to clinical team from Operations team at times, again impacting on clinical time.  Too much focus on KPIs for response times which are different across teams.  Otherwise there is a really positive culture, staff are respectful to each other and thank each other.  There is a big focus on patient safety.  I really enjoy working at Mersey Care.

We have experienced many changes over recent years, some we have not been consulted on, yet we have not seen the benefit of most of these changes.  When we raise concerns, we are not listened to.  The Management changes that were made, which we were not consulted on, has resulted in a loss of support for the Team and we are now seeing staff leave.  Management over rely on our Team to support others, yet the support is not reciprocal.

We have very little input from anyone above their level so it can sometimes feel like MSK work in their own silo within the trust.  I feel very privileged to work in this MSK team and I'm very grateful for all that they do for the team.  We've also recently had a much bigger presence and input from some management which has also been really appreciated and they've been able to provide information as to how we fit into the bigger Mersey care picture.
