While I feel that my immediate team are very supportive and value our work.  We have undergone changes over the past two years which include reduction of office space/ facilities.  As community staff we are rotated across the city and some teams do not have a base local to the areas they cover.  When working on these teams and under pressure to hit target numbers of patient visits per day we do not have time during a 30 minute lunch break to travel to a health centre that the team use, eat lunch, use facilities and be on time for afternoon appointments.  We need a central base in Liverpool as V7 is in Prescot and out of area for the Liverpool teams.  I have days when I have to eat lunch at supermarket cafes and use their toilet facilities.  Write notes on my laptop in my car.  Which does not feel professional or well supported by the Trust to do my job.  There have been recent incidents of several patients referred for home visits who have weapons such as guns, knives, cross bows and samurai swords in their home.  Through team leads escalating these incidents we have learned more about Weapons in the community policies.  However, it has become clear that other teams within the organisation such as District nurses already knew these patients and had Alerts on their EMIS domain.  The Alerts were not shared across the organisations with all teams who carry out home visits.  It has also been unclear if we have to go to see these patients or are allowed to decline home visits and recommend they attend clinics with more support and security for staff.  There have been a lot of conversations about it and a learning curve for the team.  However, support from senior management, processes to follow and decisions on home visits and how staff feel has been unclear.  Thankfully there has not been an aggressive incident and no one has been harmed.  However, more support and guidance would make staff feel better regarding their roles.

Within the Liverpool Adult community physiotherapy team, the team leads do not and have not for the past 12 months received any clinical supervision despite carrying very complex caseloads.  I felt very unsupported and disappointed with the senior leadership team response to a recent incident when a direct threat was made by a patient to a member of my team.  This patient had a variety of weapons in their home and had made a direct threat to a staff member.  This was reported/escalated via the correct channels.  We were advised to return to this patient's home to deliver their physiotherapy treatment by a number of senior managers despite the threat to staff safety that had been highlighted.  This was an incredibly frustrating and concerning response from senior management and we were made to feel that staff safety is not the Trust's priority.  The senior leadership team are so far removed from us as clinicians and the service that we deliver in patient's homes, that they are giving dangerous advice that if followed could have put staff at risk of physical harm.  Our current operational lead has a role that is completely unmanageable, managing too may teams.  As a result tasks such as occupational health referrals for staff are not being completed.

Working on an in-patient ward has affected the quality and consistency of clinical/managerial supervision - it is a tick box exercise as managers don't have time to see all the staff.  Often the 'supervision' is recorded when there has been a staff meeting to announce a new policy e.g. bare from the elbow down reminder - even if the staff member hasn't attended the meeting!  This definitely happens when the deadline is nearing for going 'into the red' for targets.  Such as shame as regular 1:1 clinical/managerial supervision has retained staff over the years, and now we are facing a mass exodus.  Also I feel not allowing staff to work on wards if unable to complete the full PSS training is discriminatory and short-sighted - we are losing experience, skill and obviously valued staff due to this policy, and will also affect recruitment in terms of potentially excellent clinicians who are unable to get down/up from the floor on a rare occasion (compared to the contribution they could provide for the 99.9% of the remaining tasks).

As members of the former NWBH Trust we are treated like second class citizens by original Merseycare, we have lost our sense of community because we were actively forbidden to continue meeting as medics as a group in NWBH, it doesn't feel as though our opinion counts at all and even if our practices were better they are just swept away with no thought and no consultation with us and the juggernaut that is Merseycare just keeps coming at us and steam rolling over us.  Mersey care seems to think everything about them is better.  My quality of work life has greatly depreciated since being taken over by Mersey Care.  They don't want to hear what's actually happening or what we actually think and want to whitewash everything with their idea of how they think things should be.  Patient centred care has suffered immeasurably since the move to Mersey care and the community team transformation.  If you raise any concerns you just get a polite email saying "This is how we do it or we've always done it just get on with it" but no real engagement, or care, and a sense that your name has been added to the naughty list

Freedom to speak up -what a joke ....  Serious patient safety concerns due to incompetent negligent fellow [job title removed].  Wider employing Trust has zero idea how to deal with this issue.  Swept under the carpet for the 3rd time after 3rd NHS England /Practitioner Performance investigation.  [job title removed] in question still in post 2 years later albeit excluded from [details removed] work while my employing Trust 'manage' the situation.  Meanwhile the whistleblowers including me in my dept, are harassed and bullied by the wider Trust and subject to frivolous malicious grievance procedures; the outcome of which bear no relation to what actually happened and how and why many patients were harmed is ignored.  Several [job title removed] in my dept significantly affected by the wider Trust's mismanagement of this situation and had time off sick with workplace related stress as a consequence.  This further affects the ability of my dept to provide care for vulnerable referred patients.  Disillusioned at the moment and certainly view the past 2 years in my NHS clinical role as the worst in my [timeframe removed] career as a UK salaried Community dentist.

I have great concerns regarding the lack of staff in Community dental services, lack of recruitment to replace staff leading to more pressure being put on current staff causing multiple mistakes and near misses.

I have no confidence that Mersey Care NHS Foundation Trust can keep me, as an employee, safe at work.  Nor do I have confidence in the Trust's ability to keep patients safe.  Policies and procedures are routinely ignored and flouted, and staff members (including me) are treated as troublemakers when we point this out and ask that policies be adhered to.  I have raised my concerns formally and have not had a satisfactory response.  I have found working for Mersey Care to be an incredibly demoralising experience and I wouldn't recommend a career here to anyone.
I'm not sure who my line manager is.
Merseycare should consider having a substantive plan for doctors recruited from outside the UK.

Over the time I have worked for the organisation I have found that medical staff are less appreciated and less respected.  Very seldom does the organisation provide any positive feedback to the medical work force in any of it's comms or in person whilst other professions are celebrated and promoted.  We are taken for granted until something goes wrong.
Parity of pay with other trust employed GPs needs to be sorted - ongoing issues for years.

Senior managers make constant changes without sufficient justification and don't listen to senior clinicians.  Staffing is unsafe and staff are undermined when attempting to raise concerns.  There is no meaningful consultation with senior clinicians prior to repeated organizational change and large amounts of money wasted on this.  Staff are micromanaged poorly and suggestions for improvement by experienced staff are ignored.  This has led to a continued exodus of very good staff from acute teams.
Thank you.

The management - non clinical and clinical are reasons why staff leave and don't wish to return to this particular trust.  Easy solutions to respect and value staff to retain them are ignored in favour of power hungry management.  Managers often leave only to be recycled elsewhere and similar valued and like-minded individuals replace them thus the pattern never changes.
The team I work in is fantastic.

There is a discrepancy between the approach taken between physical and mental health in culture.  There appears to be persistent behaviours from Liverpool Community Health that are embedded and affecting community health.  As such, the lauded Restorative and Just Learning Culture is not embedded if you don't fit the dominant culture.

There is too much bureaucracy in the organisation that stifles innovation and meaningful change.  And not nearly enough empowered decision-making closer to clinical services.  As a senior clinical leader I really value my working relationships with operational colleagues but there really are too many layers of operational management.  Clinicians are tightly regulated however we have great disparity in how clinical staff and operational/strategic staff are held accountable.  It's hard to avoid poorly accountable ops colleagues who often don't feel empowered or are ill-equipped to make decisions.  There seems much more tendency towards a Command and Control as opposed to empowering devolved, accountable, decision making to colleagues where the information is at ground level.  Part of my own role is to ensure clinical governance is appropriate in the services I lead.  As an organisation I think we struggle to embed lessons learned from COVID where we were far more pragmatic.  We seem now to have extended past the point of optimal governance, which now hinders processes and makes change too arduous and complex to be useful.  We are constantly fed 'near miss' examples to make us vigilant but the same context is not applied on the flip side to great practice which seems to be celebrated, but not applied and embedded.  This makes us risk averse but indirectly 'quality averse' too.  Motivated and innovative staff in clinics that I lead are now preferring to 'simply turn up to work' as it's just too hard to make any meaningful change.  I should qualify this by saying these are not staff simply ground down by workload; work is tough, but it is the often 'off-target' and poorly supportive infrastructure around that grinds them down.

There needs to be some flexibility in Job Planning so that work life balance can be achieved e, g condensed 4 days working provided that medical cover is available.  Condenses hours is an option which appear to be available and is universally applied to all disciplines; clinicians and managers except medical staff.
Trust to be more supportive with team and acknowledge our work ethics and reasoning to improve service.

We are under a lot of pressure because of very high numbers of referrals in to our service and insufficient clinicians to provide the treatment.  There is no cover for colleagues on maternity leave, or colleagues who are off with long term sickness.  Staff who leave are not replaced.  Management and non clinical staff have suggested that we look at "new ways of working", when really the solution is to ensure we have appropriate staffing levels.  The staff who are in work are at risk from stress and burn out.

[name removed] of Chesterton Unit, is an excellent Service Manager.  I have communicated with them about some of my struggles and concerns, and they were a good listener who provided valuable leadership guidance without being judgmental.  [name removed] fosters excellent supportive leadership and promotes a positive working environment while ensuring that all staff meet up with necessary tasks and deliver safe quality care to meet patients holistic needs.  [name removed] is an outstanding Matron for Chesterton Unit.  I have learned a great deal from their leadership style, and they have been very supportive of staff.  They assisted in signing off my revalidation and provided constructive advice, ensuring that the nursing team stays updated with their training by booking us for various mandatory training sessions.  My colleague, [name removed], who often works the twilight shifts on the Chesterton Unit, is one of the most supportive HCA I have encountered.  They are humble, respectful, and not only supports us nurses, but also supports the team, and patients with enthusiasm, compassion, and good intentions.  They are a valuable member of the team.  [name removed]: During my induction week, last year [date removed], I attended an excellent training session on maintaining safe boundaries, led by[name removed].  Their clear and engaging style emphasised the importance of professional boundaries with patients.  As a result, I have become more assertive and attentive in my interactions with patients, which has helped me maintain high standards of care up till this day.  [name removed] knowledge and passion in delivering this vital training have positively impacted my everyday practice. - My line Manager, [name removed], is a new Unit Manager for Chesterton who effectively manages the unit and staff.  While they are making progress in their role, I believe they will continue to develop their skills as a Unit Manager.  Although, they have not yet taken the initiative to visit the night shift to introduce themself to the night team - some members of the night team have reported that they have not seen them and do not know them.  I hope they will find more opportunities to engage with them in the future.  Additionally, I feel it would be beneficial for them to adopt a more diverse approach including when documenting supervisions.  It is important to avoid a judgmental approach, particularly when recording feedback from staff.  Recording solely the negative comments may not provide a complete picture; they should also consider the perspectives of the other staff involved to ensure a fair and balanced assessment.  There is still room for improvement, as we all learn every day, including myself.  I believe [name removed] will become an excellent manager as they progress in this new role.  Regarding Nurses Pay, Nurses dedicate themselves to providing exceptional care, often working long hours, including nights, to ensure the well-being of their patients.  Despite this commitment, the current salary levels do not adequately reflect the hard work and sacrifices made by nurses.  With the rising cost of living and high tax burdens, many nurses find that their salaries are insufficient to cover essential expenses.  In fact, for some, their earnings finishes within the first week of receiving their pay checks due to the demands of bills and the increasing cost of groceries to support their children and families.  It would be well appreciated to consider an increase in nurses salaries.  Such a change would help alleviate the financial pressures nurses face.  Suggestions - all patient in Chesterton Unit would benefit from having their own television in their room but this is not always possible; Therefore, I would like to suggest - to create a second TV in the small quiet lounge with access to high-quality internet so they can watch their favourite TV shows, Sky, or Netflix programs as this could help reduce occasional TV conflicts between patients.  Finally, I believe Chesterton would benefit from therapeutic technology, such as an electronic massage chair/sofa, which can help relax both patients and staff.  This device has been proven to be effective and reduces patient triggers to avoid escalations.
a few months ago I moved from my previous team where I was burnt out, traumatised, and very stressed.  Since moving to my current team my mental health has improved and I have been enjoying my role.
A positive organisation that does the best possible for both staff and patients within the limitations of the NHS and wider health and social care system.
All answers are true.

Although flexible working is high on MCT agenda in my experience it is only given to people in our team with anxiety this doesn't seem fair so if wanted for a work life balance [family member removed] come along etc. its a flat no so although wanted cant get it, as I'm getting older and people retire which I'm not in a position to do flexible working would make life easier.  Seems to be only given to staff who struggle I feel its only on MCT agenda to look good and not a real thing.  Always the get out cause for MCT is needs of the business.

Although I thoroughly enjoy being a Nurse.  I feel this role has took it's toll on me emotionally and physically.  I did not go into the profession with the view to earn a lot of money, but to help people.  However the work increased work demands, and the added pressures, increases anxiety that I could do something wrong, forget something, carry out a drug error.  There is no amount of money that would alleviate this feeling.  Furthermore, I have missed Christmas and birthdays with my [family members removed].  I haven't been on a holiday abroad with my [family members removed] in over 12 years due to financial restraints and not being able to book A/L when the [family members removed] are available due to staffing issues.  I am looking to change my career and leave nursing within the near future.  I have not made this decision lightly, questioning myself and looked at the roles I am in, blaming COVID 19 for my feelings.  Reflecting now, its the role as a Nurse that its what has been the issue and made me unhappy.

Am well supported by managers, colleagues and the Trust.  The only issue I have is that staff are not paid well to reflect their responsibility.  Band 2, 3 and 4 staff are lowly paid as are the rest of the NHS.  I worry about how they can afford to live comfortable lives and achieve ambitions to own homes, take holidays without having to work lots of extra hours.

As an international recruit I can boldly say am happy with my job and I do not see myself leaving it, am willing to grow and develop professionally and fully explore all the opportunities it offers me.  I recommend my employer as one of the best as far.

As Mersey Care is one of the largest employers in the Northwest, it prevents staff from being honest with their views, when one does raise their concerns, one is not supported.  One's manager can avoid transparency, frequently change targets, undermine confidence by micromanaging.

At present, I am struggling to fulfil my role.  At times feel vey overwhelmed and stressed in supporting complex individuals without the ability to influence care on a 24 hour a day basis.  Managers have been extremely supportive since my employment with Mersey Care.

Autistic staff are mis-treated here, there is no scope to understand them, they mask there behaviours, so they can fit in, a managers understanding of autism is negative and as an autistic person, I am expected to fit in.  Or move on, I feel disrespected for my condition and managers don't want to or know how to look after me in a caring, no insulting way.

Awful communications in this organisation.  Bullying senior leadership teams led by narcissist sociopaths with dunning kruger tendencies.  Plenty of nepotism for all levels of senior management make this a truly toxic place to work.  Mangers have fantastic ideas on running services without the faintest real world insight into how they might operate, and fail to listen to staff who have a wealth more knowledge than them.  Operational managers have a deep suspicion of their staff and this manifests in a culture of expecting failure and punishing innovation.  Flexible working offer is lip service only.  Nice way to treat the Whalley grange staff, that's how this place operates.
Better pay for the abuse we have to deal with from patients.  More staff.  Can't remember a day when the ward was fully staffed.

Better trust wide support for disabled staff Is desperately needed.  Trust wide training for all staff regarding neurodiversity's, including information regarding support, understanding and acceptance for any colleagues diagnosed with a neurodiverse condition is required.  Better timely organisation of reasonable adjustments support implementation, and more better action from the trust in regards to ensuring access to work awarded support is ordered and implemented in a timely manner by the trust is very much needed.  More stronger visibility and visible leadership from senior management and senior HR is needed in relation to support for disabled staff.  Maybe senior managers (CEO and the executive team) can consider having a bi monthly alternative virtual drop in session for disabled staff that is not the same as the staff disability network meetings but is led by the CEO, chief nurse and other senior managers.  Having this in place would be beneficial to the trust to show strong leadership that disabled staff will be better supported in future, and that there is an open forum for disabled staff to feel that their concerns, ideas and needs are being listened to and taken on board.  This will also help disabled staff to not be discriminated against, and to ensure that they will not face any discrimination in relation to their reasonable adjustments or because of the simple fact they require reasonable adjustments.  The proposed business case for the reasonable adjustments officer roles needs to be heard as soon as possible to ensure that hopefully this role can be implemented to better support the current and future disabled workforce, and improve experiences for disabled colleagues, managers and other trust wide colleagues.

Brilliant team, which all help each other and strive for excellence/best practice.  Amazing, caring and supportive manager and band 6 supervisors, who are always there to help.  Very happy in my place of work.
Bullying is not dealt with when it is not overt.

Certain members of SLT due to their relationship with execs display inappropriate behaviour to other members of the SLT which goes unchallenged by the chair.  Staff have raised concerns about nepotism and how their behaviour is accepted which is upsetting to see when it badly affects senior staff.  Work that would allow staff to develop is given straight to certain staff, again due to relationships, and excludes other staff members preventing their development.  Civility and respect does not include these 2 senior leaders they speak and behave however they like with lack of challenge.
Comments regarding my experience with 1 manager were addressed by myself during my PACE and discussed.  Staff witnessed and raised it within the team.
Communication in all areas need to improve.

Community CPNS should be paid at a higher banding, the risks, and knowledge/policies, frameworks, KPIS, sops, we are meant to be aware of is unbelievable.  Then detentions, MHA, medicines, child protection, safeguarding the list goes on.

Community nursing has changed dramatically since the 'Waiting' for patients was originally designed.  For example - having a waiting of 1 so 15 minutes for an insulin visit is ridiculous when it takes 20 minutes to even drive to the patient.  Patient's are more complex and we have taken on more responsibilities but the time for this is remains the same.  We are continuously working through our unpaid lunch time and into the evening when we are at home.  Also there needs to be better communication between the hospitals and the community as we often get no referral for a patient or a limited one that lacks important information.

Considering how many staff the team is short by, it would be nice to bring back overtime without having to have it approved at the last second when you're absolutely desperate for staff.  Also, when someone speaks up against bullying, it would be nice if those who then speak up as a result, are not treated like criminals and told by the 8B not to gossip.  Not really sure how Mersey Care has managed to miss the ball so terribly with freedom to speak up.  Usually when one person whistle blows, it causes a domino effect of other people finding the confidence to speak up.  When this happened in my team, concerns fell upon deaf ears and we were told it was a 'witch hunt' and the person accused was protected and not investigated.  The original person who was spoken up about has now left and 10 staff members have left since person accused.  Maybe you should take a look at that.

Currently[timeframe and condition removed] and totally anxious.  I have[details removed] previous and this is my [details removed] and first baby.  I am only mentioning this because I can't thank my manager enough for the help and support they have given me in returning to work from a period of morning sickness and constant worry/anxiety around this pregnancy.  Occupational health  was also amazing and really helpful but my manager has been amazing.
Easier access to equipment when faulty such as laptops.  Manager is very helpful and supportive - Good practice is encouraged fully.  More adequate staffing.

Every member of staff who is a peer states they are micromanaged.  Senior leadership team making decisions affecting clinical work with out consultation with practitioners which is detrimental to patient care.

Every year that I have worked for the NHS it has gradually got worse and just when I think it cannot get any worse, it has done.  Unfortunately, Mersey Care has a leadership culture of bullies.  It is very much "jobs for the boys".  It does go to efforts to disguise this but when you work within the organisation, to me, it is very obvious.  The poor pay offered by the NHS, I can accept and work around.  However, the toxic leadership in very senior positions who bully frontline staff infuriates me.  The Independent Sector is looking increasing appealing to me.  Who not only offer a more attractive personal salary/gains but they also appear to look after their staff better.  If you look after your staff, they will look after your patients.

Excessive and increasing workload - not achievable, increasing stress levels and staff morale very low, staff regularly working through lunch and after hours.  Feeling micro-managed rather than supported.  KPI focused.  Documentation exorbitant and repetitive.  Not feeling listened to.  Clerical support has declined since clerks are not based in offices.
Fantastic supportive manager absolutely divine role.
Far too many chiefs not enough Indians is the problem in the NHS.

Far too many new ways of working/pilots introduced into teams.  Unsafe to introduce so many pilots together.  Staffing levels are not reflected/increased when extra work is put on to teams.  Very poor visible presence from Senior Leads 8B and above, despite requests for support from Teams.  Definitely looking to move elsewhere within the NHS.
Feel that succession planning could be better.

Flexible working is zero.  Some people get unofficial family friendly hours and some don't.  Organisation sold the working pattern of 2 LDS and a short shift but this is non existent for some people but others are aloud to do it meaning they get 5 requests a week when some only get 2 which is unfair.  Off duty is not always done fairly.  People with no or older kids have to pick up what's left.  Some people get bank booked by manager however some don't which is unfair.

Found support from HR/Pensions when enquiring about possibility of secondment role impacting special class status very lacking.  Therefore did not pursue the secondment which would have given me chance to further develop management skills and experience.

From above my ward manager the support is non-existent.  No support for caring responsibilities outside of work, no support with disability.  Trying to deny family friendly working when it doesn't impact the ward.  No support with sickness due to disability.
Great leadership team who are supportive.
Great thanks.
Happy working here.
Having retired form my full time post, I was able to return to work and commence in a different role which hinges more on the physical aspects of nursing in a totally different field.

Having worked in the organisation for the whole of my adult life.  I have always worked in Mental health division.  Had I have still been there my answers would be completely different.  The culture is Mental health division is shocking.  Since moving to [location and timeframe removed] ago I feel happy going into work to do a job I love supporting patients and families making a huge difference in people lives.  Had I not have left Mental health division I would have left to go to private sector.  I now have a good work life balance and I'm supported to do so.
Helpful survey makes you think and triggers thought.

Hi.  As an [ethnicity and nationality removed] with a postgrad visa working as a support worker, career progression is not possible for me because of the visa which Mersey care doesn't sponsor band [number removed] to stay in country, it would be lovely if they do cause I would like to progress in my career as a nurse also willing to continue to work for NHS cause it is really a lovely place to work.  The staff are very nice and accommodating.  Thank you.

Honestly speaking I feel the comments or negative thoughts would not be addressed in surveys as I feel this exercise is a tick box.  The information that is collated never gets fed back to staff delivering the care.  As if that was the case here would be changes in the organisation.  As staff have approached myself about being frustrated regarding management and have been honest completing this survey.

I am a new starter to CRHT and I am very happy in my role, the team are amazing.  I am very proud to work alongside them all.  The impact the team has upon patients is truly appreciated from patients and family's.  It is a pleasure to see each day.
I am a returning staff member.  I left the NHS for over three years but I am happy to be within my new team and role.

I am aware of the intense pressure the DN Staff work under, and this is reflected in our RADAR reporting system, incident that could be avoided, and have seen Nursing staff worn out, burnt out and tearful due to lack of staff and increased workloads with patients increasing complexity in the Community.  No time for staff to complete Training, new nurses taking on patients and increasing workloads whilst in preceptorship.

I am committed to my organisation and am happy with support I receive from my line manager.  The only issue is working Late shift which is 1-9pm.  I would be more pleased if this work pattern is changed so we can focus on 9-5pm or 8-4pm like all other CMHTs.  Reason being childcare issues.

I am concerned that the senior leadership team are not visible.  The SLT apply/enforce working practices that are not realistic, they appear to only have concerns about stakeholders and appear to have no concerns for patient or staff well being.
I am happy in my new role.  It is new and challenging but I am supported by the operational lead.

I am hugely disappointed with Mersey care and its total dismissiveness to the concerns raised by its staff and the ongoing ignorance of this year after year and terrible level of leadership within secondary care from a senior management level including awful service changes decisions that have had a catastrophically negative effect on safe staffing as a a result, inevitably morale, including the terrible decisions to HALVE staffing at night for A and E Psychiatric Liaison CORE 24 teams and merging of Home Treatment teams which has resulted in inevitable staffing exodus and worsening of service standards and patient care and safety.  The worst and most insulting thing about this also, is not just the effect this has on staff sickness, loss of experienced staff but also when we are bombarded with self congratulatory emails of the trust praising itself for a delusional "job well done" mentality in the middle of daily ongoing chaos and senior managers giving each other awards while everyone else suffers, as well as patronising reminders of supposed 'welfare' resources that are woefully inadequate to address the trust's monumentally unsafe staffing levels that staff face every day, which we would not have to consider even using if staffing was ever at normal levels which it now hardly ever is.  This is the worst I have ever seen mental health service provision in my life time and makes me openly embarrassed to work for this trust.  For shame.  I see absolutely no hope of this ever improving without reverting back to previous work practices now cheerfully abandoned in favour of a false economy of 'saving money' that will result in more patients suing the trust for unsafe care provision and thus cancelling out any supposed savings made.  I find this tragic as the trust's staff are hugely hard working and their good will is always willfully abused and any form of constructive criticism form staff is always met with unfair instant accusations of 'being negative'.  There is a difference between being a pessimist and a realist.  If the trust actually listened more to the genuine concerns of staff, they would actually learn to find service improvements that actually work and not fake ones at the expense of staff and patient health.
I am just happy to work in my organisation and looking forward to learn more in the near future.
I am proud of the team I work with and have very supportive colleagues and manager.  I'm also proud of the work we do for our families.
I am proud to work for an organisation that places the people we serve and staff at the centre of everything we do.

I am really happy working within my team.  Although the job can be stressful due to the nature of the caseload, there is always support via debrief, reflective practice, supervision, working collaboratively with other colleagues for support.
I am struggling with work at the minute not enough staff to do the role.
I am very satisfied with my working life in my team within Merseycare.

I do feel that since being taken over by Mersey Care things have changed.  It appears to be more about quantity than quality with work and the paperwork has tripled.  We spend most of our time completing paperwork than doing face to face work.  Mersey Care advertise flexible/hybrid working however we are never allowed to work from home.  I know certain staff work from home it depends if your face fits at times.  This Trust is very good at saying we will sort things out when there is a problem but that rarely happens.  Too many managers and not enough front facing staff.

I do feel valued and appreciated by my colleagues within my team however, we are not supported by operational and clinical leads at all.  The service is in crisis and we have been made to feel it is our fault.  The team as a whole feel deflated and defeated.  Some members have gone to freedom to speak.  RCN are also investigating and supporting the team going forward.  We are being managed by people who have no clue on what is going on on the ground.  And for those that do know the role they are dismissing our concerns and have an answer for everything.  Like politicians.  To run a DN service effectively and safely you need to know the role of the DN.
I do not feel supported by my direct line manager or feel they are a good role model or leader for our team, however, feel very supported by our other team leader, which I am very thankful for.

I do not feel that Mersey Care is invested in providing the right support to front-line staff.  Senior management is not seen by frontline staff, they do not appear to be consulting staff on what is needed!  They need to bring back free up Friday and start being more visible to staff.

I do not feel that person centred care is the priority of this trust.  Patients are moved out of area due to staffing levels in the area that they live, this has been detrimental to my patients.  Crisis care for our patients with dementia is non-existent and I feel that older persons community care is under resourced and under funded.

I enjoy my job and feel a part of an excellent team.  I work in a small team with one other colleague and an admin assistant.  The job can become very stressful when my colleague is on annual leave as I still have the same amount of work for 2 people to get through.  Also when the admin is off it is very stressful as I am expected to do my job as well as all the admin side which is very time consuming and greatly impacts upon my work and the amount I can complete.
I enjoy my job role and the ward I work on, including the staffing team.
I enjoy my job working for Mersey care there are challenges however I enjoy the variety.
I enjoy working for Mersey Care.
I enjoy working for the trust and feel they offer plenty of opportunity to improve yourself and progress.

I enjoy working with my current team, we all have a respectful strong relationship and support each other.  I also feel very supported by my team leader with regular catch up.  I do not feel supported by higher management, My self and my team lead feel we have no autonomy or say with regards to our own team.  Regularly feel disregarded and ignored by higher management who have no interest in our or our staffs mental health wellbeing.  Higher management have "regular meetings" which feel nothing more than just a tick box exercise with little or no difference seen.  8-8 where brought in without considering staff needs, again this felt like a tick box exercise, the reasons for the 8-8 has not helped and instead stretched services, no continuity in patient care and services are now short which has a knock on effect within the teams as we are constantly helping and picking up other calls for teams.  I left a team where I wanted to remain due to the constant stress I was under.  I started a new post which felt critically unsafe daily with no support from senior leadership with hurtful comments made.  I was currently having MSK issues which required surgery to fix this, and I think if my operation date didn't come when it did I probably would have ended up off sick with anxiety and stress.
I enjoying working for the organisation.
I feel communication within the service could be improved to reduce anxieties and a clear plan as to where the service is going.

I feel extremely let down by my SLT, after a period of sickness I returned to work and my role had changed.  Not one of my SLT kept me updated during my sick leave or upon my return that my job role had changed.  I have been criticised by my line manager and offered little support since my return.  I feel undervalued/ignored/demotivated and hurt since my return.  Supervision is a tick box exercise with little value other than to support the organisations target and manager that 'issues' are documented with no follow up or resolution.
I feel like the joy of working is being slowly stripped away through micromanagement and a distinct lack of comprehension/respect for roles.

I feel management isn't listening to me I am a full time carer for my [condition removed] [family member removed] I have just found a shift pattern that works for home and work and now they want yo change my shift pattern but this won't get looked into so pointless putting anything in really just more worry and stress no one to talk to so I can get my point across.

I feel Mersey care could be improved by having more nurses on the floor, there are to many managers who cannot do the role they are appointed into, less band 8 roles and more clinical staff throughout the trust, especially in the community.

I feel Mersey Care has caused a lot of staff to leave because of poor management.  Bullying tactics.  I have never known staff morale to be so low.  Nurses can't use their own ability and skill to make decisions there is a tick box for everything it's too much.  Its becoming a dictatorship not a workplace.  Been a nurse for [number removed] years never felt like this.
I feel my division cares more about performance then patient safety.  Often feel decisions at an SLT level are very short sighted.  Unreasonable work load pressure at times.

I feel my immediate manager is very supportive, they listens to my concerns and will always but my health before work.  I do struggle sometimes with the higher managers and the way they communicate things with me especially when changes need to be made in the way I work.

I feel my service has been neglected, for many years we have been expected to deliver high quality care and ease the pressure of secondary care services, all without the basics of a team base, or even room provision to see patients.  This impacts on our ability to deliver care appropriately, connect as a team, and the very basics of administrative tasks such as printing and sending letters.  Many staff have moved on from the team since it's inception around 4 years ago as the work is simply not sustainable, in terms of high expectation coupled with the deprived resources we are afforded.  Team meetings, when they are at all held in person, are not fit for purpose; often with staff having to stand for an entire 2 hour meeting due to insufficient chairs/space.  I can only see more staff vacating their posts, until maybe they can no longer be filled.  This is all whilst working psychologically with a demographic of patients with complex trauma, emotional and relational difficulties, which is extremely hard, even if in the context of being adequately resourced.  I hope these issues can be highlighted and addressed.

I feel our CLDT is often overlooked and widely unheard of however I feel we are a strong compassionate team that are improving the lives of people who have a learning disability daily.  Our team would benefit from the employment of more specialist nurses to the team to help continue to provide our high levels of patient care.
I feel our role is not appreciated and despite staffing pressures within the team staff are often moved at short notice to support other service lines.  This is very unsettling for the team.
I feel really fortunate to work the manager that I do, they are very supportive, understanding and an all round great person.

I feel since this trust has merged I feel there is poor communication, the staff establishment is very poor with very little effort into retaining staff and looking at why staff don't wish to be mobilised to certain wards.  Mersey care have too many senior members of staff with very little effort into safely staffing the wards and no focus on patient care - there is more focus on KPI and targets rather than patient safety and care.  There remains little support from senior managements to ward staff and as a band 6 I have no management days and work within the numbers and have a high expectation to complete managerial duties whilst being the nurse in charge.

I feel supported by my line manager and my team but not the higher management who have recently brought about an organisational change and left us in chaos.  One [job title removed] changed our off duty and didn't reply to several emails.

I feel supported by our managers and higher up managers too, I feel that I am able to approach managers and colleagues to discuss work when required.  I feel that the managers also encourage us to develop our careers further.

I feel that all the correct things are said however sometimes this feels like management speeches, not personal or individual.  The lack of staff and lack of vacancies other than mental health roles does not make any improvement to staff moral.  Staff are exhausted but keep being told that the ratios are correct.  It feels like there is an ever increasing gap between those working on the frontline and senior leaders.  The rules are so conflictive where staff have to wear uniform in the office environment however senior leadership can wear what they like?  It feels very divided and lip service is paid to the struggling teams out there.

I feel that as a district nurse working x5 days a week I often feel I do not have a good work life balance however I feel with our current salary I would be unable to drop my hours to part time.  I am also aware that in the field I work in within my organisation I am not able to do long days as they are not offered.  I also feel that I cannot do bank work as this would affect my mental health as I already work full time hours.  I also feel that I have had stressful times over the past year due to car breakdowns.  As my work requires me to use my own car to visit patients this has a detrimental effect on the service and makes me feel guilty if I am without a car due to breakdown.  I also feel we do not get enough money in terms of expenses for wear and tear on our own vehicles being used daily to sometimes travel up to 30 miles a day.
I feel that our team has an excellent care culture - very nurturing and a lovely team to work in.

I feel that since we have joined Mersey Care the work environment has significantly deteriorated.  There is low morale, and my team are hemorrhaging staff.  My team no longer feels like an extended family.  Staff go home upset on a regular basis.  It feels like a tick box culture.  I feel the care of many patients is affected as when staff leave they are put onto other peoples caseloads.  The IT systems are poor and changes that are needed are not undertaken in a timely manner.  I used to love my Job under the previous trust, this is no longer the case.  I will remain for now as I am trying to improve my patients care.  If the opportunity raises it's head I will leave.

I feel that the staffer staffing model needs to be implemented appropriately - to avoid staff burn out, stress and pressures.  Particularly with winter pressures impending.  We as a team work on minimum staffing number meaning staff sickness etc. really does impact service delivery - leading to long waits and stressful working environments.

I feel that there are communication issues in certain divisions in the organisation which hinder progress of the good work that is happening in the organisation which can often lead to frustration and poor job satisfaction amongst employees.  The trust also seem to focus on what the employee does for them rather than how the employee can develop in their career pathway in processes such as PACE which can also be disheartening when employees want to develop their skills further which will ultimately benefit the trust, staff morale and patient outcome.

I feel that this trust is bullying staff and changes rules and regulations to suit what they want to do.  A member of staff in my team is being treated appallingly currently by senior management.  I would not recommend Mersey Care as a trust to work for.

I feel that when I first joined this organisation a few years ago; the trust was an excellent place to work.  Sadly; over recent years there has been a huge decline in the overall culture, especially from senior management.  I often see what I would call bullying behaviours on meetings and feel that recruitment to senior posts are already a; ''done deal'' prior to even been advertised, there has been a definite increase in nepotism in regards to recruitment, especially around family members of senior leaders getting jobs and being promoted.

I feel that within MERSEYCARE the goal of expansion and increasing wealth is often main priority rather then excelling at patient care.  The trust takes over vast areas of care however never reinvests back into the existing one, it loads more work onto already stretched services and then expects everyone to just concede to the new way of working despite it being potentially unsafe.  It feels as if whomever bids for the services we take on has no clue about what is actually happening on the ground and just decides to take on work passing the weight down through the management structure to the staff on the ground, expecting miracles that are unreasonable and not what staff actually want.  The expectation to work within this archaic structure of 'I say and you do' is soo antiquated.  The staff are highly intelligent, flexible and adaptive individuals with good insight and excellent care but this is stretched thin when the trust demands raise and the capacity does not reflect.  WE are not transparent to our partners in PCNs or ICBs, we hide the fact that staffing isn't safe or within line to receive the volume of work we have agreed to and we don't do anything well.  WE scrape by doing things to ensure the homeostasis however we don't do anything very well or excel at anything.

I feel that within the organisation, our work load is continuing to increase and we are expected to just get on with it.  On days when our team are struggling, I feel the problems are papered over and no real plan is put in place for a long term goal.  More and more is expected of us, but when things go wrong, I still feel we have a blame culture and are not supported.  We struggle through more and more work loads, and there is no cut off, we are expected to just continue, sometimes with no support at all.
I feel the main priority for Mersey care is mental health care/workers rather than the adult nursing areas.

I feel the organisation is very focused on saving money to the detriment of patient care.  There is no succession planning, feel that the organisation on a whole are not proactive for planning for the future and ensuring teams are safely staffed prior to staff leaving.  Budgets are not regularly reviewed and haven't been updated for 10 + years to reflect the patient complexity and acuity.
I feel the senior leadership team need to work on empathic communication and understanding roles of those who work in teams.  Too much of a gap between expectations and understanding of roles.

I feel the stages for sickness are unreasonable and quite unfair for staff who experience long term issues, whether they are waiting on a diagnosis or not.  I believe this causes unnecessary stress and this is not fair at all for staff.

I feel this trust as a culture of you do as I say and are not approachable to staff, they don't listen to staff concerns nor try to resolve this.  Going to freedom of speak doesn't help either as we as a team have approach this route with no resolution for [timeframe removed] and we are still waiting.
I feel valued and supported by my immediate management team and that is the only reason I have stayed and intend to remain in this current employment.
I feel very grateful - I very much enjoy my role and our team are so supportive of each other.

I feel very settled and happy within my team.  My manager is very supportive and approachable, which keeps the staff moral high.  My colleagues are great to work with, and we are very supportive to each other.  I feel lucky to be part of this amazing team!
I feel very supported in my work place by my colleagues and especially by my manager.
I feel very supported within my team and very grateful to work here.

I feel well supported in my current role.  There is strong clinical support, which provides me confidence in my current role.  Recently, I feel the team dynamics have changed.  On the surface people seem respectful and friendly with each other, however I have sensed difficulties within the team relating to people feeling disconnected, some disciplines seeming more 'protected' than others in response to increasing demands, and limited resources, however decisions are not very clear and therefore creating misunderstanding within the team.  Limited information is cascaded from senior leadership meetings.  HR have not previously been supported when faced with challenges relating to performance management.  On-going concerns, impacting sense of team working, has impacted on relationships with the team..
I find it hard at times to keep up with all the changes within the organisation.

I found that when some colleagues have been given the opportunity to step into a role for months, with the belief they will be accepted to complete the role and stay in the role if proven to be good at that job role for years to come.  But then not be informed they aren't allowed to continue in that role and they have been informed they no longer have a job role within that team, when their is known vacancies for that job role they have been doing.  Then they have to wait to hear if they have a job to go to, when other staff have been brought in their place and they then have no job and they end up leaving for another job role out of this trust which is a huge loss of expertise and fantastic skilled nurses.
I generally enjoy my job, however some colleague's behaviours are very unprofessional and I do wonder if this is tackled my higher management.

I get annoyed with being pressurised by my organisation to fill in this questionnaire every time it is produced.  Just so they can get their % up!  I see little point in completing it.  No changes are produced as a result of it.  No feedback is received.  It feels a waste of time.  And why does it matter if my sex is the same as my birth sex.  There are 2 genders male and female.  The correct grammar is he or she.  So why all these ridiculous questions?  All the NHS management side cares about is ticking boxes, passing an inspection etc.  Nobody shows much interest in the day to day issue of staff numbers between these inspections.  Report unsafe staffing - we do!  Nothing changes.  Its been like this for years.  I have worked in the NHS for [timeframe removed].

I have absolute faith in my manager.  However, the Trust as a whole is money orientated and the feel is that they really don't care about staff.  I'd have my team look after my family but not the organisation.  We don't get breaks so there's an hour that I don't get paid for and because of that I'm working for an hour every shift.  There is no organisation between MDT's.  Trying to get hold of someone outside of our team is impossible and this impacts patients.  Moral is horrendous.  I'm not sure what higher management are getting paid to do, which is handsome, and what they're actually doing.  I don't think people outside of each team realise how others are struggling.  We are angry fed up and working for free.  Things are at breaking point.

I have always found Mersey Care to be an open, engaging and supporting organisation.  I have enjoyed the support of excellent line managers.  This was a significant contrast to the previous NHS Organisation I worked for.

I have been a ward manager for [number removed] years and I can honestly say I've never known things to be so bad.  This trust does not prioritise it's patients or it's frontline staff.  It's priority is recruiting more and more senior managers who bombard frontline staff with constant demands to complete tick box exercises.  As a manager, I've always been responsible for the recruitment of staff to my area, however, this part of my job description has been taken away.  My trust now opts for recruitment days, where individuals are interviewed by non clinical managers, which in my experience, doesn't work.  The recruitment process is taking far too long, up to 5 months, leaving wards dangerously short.  As a manager, I'm constantly pulled into the numbers, meaning I don't get enough time to complete the monthly returns.  Despite this, I am still bombarded by senior managers to submit these on time and I'm scrutinised when something is incomplete.  This has become the norm and this is not okay!  We are encouraged to put on a performance during QRV's in order to receive a good rating, which is not an accurate picture of any wards within my area.  If issues where raised during QRV, this would come back on the ward manager, further increasing their work load.  The changes need to made at the top.  We are struggling to recruit qualified nurses and yet we have a group of senior managers with PIN numbers, who, in my opinion should be filling those gaps.  It's a constant battle when it comes to staffing.  There is no consideration for anyone's experience and no one wants to address the real concerns.  Senior managers focus seems to be on introducing audit after audit as a way of micromanaging it's staff.  Promotion if for staff who are more mouldable and less able, whilst those who want to make positive changes are overlooked because they have challenged the system.  I have worked for the NHS for [number removed] years and I've never felt as fed up as I do now.

I have been in this current role since [date removed] so some of the answers related to my previous position so is this looking at the full overall score as my role now!  [timeframe removed] community nursing.  [date removed] - present bladder and bowel role.

I have been shocked to the core with the standard of care and staffing in my place of work.  The reliance on agency staff who bring absolutely nothing to the environment is staggering.  The ward feels like a pressure cooker about to explode and I just hope and pray I am not in duty when it does.  Most shifts are an accident waiting to happen.  Permanent staff are run ragged to ensure the basics are provided - there is no scope to provide anything other than basic care.

I have been working in Mersey Care for a few years now and it is a trust I am proud to work for.  Compared to the previous trust I worked for, the senior leadership is very visual and supports all staff.  I will never leave Mersey Care as I feel valued, and would encourage people to work for this organisation.

I have felt very pressured and stressed within this organisation, I would actively encourage friends or family not to work for this organisation, listening to senior management stating "its a service requirement".

I have flexible working now, but this was difficult to get but now it is in place it works well.  Sometimes get one bad person who can spoil a team, and this is not addressed.  I have allocated work and meet my targets, but I know others in the tam do not as we have a weekly meeting and things are shred.  To me this should be private as they might feel bad, and I feel I'm doing more than others.  Best thing is my team and enjoy spending time in work due to them, too much working from home takes this away.

I have found that this 12 months I have been constantly frustrated by poor immediate management.  Teams can be very good and the trust can have excellent objectives but without good management these things mean very little.  I have worked in the NHS for many years and this is the first year that I can honestly say that I feel burnt out and dislike my job directly as a result of poor management.  In addition to this I feel that many of the teams that I work with are reaching crisis point due to the lack of staffing.  Somehow no matter how low the numbers of staff available, the expectations that we complete the level of work identified as appropriate when fully staffed never changes or lessens.

I have found the role very stressful due to low staff and sickness, I have requested to reduce my hours for my own heath, and although this has not been refused so that I can make my choice to leave they have promised it for the last twelve months and it has never happened.
I have found working for Merseycare frustrating at times due to the lack of communication, systems get change i. e. Oh referrals and you are not informed you have to find out for yourself.

I have no faith that this organisation with take care of the physical and mental health needs of staff when it comes to managing case loads.  This has historically resulted in staff leaving and going off sick.  The manager is extremely supportive of people attending courses to progress in their career and for me this outweighs my current thoughts to leave.  Admittedly I love my role, the families I work with, and my team.  If it wasn't for them and access to training courses I feel I would have considered leaving more seriously that I currently do.
I have no intention of leaving Mersey Care during my career.  I feel Mersey Care is a good Trust to work for, particularly having experienced first hand/feedback from other NHS organisations.

I have over [timeframe removed] service in the NHS and have always been considered hard working and conscientious with a strong work ethic.  The feedback I receive from my colleagues /team I manage is that I am caring, an excellent role model/leader and fair.  I do not feel I get the same feedback and feeling of value from some members of senior leadership team who manage me (in the last [timeframe removed] in particular) due to a very recent change in line management.  The Trust values are not part of their culture, I do not feel valued or respected for my work, contribution, views or ideas, and constructive challenge is not welcomed.  Work life balance is not recognised by them or implemented.  Despite policies and initiatives by the trust to take this forward (to look after and create commitment and loyalty from its workforce) there is no flexibility within our Division.  Therefore Work life Balance feels empty rhetoric in my circumstances.  The NHS People Plan which I believe I was delivering on within the team I manage, is now being reversed by Senior Leadership.  This appears extremely short sighted within a challenging labour market with no clear evidence or rationale for this directive.  Patient experience is set aside for a narrow focus on targets i.e. number of patient contacts rather than quality of patient contacts.  I despair of the direction we are being taken in and sadly believe this will be to the detriment of the staff employed and the patients we serve.  I would have greater faith in the Trust staff survey if there was some sort of monitoring across teams of how for example work life balance policies and initiatives are implement at locality level.  This would in some way ensure that the trust values and respective policies (Restorative Just and Learning Culture, CARES Values, Flexible Working, Hybrid Working) are a reality and rolled out across teams in the spirit in which they are written, meant to be interpreted and applied; rather than ignored by individual managers who have zero interest the wellbeing of staff and the consequential knock on effect on quality of service delivery.

I have recently changed teams.  My new team is better managed and I feel supported.  My previous team had several toxic nurses who were unprofessional and nothing was dealt with despite numerous complaints.  I do not feel Mersey care take concerns from staff seriously.

I have recently moved back down my bands from management to clinical as found management to be to many repetitive processes which are not enjoyable, far too many systems to use making it highly frustrating, HR/Occ Health/performance/BI hub/IMP etc.  Due to demands on managers when there are SLT changes and there are vacancies at band 8a it is difficult as operational tasks are passed down to team managers, this is as well as trying to manage a successful service and keep a team happy.  There is a real lack of funding in mental health and we are running with high acuity and increased community risks and trying to juggles these pressures as team managers with limited resource.  Lack of beds is a real issue and we are managing extremely complex patients in the community which should not be the case when they need admission, escalating it to patient flow does not achieve a positive outcome often.  For this reason I have made the decision to return to clinical work for less pressure as I found the uplift in wage a nominal fee for the expectations.
I have retired and returned in a different role so work only 2 days a week now.  My answers would have been very different if I completed this when I was working full time a few years ago.

I have retired and returned to part time working and have a great work life balance.  I get a great deal of job satisfaction from what I do, work in a fantastic team and have opportunities to develop.

I have seen there has been a drive for the recruitment of staff and this will, in time, help relieve some of the pressure felt by the staff on the shop floor.  My concern is the retention of keeping the experience staff that are already employed by the Trust.  Since staring within the UTCs/WICs I have spoken to many staff across the different sites and there is an undercurrent of unhappiness in the role, the staffing and the pressures faced daily, with many looking for new roles.  This feeds into my comment around not feeling valued as employees as it does not feel like wellbeing is being supported as expected in the Trust strategy.  Recruitment is no quick or easy task but I feel it is important to understand the effect this is having on the staff on the shop floor, feeling unmotivated, tired and under valued.

I have witnessed the trust mistreat colleagues and go against there own claimed values.  I have witnessed bullying from senior colleagues in the trust, for example a full time employed consultant was supported against a locum even though the full time was in the wrong and totally unprofessional in their conduct.  Trust values seem to only be appropriate when the trust feels it's in their interest.

I have worked for the NHS for many years and had minimal sick leave.  I have had one day sick leave for a migraine within 4 years (including working through COVID on the wards).  I feel that the Merseycare sickness policy is awful.  I had to ring a number and justify why I was off sick, then wait for a return call from higher management that prevented me from resting.  Further to this I had text messages and constant reminders as to when I would be returning (from the sickness team).  I think this is harassment and unjustified.  If I am off with a migraine then I should be left alone to sleep.  I returned to work the following day not feeling great.  This is not a reflection on my immediate managers but on the Mersey Care sickness policy.

I have worked for the organisation for many years and really enjoyed my career.  The only thing I would say is that we need to be more negotiable in pay I have experienced promotions that have led to more pay due to enhanced hrs etc but when you go up the banding and most jobs are 9-5 and you lose pay this with current cost of living causes stressors, I am a Band 8a senior clinical nurse with lots of experience to offer clinical teams within the MHCD and to improve Patient care/safety but due to financial commitments I have taken a band 7 post with HSS with R&R payment and enhanced hrs so dropped a band and will be financially better off, I believe that when we complete Job exit interviews could this be discussed and what are the best ways to support retention as loved my band 8 a job but had no choice really to take the Band 7.

I have worked for this organisation for many years and in this time I have seen a deterioration in the way that staff are treated.  Unfortunately work related stress has increased to record highs and this needs looking into.  The high turnover of staff, due to staff leaving the trust is unprecedented and this is becoming an unsafe work environment for staff and patients alike due to staffing issues and unrealistic workloads.

I have worked for this organisation since qualifying many years ago and have been supported throughout my career to return to university on three occasions to continuously further my professional and academic knowledge, all whilst bringing up a young family.  I have extremely understanding and supportive managers who will always strive to do the best for us.

I have worked in the same department for the last [timeframe removed].  I do so because I have a fantastic direct line manager.  They put their staff first yet ensures our department maintains the highest standards.  I would be unlikely to stay in the Trust if they were to leave as I feel they give our department its identity and direction.

I have worked under different senior leadership teams within the organisation and I would like to say that some are far superior in terms of displaying of trust values and the culture they promote and how they interact and listen to their staff.
I highly appreciate Mersey care and never like to leave here but my [family member removed] is sick and can not bring them to UK because they need my support and it makes me so sad.

I just hate my job now after[timeframe removed] of working for the NHS as a nurse.  My current Trust is the worst I have ever worked for they do not care about the staff at all do not listen which obviously impacts the patients in our care.
I like my job I just feel pressure sometimes as we have a lot to do in a short amount of time.  My manager is brilliant.

I love my job and find it challenging and rewarding every day.  I have excellent managers and work colleagues, working for a Respiratory Team that provides excellent care and for which I am proud to work with.

I often do well above my contracted hours for no extra pay due to pressures of my caseload.  I often feel stressed and mentally exhausted and this affects my personal life.  My work life balance needs to be reviewed.  It is the nature of the job and I accept it is the way I work, but I feel to do my job the way I want to - I have too many pressures.  This is the same of a lot of the other nurses in our team - that the job is undoable and can be extremely stressful.  I also want to partially retire and reduce my hours- to be able to claim my pension that I should have claimed in [date removed] as I have Special class status and can claim my pension at [age removed].  ([age and date removed]).  I don't want to just retire and return as I feel that I need to reduce my work hours to be able to improve my work/life balance.  I enjoy my role and feel well supported, I don't really want to leave my role but I want to take my pension and feel like I can't currently in my current role.  I understand that it is a full time job- My manager and Team leader have considered my request but currently not able to reduce my hours.
I plan to retire in soon.

I really enjoy my job I feel the work we complete within out team does support families within the community to improve their health and wellbeing.  I work within a special team who are supportive and look out for one another we work with students and always make sure they are included in out team and we have an excellent team leader who is always ready to listen.  The only part of my job I do not like is the pay as with the cost of living it is not enough to live a decent life outside of work, nursing is a stressful job therefore it is important to have a life outside of work were you can relax and enjoy your time off.
I really enjoy my role and working for the team I am in.
I really enjoy working for Mersey Care and would recommend my organisation to others.
I really enjoy working in Mersey Care - feel that I am valued and I am lucky to have a supportive leadership team.
I relocated to Liverpool post qualification.  I was excited to start my new role.  However, I have not had a good experience at all and I am looking to leave everyday.

I sent an email regarding starting up a men's health clinic and had nothing back, also my levels of anxiety has increased due to a suspension of my salary sacrifice due to a UC claim.  I sorted the issue out within a month and the salary sacrifice still has not been sorted out for[timeframe removed] and I feel that I am accruing debt and possibly will lose the car which I require for work.

I support my organisation, and always work towards the trust values.  They invest in staff because they want their staff to stay working with the trust so they them selves can become one of the best leading organisations.

I think more awareness need to be done regarding ethnicity as some staff are experiencing micro aggression in place of work and being discriminated at because of their accent an personality including myself.  We all contribute to the development of the organisation so there should not be discrimination in place of work regarding how anyone is perceived.
I used to work for another trust and would never go back.  This has been the best trust to work for.  Super proud to represent Mersey Care.
I wanted to add this.  I am hoping to leave the NHS in order to secure a visa to work' live in [location removed].  This is my plan in the future.
I wish staffing level can be improve.
I work for Knowsley PCN network and thoroughly enjoy my job.  I feel I am making a positive impact on patients in primary care and feel hugely supported by my team.
I work for the ICRAS team, Urgent Care and I have always felt valued in my role, and always feels supported by my managers.

I work in an amazing team with amazing colleagues who are incredible advocates for our vulnerable service user group.  My manager has supported my professional and personal wellbeing/life to make my experience at work as easy as possible and I am grateful for all of there support.  Recently, pressures on the team have been extensive and staffing has been short.  We have a new senior leadership team and it has felt like we have been under unfair and unnecessary scrutiny.  There is a lot of pressure in relation to admin work completed by professionals (which is extensive and requests have been made for this to be reviewed).  It feels like there's a lot of micromanagement specifically for our team only and staff feel undervalued and under appreciated resulting in long standing members of staff wanting to leave.  Concerns have been escalated over and over to no avail from senior leadership.  I can't stress enough how good this team is and the feedback we receive from patient, families and services is always good-excellent.  At the same time as feeling scrutiny from senior leadership, the trust has enforced sensors under desks which adds to the pressure and feeling under scrutiny.  Change needs to happen as staff retention is poor and staff wellbeing is at risk.
I work with an excellent team, who are supportive of each other and the patients we see.  There is always an opportunity for formal/informal supervision.

I work within the walk in centres.  Staffing is very poor.  As a band 7 nurse I very rarely get a break due to poor staffing.  Very busy departments.  Feel like management don't listen to our concerns.  We are budgeted for a certain amount of staff yet never have that number of staff on duty.  We are struggling and not looking forward to the upcoming winter pressures.
I worked for the trust for over 10 years then left and have returned recently I think Merseycare supports staff well.
I would like there to be more clinical (rather than managerial) band 7 roles available within health visiting/children's services.

I would like to raise my concerns in regards to the constant mobilisation of nursing staff which can be very stressful due to working under immediate pressure, taking charge and in new surroundings without sometimes any background knowledge of the ward/unit.  I would welcome an adequate risk assessment and review of any adverse effects this has on staff in general.  I would also welcome that my organisation starts to utilise my Employee-On-Line to indicate if and when I have been mobilised and which ward it is.  Merseycare used to comply with this until in recent times where they reverted back to the old practices of keeping nursing staff in the dark where they are working until the very last minute.  Additionally as an environmental issue, I would welcome extra charging points for EV cars for the High Secure Services where there are currently only 2 charging points (4 connectors).  This is very limited and impacts on many staff being able to effectively charge their cars.  Thank you.

I would like to see promotion based on experience, education rather than job adverts being changed to allow people to progress who haven't got the relevant education.  A MSC isn't the same as [timeframe removed] doing a job, education is education and experience is experience.

I would personally like to see a shift to focus on staff health and wellbeing as much as obtaining/exceeding KPI's etc.  I believe the more you prioritise your staff and ensure they are appropriately supported the research indicates the efficiency increases within the workforce, so this should definitely be considered.  I would also like the organisation to completely consider any changes they make to services and have a comprehensive plan before they are implemented.  As there have been changes rolled out recently which have not been clear and it has left staff like myself on the front line confused in terms of what we should be completing in our respective roles (Re: changing from CPA/Non-CPA to CMHF in the community) and questioning management who are equally confused as there has been a lack of clarity from higher up positions.  This should definitely be reviewed to ensure that we are operating efficiently as a service.
I would really like a desk of my own.  Hot desking does not work.
I would recommend Mersey Care a place to work.
I would still like the flexibility of WFH.

I would value the organisation joining council run facilities to reduce price of active lifestyle gym memberships / any gym facilities - there is no tangible health offer that encourages / supports staff to lead a healthy active lifestyle.  4 day working would be life changing for all.
I'm proud to be working for the NHS again despite the challenges that come with it.

I'm very lucky in my current role as I have very supportive managers, great colleagues and I really enjoy what I do.  Working in the NHS for so long I haven't always enjoyed these things, and I feel Mersey Care are a very good Trust to work for based on my personal experience.

If there was enough staff then the job would be easier to manage, and if the general hospitals took responsibility to take 136 patients when the suites are full this would also make my job more manageable.

In my role we are expected to do things that cause back ache i.e. bandaging limbs of very obese patients and having to take the weight/load of each leg.  This causes pain to back /neck.  Also we don't have access to e roster and we are only only given 3 weeks off duty in advance.  This goes against the advice given by NHS England.  It is very difficult to organise social life.

In terms of mileage claim, staff like myself and others find it so difficult to input the mileages to make claims hence leaving myself and others out without being refunded with amounts of mileages driven for.  I feel this should automatically be carried out by a special staff for this otherwise staff should be refunded with certain amounts of money that gets unclaimed.  It would be of immense relieve to see the organisation looking into these as many of us - staff feel hurt regarding this despite much efforts put into reaching patients and offering them much relieve.
