In the past I have always felt included in changes proposed within my area of work however over the past 2 to 3 years this has significantly changed with little engagement from the senior leadership team in change management, I along with my team colleagues regularly comment on how we now feel "done to" rather than "done with".  This is causing a lot of unrest and anxiety to and resulting in staff looking for other jobs outside of this organisation.  It appears Mersey care are more interested in ticking boxes rather than individuals health and well being, for example on a personal level following S&A period after a very difficult [details removed] no return to work was conducted, however stage 1 of the S&A policy was invoked.  A more recent S&A episode resulted in a meeting for PDR scheduled before a return to work interview, whilst this may seem trivial to some as a previous seasoned operational manager I would say priorities appear to be somewhat questionable and may lead to feeling of the organisational priorities coming well before their employees well being.  Staff are moved to other areas without discussions leading to unrest and staff welfare only taken in to consideration once staff sides support has been requested.

Inexperienced managers are making mistakes in the management of difficult personalities.  Views of staff ignored.  No changes after awaydays.  Some staff have stopped speaking out as it makes no difference.

International black nurses are not supported in the wards, they are not given the opportunity to learn and develop in their role as nurses in a foreign country, they are used to patch up the shortage of HCAS.  In order to learn and have competencies signed off, it's almost as if they need to be [word unreadable] about it but there is always time to teach and equip newly qualified UK trained white nurses.  This is hugely discouraging because black internationally recruited nurses are professionals with years of experience but are treated like they are not qualified or like they don't belong.  They are not given a chance to carry out proper qualified nurse duties, they are utilised as HCAS but in times of high pressure they are reminded that they are qualified.  Which is unfair, makes them look incompetent and is de-skilling to a certain extent.  During the recruitment process, the Trust is not honest about the unit they are recruiting until the the international nurses arrive in the UK, this does not give the prospective nurse a chance to decide whether they do want to work in a certain unit or not.

It is disappointing that we are now being told that wearing uniform is mandatory - when working with children and young people in the community, a uniform can often be a barrier and can lead to dis-engagement.  It is a shame that engagement is lessening due to this barrier as some children and young people will refuse to be seen in college/school if in our uniform.
It is nice that my employers want to hear feedback from staff and will act on feedback received.  I am well supported in my role.
It is upsetting to be accused of 'complaining', when all you did was ask for help on a busy virtual ward.  It is unsafe practice, and I am sick of raising these issues only to be ignored.
It would be better if each service helps each other rather than passing the buck.

It would be nice if years of service are recognised within the NHS and celebrated to help make the staff feel more valued.  Brother in law works for the fire Brigade and they put a big emphasis on this i.e. celebration for 20 years service, 30 years service etc.
It would help to have consistency in staff.  People do not stay and lots of sickness within the team.  Leadership needs to be looked at in my opinion.
Its my opinion Mersey care trust lacks focus on quality of care provided to our service users.

Job has become too KPI orientated not being family orientated.  I have had too work with [details removed] ([condition removed]) when I am [condition removed] because I was warned I would go to the next sickness stage!  I could hardly walk or get a shoe on.  I was racially abused by a client for being [ethnicity removed] when delivering health promotion.  I reported it to my manager who did not take it seriously because "[ethnicity removed] people" cant be racially abused!  .  I have a serious [condition removed] affecting my [details removed], well known to the team.  A team building day was made for a [details removed] automatically excluding me from the team building day!
Just over a year left to work.
Just to look into staffing issues as it really affects our performance and affects our mental health.

l have been health visitor for many years and I love my job.  I have always looked forward to going to work, and felt I made a difference.  However, in the past few years working to targets has really deteriorated the care and continuity that we give to our clients.  To meet these targets we often have to sacrifice good quality care.  For example, a health visitor will build up relationship with a mother over several visits then because of a 'target' further visits with that mother will be rescheduled with other staff members if the health visitor is on holiday/day off.  This can often affect the professionals/patients relationship for example, if a person was starting to feel comfortable with her health visitor she may be ready to disclose domestic violence or mental health issues but this bond can then so easily broken by a manager sending someone else to cover a non urgent visit just to meet a target.  Another example of this could be a routine birth visit which are targets say we must complete within 10 to 14 days-what is wrong with the health visitor the family now doing the birth visit on day 15 rather than somebody new having to do it within the 5 day time scale?  The midwife is still attending, it is non urgent.  Do the commissioners not want quality care?  .  My [family member removed] recently had a baby and their health visitor who they previously knew from antenatal contacts could not visit within the time scale before a weekend so they had to arrange for somebody else to visit.  The day my [family member removed] was given for this visit was the same day that they had the midwife visiting, they were attending the feeding clinic, and they were taking the baby for a hearing test.  My [family member removed] requested the visit be another day as they had so much on but the health visitor could not offer them another day and just changed their time to visit later.  I gathered that they were under pressure to visit before the weekend however doing so the visit was a complete waste of time as my [family member removed] and the baby were so tired from a busy day and they actually retained nil information from that visit.  Whereas a visit two days later would have been so much more beneficial.  I do not understand this and how this is supposed to benefit our service users.  I feel so sad that our profession is deteriorating so much and even sadder that it is affecting my own family.

Lack of communication about changes and decisions being made about services without actively involving the staff who work in the service or run the service is a frequent frustrations.  Feels like decisions are made without our input.

Lack of engagement and involvement from managers regarding clinical staff (senior and non senior) involvement in pilots, new ways of working, development of team and service.  Staff request to be involved, requests ignored.  Feedback is not listened to or acted up on.  Lack of opportunities for staff involvement in service development, opportunity to raise concerns regarding what works well, what does not work so well, impact on patient care, and lack of action.  Staff do not feel listened to.
Leadership are very supportive in all aspects of work.  Team is positive and proactive.  Leadership have been VERY supportive to me personally regarding on-going social stressors at home.

Like other organisations, the trust needs to improve in areas, but they do strive to achieve this, it is important that they continue to listen to staff and service users.  The new executive will be a breath of fresh air.

Limited opportunities for career progression/to complete university courses and further enhance knowledge and skills due to funding issues and short staffing.  Trust not pro active in supporting staff development.  Staffing levels in district nursing extremely poor.  Too much money spent in mental health services which is impacting on community services.  District nurses not appreciated enough for the imperative role they play in the community caring for end of life patients and witnessing emotional events/traumas for loved ones of families.  Mental health services highly praised within the organisation and not enough praise on community services in particular district nursing.  Not enough money put into district nursing services.

Line managers are promoted far too quickly, they have limited experience of the role let alone management, they are pulled into a role then fail to communicate with the teams, the hierarchy response system means staff have to raise issues with team leaders first so managers are never seen and unsure they ever hear about issues on the shop floor for staff.  Constant firefighting, no plans, too many priorities' then when you complain you are seen as trouble.
Lots of staff are leaving or on the sick.  Nobody asks why.
Loved the change from working at Alder Hey - fantastic organisation and [name removed] is just wonderful!

Main issue with my job is, we are always short staffed and expectations on DNs workload is increasing.  There is no maximum to the amount of patients referred to us so caseloads are getting bigger without extra staff to support.  Caseload in our area is getting bigger and more complex.  When there is a day were workload appears manageable staff are moved to help other teams therefore there is rarely an opportunity to catch up with non direct patient care.
Mersey Care are the worst organisation I have worked for, they have staff working on unsafe staffing levels every day.  Staff are burnt out and ready to leave.  Mersey Care can't retain their staff.

Mersey Care has undone a lot of the good work and best practices of North West Boroughs HCT after the take over.  A lot has gone down hill, particularly in regards to timely care.  I have never seen so many people leave our team, and subsequent fast turnover of staff since we became part of Merseycare.
Mersey Care is all about mental health all the other departments i.e. childrens.  Adults are not thought about or given as much consideration.
Mersey care need to concentrate on the services they currently provide and ensure they are working 100% before acquiring more companies as staff and patients are starting to suffer.

Mersey Care needs a better approach to work and home life balance not having fear at requesting flexible working hours with the threat you could be moved just because it fits the trusts' requirements of where another person is needed with in the trust, there should be more colleagues being able to do long days not just one person in a team that has the long days.  Waiting tools need to be looked at in regards to writing up/paper work needs to be added on to the waiting as writing up, care plans, schedules take time.

Mersey Care needs to concentrate on its existing portfolio rather than taking new services on - like care homes, which they cant staff and then disrupt established teams to poach staff to work in them - often out of their scope of practice.
Merseycare is a good fair organisation to work for and I have been lucky enough to enjoy my time working in all the roles I have had.

Merseycare is a great organisation to work for.  It is very supportive of its staff and does its best to learn from any mistakes.  It does ask quite a bit at times and this can be quite stressful.  All in all a good employer.
Merseycare NHS foundation trust is one of the best organisations to work for.
Micro managing can be counter productive as increases stress for staff and thus increases sickness.
More recognition, more support, more staff, better wages.

MSC demand a high standard which if good.  However the expectations of how long task / plans etc take to implement is unrealistic.  This leaves staff running about in their own time regularly, feeling as it they are not effective enough Mandatory training rains in without warning because MSC wants something doing, having moved most to online putting the onus and all the pressure on staff for not completing, so often staff do in their family time.  While not considering the time these modules take to complete and the impact of poorly designed packages and failing IT server issues on clinical time / pressures.  Then MSC harp on about work life balance.  It bit more consideration and clear honest look at 1 element MT and how long this takes to complete may ensure senior managers plan more efficiently.

Mt team and manager are very supportive and fair and a pleasure to work with.  I had an incident with some offensive and sexually inappropriate behaviour from a patient and my manager supported me so well however I felt the way the issue was dealt with by Mersey Care was not sufficient or supportive.  I was unable to access health and wellbeing services in an appropriate way that would fit round my job role and have had no further input from them which was disappointing.

My answers are based on previous jobs within the trust as well as bank nurse roles I have undertaken over the past 2 years.  Overall I'm happy in my job/team, we have some wonderful staff in the NHS!  Unfortunately, there is increased micro managing of staff/less flexibility (in some teams) lately.  This is causing staff to feel unhappy in their jobs and results in high turn over of staffing.  This, in turn, effects staff morale and ultimately impacts patient care.

My comment is to reduce number of managers on certain meetings and delegate appropriate according to banding rather than all bands attending all meetings and increase working time for all to allow a better work life balance and sharing of appropriate work loads and allowing teams to have some responsibility and autonomy in their own areas.

My current manager [name removed] in mid Mersey first response is the best manager I have worked for in many years, so supportive and approachable, they are a credit to Mersey care and that is such a breath of fresh air after leaving a team where the managers are unapproachable and bully the staff -it was raised with senior leadership team but I do not believe they see the accurate picture.

My direct line manager is amazing and is one of the only reasons I remain in my current role.  They are beyond supportive and I feel they are an excellent manager.  I have previously been burnt out and had time off for this.  I did not feel ready to come back, but had no choice but to do so.  My manager has been as supportive as possible, but I feel I am already reaching burnout again.  We are understaffed, underpaid and underappreciated.  We are put in situations we shouldn't have to go into and we are expected to go into palliative dying patients and have no support following deaths, we are just expected to carry on and go to the next patient.  There is no facility to have an outlet for issues we are experiencing at work in terms of having monthly meetings with a registered mental health professional to debrief on the situations we deal with.  The counselling provided through occupational health is not beneficial and I found it did not help me at all.  In our team we have a multitude of issues with regards to parking, with staff regularly having to pay out to park to see patients and/or getting fines.  This increases overall negativity and stress.  I feel stressed at work, but we don't receive enough pay for what we do and more and more expectations are put on us.  I have a lease vehicle and feel it is actually an extortionate amount for a vehicle.  I feel we are not considered as community staff who have to travel for work so our mileage has to be higher, in comparison to colleagues who just commute to and from work.  I like my team, but I live 15 miles away from base and the amount spent on petrol is just increasing.  The morale overall is shocking, we cannot retain staff and the boundaries different teams cover need to be established as it is stressful trying to handover out of area patients, with other teams refusing even though they have patients on their caseloads in that area.

My immediate team are incredible, and my line managers are wonderful.  They have been really supportive of me both professionally and personally, and created a psychologically safe space for me to speak about challenges and mental health problems and their causes.  However, what the trust does, and what it says it does as a whole are not compatible, and the chasm is growing.  There have been a number of times when I have tried to raise issues, and been told that's not your fight or it's only one person, it's only one team despite the fact it may be symptomatic of wider problems in the trust.  Many people have told me that they do not feel safe speaking up, because they believe they will be targeted on the back of it, do not believe in safety or anonymity through surveys, F2SU, etc.  What this trust could be is absolutely phenomenal, but there are certain areas and levels of management who are unwilling or unable to accept feedback and/or change.  There is apathy in large parts of middle management, and this risks spreading like rot.  It needs to change so that we can all reach our potential as our staff and patients deserve.

My immediate team is great and very supportive, however senior management and the trust are less supportive and we feel like just a number to cover shifts rather than actual people.  We feel listened to but rarely do we feel we have had our concerns acted upon and we're just listened to to placate us.  I would recommend to work with my team and for family to come to where I work however I would not recommend the organisation as an employer.  I have had external training opportunities declined numerous times and feel like there isn't the support for growth in my job in terms of learning and training to enable me to be confident in my knowledge to stay up to date on developments and guidelines for my role.

My job has improved over the past 6 months, I feel this is due to new manager who is very supportive.  The survey does not capture change in management that can either have a negative or effect on our job roles.  If new manager was not in post my answers would have been very different and less positive.  I would suggest questions regarding new management to capture this information.

My organisation is monopolising the NW as such the options of where t work are incredibly limited for a large area.  Mersey care are too big and they are unable to staff there sites.  Its unsafe and a terrible trust to work for, but choices of joining any neighbouring trust is limited to GMMH or CWP- Not everyone drives or has transport.  Mersey care has made sure there are no other trusts around in reasonable distance so anyone in Merseyside and surrounding towns has to work for them if you want to work in the NHS.  This should be a crime to monopolise healthcare.

My organisation is strongly focused on reporting which takes a great deal of time away from direct patient care on a daily basis.  The organisation is introducing a vast amount of changes which is hard to keep up with.  Several Pilot schemes have been introduced within my team without any prior information being disseminated or a discussion from/with the senior managers.

My team is classed as being fully staffed but the team is running at full capacity day in and day out.  The majority of staff are still on EMIS catching up on documentation in the evenings as even the band [number removed] take a list of visits on a high weighting.  It feels unsafe and it feels as though patients are not getting thorough assessments.  Incidents happen and quite often the reason behind them is lack of staff but nothing seems to change.  The quota for staff seems dated but the complexity of patients and the diversity of the area that we cover is very demanding.  I feel that if staff and patients were valued then staffing levels would be increased to ensure that patient centred care could be carried out.
My team is extremely micromanaged which often makes the job challenging at times.
My team is very supportive and welcoming.
My work life balance has now improved since we have been able to work 2x long days per week, I found that my work life balance was negatively impacted when I was unable to work this shift pattern.
Need family friendly working hours.
Need more staff especially nurses, SALT and psychologists.

New systems and processes are being introduced with very limited inaccessible support and training due to work pressures disabilities etc.  The organisation makes tokenistic claims and statements but these are not being implemented effectively in the divisions.  KPI's and new system targets are making this job unbearable.  There has been a significant reduction in support for clinical staff to do their jobs.  New systems and processes keep being brought in by I'm and the trust to make trust exec lives easier but they are making staff's lives harder.  This trust is becoming a horrible environment to work in which is sad because I used to be really passionate about this trust but it feels like support services are more interested in looking good and winning awards for themselves than supporting clinical services.  The trust feels very separate and working in isolated ways.  Integration needs to be prioritised and supported more than it is.

No continuity with leadership teams, too many management changes, too frequently which impacts on creating a cohesive teams.  Listening to other Mersey care colleagues who verbalise lack of support, increased work loads and not listened to.
No further comments thanks.
No I am happy with working condition at the moment.
None at present, We have a new amazing Team leader at present.
None at this time.

Not enough in-patient beds.  Patients who should be detained in hospital not been admitted due to a lack of beds.  Patients discharged from hospital without a coherent care plan.  Very little psychology input.  Poor personal safety resources for community staff.  Very little support worker input.  Lack of clinical supervision for junior/newly qualified staff and new starters.

Nursing has changed so much over the years- I have been a nurse for many years and used to love my job.  Policies have taken over nurses using their own clinical judgement and I feel newly qualified staff have a fear of everything and feel they have to do absolutely everything in one consultation with their patients for fear of missing something and being penalised- I feel this will lead to burn out at a much earlier age as they all appear so stressed.

Ongoing issues with staffing which means extra work for us on top of our caseload.  Very frequent staff sickness which I feel sometimes some colleagues can take very last minute sickness and possibly abuse the sickness system- taking sickness when not necessary suitable to e.g. childcare issues whilst other members of staff go above and beyond to ensure this is not an issue and are more committed to their role.

Organisation is good, but organisation, like many, is a little too preoccupied with popular culture, WOKE idealism and positive discrimination for (and against) people.  I'd prefer to see a greater service-efficacy related focus.  Work should be about work really.

Organisation should be more adaptive to older workers and those that transitioned from the Whalley site and adapted to the changes and extended working hours due to driving each end of the shift making the day extra long.  Maybe offer earlier retirement to those staff members.

Over the last year the service has changed and working hours, this had a massive impact on myself due to childcare responsibility's.  I personally felt that although the trust said you don't have to change your hours, that was not genuine.  We were put through multiple meetings with senior managers and told we had to be flexible and questioned on family dynamics and why we couldn't comply with change of working hours.  Given dates from senior mangers when I would have an answer to flexible working request for them not to reply by this deadline and me have to chase them for a response.  I spent months thinking I would have to leave a job that I love as was unable to comply with change of hours as no childcare.  I feel as a mental health trust the communication has been terrible and not caring about there staff one bit.

Over the years the bar has been gradually lowered and the standard required at each band has drastically lowered resulting in large discrepancies in nursing care.  More priority is put on certificates gained rather than relevant and appropriate experience for the role.  This is evident at every level within the service.

Overall a good place to work, only negatives is financial.  The clinics have poor equipment and wage is poor.  Supportive colleagues and management.  Flexible working is a bonus.  Workload seems to be increasing, documentation wise, put more a strain on the job it's self.
People are burnt out, inpatient services are beyond crisis.  Soon there will be no staff.
Person centred employer to work within.
Please implement e health roster so we can see our shifts.  Please make longer timeslots for patients so we don't rush, ESP for new patients onboarding.
Poor leadership.  Not able to speak up.  Challenging.  Not listened to.  Very defensive.

Poor staffing in other areas impacts upon areas where staffing is good which in turn causes an impact to the services that have the appropriate staff to do their work.  The constant request for staff from our service affects the staffing in our team which increases stress, impacts our KPI, effects the service service provision and prevents us from achieving set targets.
Pt lists can be very busy were we do not have time for dinner very stressful.

Really enjoying my job on [name removed] ward my deputy manager [name removed] and manager [name removed] are always there when I need advice and guide me in the right direction.  They are very helpful and I love all the staff on the ward who have all helped and supported me in my new role.
Recently started - really enjoying.
Respect and civility is extremely poor at the moment amongst staff.
Responses to questions relating bullying/harassment relate specifically to behaviours of [number removed] members of staff within the team.

Same comments every year.  Nothing changes.  No progression unless friends with senior managers.  Interview's often unfair in that they already know who is getting the job.  Or, in my case; didn't get the job because a newly qualified middle aged person could remember policies off the top of their head, whereas, I and menopausal and struggled with that.  Never mind the fact that quoting policies is rote learning, rather than genuine understanding of policies and how to use them.  10 years of doing my job well has led to nothing but disappointment and now genuine apathy.

Same issues never resolved within the team.  Under staffed constantly, relying on bank and historically agency to stay afloat.  Very complex patients.  Escalating for more than 3 years the same concerns to operational managers yet we are still struggling with no sign of improvement.  Staff retention is poor due to working conditions in this trust within District Nursing.  Operational managers with no District Nursing background overseeing the service when they don't understand the sheer amount of extra work we put in.
School health is never listed as a choice in registered nurses.
Senior leadership team causing significant distress amongst team and encroaching on role of our direct and line managers in a negative way.
Senior leadership teams need looking into and investigating.  They are extremely corrupt and as a whole are absolute bully's.

Senior management make decisions about organisational changes which directly affect my role.  They will often invite us to meetings but job pressures occasionally prevent attendance.  Decisions are often made despite our objections.  Senior management continue to expand the organisation by taking on roles from other organisations, without adequately assessing the impact this will have on the staff whom are expected to undertake those roles.
Senior Managers are mainly concerned with meeting government targets, and 'ticking boxes' rather than genuine commitment to good quality patient care.

Since merging with the trust I feel that some of the good working practices have not been taken on board and dismissed for the changes to be the previous ways that Merseycare worked.  The systems that we work with mainly the sickness line is impersonal to use and I would prefer to speak to a human and my manager about my sickness.  I feel that my work life balance remains unmet.

SLT are absent from the 'front line' of our healthcare setting, and appear to have little understanding of what is involved in the day-to-day care of service users.  Many of the tasks dictated by SLT have minimal impact on improving care and the service we provide.  They are instead bureaucratic and 'box-ticking' duties, from which there is little to no feedback.  SLT do not appear to have their priorities aligned and will frequently communicate different concerns which apparently require addressing immediately.  It feels like the next day there is a new concern which then takes precedence.
Some times the organisation care about ticking boxes rather than their patients and staff.
Something to note on the qualifications I have dual qualifications, nursing and social worker.  Please add that on the survey drop box.  Thank you.

Staff mobilisation policy causes uncertainty, feeling that I am just a "number" who can be moved around.  Disregards the Trust's pledge for "perfect care" by moving nurses to work in other teams in roles that they don't know to fill staffing gaps.  Nurses cannot provide perfect care if they are moved to another team or area to work at short notice with minimal or no induction to the role.  I am always anxious about the possibility of being moved to work in a different team / area where I am expected to know my role without sufficient induction / shadowing / training etc.  I think this practice is unsafe and contributes to staff burn out, low morale and sickness.

Staff morale within the service has deteriorated since the trust was taken over by a much larger trust.  Management no longer interacts in a positive way and does not involve staff in decision making about the service.

Staffing issues need to be addressed as every shift is dangerous.  Upper management need a presence on the wards to help support staff and patients.  The only engagement with upper management with staff is if there is any is when errors have been made.  They are no incentives for staff to work extra shifts/hours.  Other organisations offer over time for any additional shifts epically if wards are understaffed, I have personally been offered over time once in my 4 years of working within the trust.  Safe staffing levels of a night are constantly dangerously low, I feel the trust should review the use of agency nurses especially to support regular permanent nurses of a night for extra support.

Staffing levels / Role Responsibilities.  - The staffing levels seriously in pact on the delivery of care we can provide and often the staffing levels fall short of the patient demand/need.  Staffing shortages on all levels from band 3/band 5/band 6.  -Furthermore when staffing level's are short, there is a lot of added pressure on the team which affects the safety of the wards.  Mistakes have happened as a result of high acuity and staffing shortfalls.  The general impression on the wards is that staff are burnt out, medication errors occur as a result of being stretched and pulled in multiple directions, high incidents of violence and aggression / self harm and no staff to adequately respond.  -The pay structure on the lower levels needs reviewing, people are caring and want to make a difference but with the job stress and exposer to serious incidents, I don't believe staff are paid to compensate the challenges of working in acute wards, there are easier jobs paying a fraction less then a band 3.  Whereby they wouldn't have to cope or deal with patient with high care demand needs.  -The daily operation of the wards requires two nurses to ensure all duties are requirements are met however more often then not your working alone.  - The list of jobs and paperwork needs reviewing as sometimes you are inputting the same data on serval documents.  OT.  Their is a lack of OT presence on the ward and this is often covered by NA's, meaningful activates are often a challenge to deliver as a result..  Breaks.  - On a regular basis there is only one nurse on shift and they do not get to take a break that they are legally entitled to, resulting in rest breaks being taken from wages when the nurse has not been able to leave the ward.  - Managers have stated to nurse that they should be more organised and bring in their own food, but are disregarding the fact that lunch breaks are a legal requirement and should not be imposing how the rest breaks are taken..  Work load pressure.  - The workload pressure and work demands/responsibilities when more often then not, working on limited numbers becomes impossible to achieve and things are getting missed, physical health checks are not always completed.  Environmental checks are missed, on days where service is affected staff are encouraged to complete RADAR'S to highlight this however this creates more of a work load on a day where you are already stretched thin..  Diet and fluids.  -The food for the patients is poor and I know this probably will not change but if I was a patient and that was the food I was to eat every day whilst struggling with my mental health, would probably make me feel awful, they are poorly made and look unappetizing, they are basically cheap microwave meals.  As we know a healthy balance diet and do you the world of good but a lot of people raise issues with the standard of the quality of the food and has been poor for years.  Values of the trust.  It's not all negative and from my experience the ward and team at Mersey care do appear to have a heart of gold and really appears to cares about their patients and this is displayed on all levels, from on the ground with NA's showing compassion and empathy for their patients, to going above and beyond, often doing extra work/additional hours, raising concerns to safeguard and protect their patients wellbeing.  There is a genuine warmth and care form a lot of people throughout the whole hospital.  I am proud to work for the NHS.  I just wouldn't be doing my job if not to raise the concerns that we often face as a team and the affects this has on the deliver/standard of care.  The ward dose have some strong management and they often are observed on the floor, implementing ideas on change and in addressing some of these challenges.  They are strong leaders who you can tell care about how the ward operates...  Bed demand.  Sometimes patients are admitted into the same bed where we have someone on leave resulting in a bed not being available to the initial person admitted into it.  Risk.  If the ward continues to run on low staffing, we are setting everybody up to fail.  There should be some serious consideration to the pine spine as I don't believe people are paid enough or have an incentive to stay on the wards when they can find work else where that pays similar or more with less work life stress.  The wards staffing has decreased dramatically since I first joined the trust and is reflected on all levels.  I do believe the trust has good progression routes and has a just and fair learning culture but retention appears to be a massive issue, for the reasons outlined in the above.
Staffing levels and the shortage of male nurses are reducing efficiency in the delivery of daily care in our services.
Staffing levels are a nationwide problem.  I do not feel enough is done to address this to make nursing more appealing.
Started employment in Mersey Care this year, love my team and have been well supported.
Stressed workforce, no staff, staff off sick.  Limited communication from senior managers, they change all the time and we don't know who they are especially from band 8 level upwards.

Teams within the locality need to provide more support to the district nursing service.  The demand on district nurses is like never before.  It is driving staff away and when raised to higher management no feedback is received.

The current management structure is not supportive of family needs.  There is an element of bullying behaviour within the management structure.  I have sourced a new job, and I cannot wait to leave the team.

The demands from the Trust for ever increasing documentation/templates to be completed weighs heavily on the staff, increasing time pressures with patient appointment slot's - as we are also finding patients are presenting with more complex needs and problems.  This needs addressing as it is the same issues that would make staff want to leave their job.  Practice Nurses are very task orientated and do not delve or deal with patient's additional problems during their consultations?

The internationally recruited nurses in the community care are rarely given opportunity to progress.  When applying for jobs you get feedbacks that you don't have experience but you have answered all questions well.  Some places even failed to send you feedback on an interview you did.
The job I do is non patient facing.  The actual work is fine, however I do really feel staff are not listened to.  There is little opportunity for career development.

The lack of support, the lack of insight into/understanding of team needs and the very poor communication from senior management team (8a/8b/8c and higher) is just appalling, disheartening and demoralising.  The core values of Mersey Care have been severely lacking in the past year by our senior management team.

The main cause for concern is the work load and unrealistic expectations in district nursing. I spend many hours completing my paperwork from home instead of spending time doing homework or spending time with my child.  The work load has caused many arguments in my personal.  Life with my partner as he feels I'm always working and never present.

The NHS as a whole is in crisis, as we are all very much aware.  After 30 plus years as a registered nurse I have seen my role and responsibilities increase dramatically from that for which I was trained, and without the motivating financial reward.  Commissioning means trusts are "chasing pots of gold" and spreading staff across services resulting in increased workloads and reductions in ability to provide outstanding care.  If a service costs for example £50, it can not be implemented for £20, and this is happening everywhere.  If it cant be done properly then we need to be allowed to do what we can do, to a high standard.  Sounds simple, because it kind of is?  .  Stop saying yes and learn to say No?

The only concern I have is that the trust has a uniform policy that is outdated.  We work with children who are in care and live with foster carers, family members or residential settings.  A lot of the children struggle with being in care and having nurses visiting them in uniform immediately draws attention to them that they don't want.  Some children specifically ask for us not to wear a uniform so we don't stand out when we are visiting the home but the trust is not listening to these children and ordering us to wear a uniform.  I bring this up every year and despite the voice of the child and you said we did campaigns this is not being listened to, we are happy to wear the uniform when required but we want to build meaningful relationships with the children and young people but this is difficult when we are seen to be not listening to their wishes.

The organisation appears to be pre-occupied with administration and governance as opposed to direct patient care.  Despite highlighting unsafe practices on a number of occasions, including via the Radar system, the organisation and in particular the department persists in continuing with initiatives and projects that are detrimental to patients and staff.  Staff are not supported clinically in an effective way to deliver these initiatives and this puts patients in direct danger.

The organisation focuses too much on 'business' rather than patient care.  There are inequalities in the organisation between divisions and overtime/additional staffing approval and this is not consistent across services.  I feel the size of the organisation has an impact on staff experience and staff feeling valued.  The trust continue to take on additional responsibilities when we are unable to safely staff the existing services.

The organisation has expanded greatly and the changes come in on a frequent basis making it hard to keep up with all the changes.  Service specs change and the information we get about them is limited until it can be seen that we are not meeting KPI's.  There are an increasing number of managers and line management changes regularly which does not give any continuity.  In this rapidly changing occupational climate it's not the nicest place to work.

The organisation has got it right.  However, line managers often don't; there are lots of microaggressions.  I'm above you, and you will do as you are told.  Line managers do not understand individuals disabilities or how these impact them.
The organisation has removed admin and not replaced it appropriately.  Trained nurses are expected to under take admin duties which is not a good resource of their time.
The organisation has supported my career development for many years.  I feel valued and will remain working within the trust until I retire, hopefully.

The organisation makes too many changes such as formularies, policies, procedures etc.  It is hard to learn what you need to do and then learn again just as you have got used to something.  Some changes do not make sense or work, for example taking off very effective dressings away from the formulary and replacing them with another dressing which is very cheap and does not work.  Training opportunities are lacking, not enough on or get cancelled.  Also some are too far and staff unable to get there due to short staffing.  5-8 working new to district nursing is not working as it should.  In the teams there are very few people that can cover this and it is putting strain on the ones that are doing these shift patterns.  Many of us are working from home after shifts or on days off as the workload is too much.  We are told that we should be able to complete this in the shift but there is no way due to capacity.  Job roles are not desirable such as band 6 district nursing role, too much pressure, lack of support etc.  Staffing levels are poor, certain teams are struggling more than others and the other teams are pulled to support, ongoing issue which never seems to be getting better.  Skill mixes within teams also poor, again putting pressure on people.  Some staff have been treated poorly by the organisation such as not keeping their promise regarding permanent jobs following secondment.  Being told to move to other teams as job not available in their own team.  Some staff not having support from the organisation following incidents such as bullying allegations etc.  Some staff have received more support than other people due to lack of competency/skills and they continue to work in the trust and still not up to scratch many months/years later.  The trust is not doing enough to appropriately deal with situations like this.

The support and encouragement from my manager is second to none, not only to myself, but my work colleagues also.  Unfortunately, I feel that the same cannot be said about the trust.  In my opinion, they are so focussed on the business and financial side of things, that the staff are often left misguided, misinformed (or without information) and appear to be wanting to take on the world.

The team is a wonderful team who are supportive of each other and give excellent peer support.  Our manager is very fair and understanding and will try to accommodate every member of staff and support them however they can.
The Trust needs to improve its culture and improve speaking up regarding current and previous complaints.

There appears to be a shortage of staff again and wards running well below numbers.  Staff who cancel bank shifts at last minute should be given consequence of this as it affects staffing.  Same for staff who don't turn up for shift should also be disciplined/consequence as at present it is the belief from staff that they can come and go when they chose and nothing happens to them but their actions imposes on the staff who are on shift and creates a difficult shift with low staffing numbers.
There are a lot of things within the Trust that need addressing.

There are far too many experienced nurses in band 8 + roles leaving very few experienced nurses in clinical environments, leaving no one to supervise newly qualified nurses, which has a direct impact on patient care.  There is little to retain band 5 nurses in band 5 posts, and often they are applying for promotions quickly to earn more money, but lack the experience to do this role well.
There are no clear objectives or goals to work towards promotion within my organisation.

There are so many things that do not work within the trust and are never addressed despite flagging things up on a regular basis.  Poor retention of staff, lack of resources and too much paper work makes the job extremely difficult and causes burn out and people to leave.

There are to many process and people involved in simple decision making in order to develop practice; this can be frustrating having to have multiple presentations/conversations outside of the agreed governance process to satisfy a senior colleagues need to be involved.  A governance process is clear and explained, and then there are 2 to 3 more that are introduced along the process with an insinuation a 'process' hasn't been followed.  This is demoralising for teams.  Alongside this Roles and responsibilities are not clear, as above you think you have went to the correct channels and people for approval/consultation and then a new role is introduced and/or the person in a role feels like they should have been involved and the process starts again.
There does not appear to be any opportunities for progression or flexible working when you are in a small team.  It is a constant top down approach with little or no consultation.

There have been many occasions where we have been extremely short staffed and under pressure due to staff sickness/leave, I often feel like we are not supported by the organisation at all during such occasions and staff have been left feeling stressed out and exhausted.

There is a serious issue with retention within the trust, especially of senior and experienced nurses'.  We are employing more junior staff without the necessary qualifications and support.  Management is very 'reactive'.  There is covert bullying - lots of micro aggressions.  Some HR employment policies are concerning - verbal feedback after interviews suggesting 'your sickness is awful'.  Suggestion that staff will be demoted if failing courses.  The job itself is lovely and the patient contact is great.  The managers and the trust are not.

There is currently lots of changes happening in the service I work.  This is exciting and should be a positive experience however the way in which these changes are managed and implemented leave staff feeling vulnerable and unsafe in practice.  The changes are often presented as they happen as in the day of or days before the change with minimal or vague information provided.  Change is often delivered via email and with a flow chart presented to follow.  Often no support or additional training is provided or access to relevant systems to enable to complete the new tasks expected.  The changes that are currently happening involve staff linking in with other services or working in new services with differing systems.  This has left staff feeling frustrate and inadequate.

There is no follow through - staff members on stage 3 - no resolution and the staff remain causing undue pressure on teams.  Whilst we all want to be supported and support staff, the fact that HR policies aren't followed through to the end, leaves managers frustrated, with high levels of long term and short term sickness, that places a significant strain on teams/services.  HR should follow their own policies and listen to the managers of that service.
There is not enough scope for progression which makes me feel deflated, I feel detached from my team due to caring responsibilities and also feel judged because of it.

There is some exceptional staff in the Secure Care Division.  It is good to see that sickness absence is being effectively managed now.  There are high numbers of staff attending training and development courses which impacts on ward staffing numbers.

There is still less number of BAME staff in senior nursing position despite this having been identified in the annual report by [name removed].  Therefore they appears to be unfair promotion as there is still lack of progress in this area.
There should be a box were you can explain the reason you have given a certain answer.
Things need to be fair.

This organisation has become too big to understand and know what is happening at ward levels and middle and upper management do not care: as long as their audits are done!  .  Ward nurses are leaving to 1 a day now, due to the amount of other peoples work they now have to complete in a day shift, like pharmacy jobs, social workers jobs etc. etc., spending at least half the day (6 to 8 hours) on the computers which never seem to work to complete forms and CPA, which leaves no time to interact with the patients.  The Trust has employed and turned nurses into computer robots that very little "nursing" is being done.  .  This Trust is promoting newly qualified nurses into band 6 with as little as 1 month after their preceptorship has finished or a ward manager with less than a years experience: with no clinical experience.  Due to this there is now a "revolving door" of the same patients coming in, spending months on the wards and then returning within weeks due to the inexperienced staff employed in the community with no clinical experience.

This survey is more like a snap shot rather than a reflection of overall working life.  Currently we are extremely short staffed so feeing lots of extra pressure but if I had completed this survey a few months ago I think I would have had a lot more positive responses.

Tier of middle management who appear to be "micro managing" well functioning teams.  Members of the team have been re-deployed to help other services which has often put the team under pressure with an impact to team and service.
Too many changes within the service at present.  People cant talk to each other/ friends/ splitting people up friendship groups etc.  Atmosphere at present is not nice.
Top down management approach is all too evident.

Toxic bullying risk averse trust that's driven by inexperienced senior.  Leadership with fanciful titles.  Talentless people with little knowledge of their roles who don't care about the well being of the people in their care being led by a High secure risk averse gang of tick box fools.

Unable to complete my masters through the trust as I do not have a [details removed] despite having a degree, CPD at level [number removed] and [timeframe removed] experience.  Unable to self fund as I am [details removed].

Unfortunately I still feel that there is a pervading ableist attitude that remains in the Trust.  Promotion prospects are probably diminished significantly and many staff fail to declare disabilities due to the concern over discriminatory practice from members of their management team.  Some staff are lucky and have understanding managers but this seems to be a lottery.  Despite the Trusts wish to present itself is none discriminatory this continues with inadequate facilities lack of opportunities of advancement and in some cases negative attitude from colleagues.  This not only harms the individual employee it affects and undermines the quality of service provision delivered by the Trust.  This impacts team dynamics reduces collaboration and stifles innovation.  We seem very good at collecting awards that often in the cold light of day we rarely deserve as the commitment to inclusivity seems lacking, resulting in employment tribunals and staff sickness due to bullying and micro aggressions.  Implementing mandatory training programs focused on disability awareness sensitivity and the importance of inclusiveness can help reshape the cultural attitudes prevalent among staff and management.
Unsafe staffing levels.  Lots of good staff leaving due to this.  Increased workload.  Lack of support from higher management.  Things being "brushed under the carpet".
Very stong clincal leadership across the Trust, keeping the patient centre to quality improvement.

Very unsettling for the team when staff members are continually being moved to help others for long periods of time.  Makes our service feel very undervalued and not appreciated.  Staff morale is at an all time low staff in the service are feeling very deflated over the way team members are moved.  There is a lot of anxiety in the team about potentially being asked to move at very short notice.
We are expected to attend far too many unnecessary meetings, this eats into precious clinical time and results in staff having to do extra work in their own time to catch up.

We are just too overworked.  Increased workload in community nursing but no extra resources put in.  It is too tiring and not enough work/life balance.  I work until late at night and on days off/annual leave days.

We have recently changed to the 8-8 model.  This has added additional stress within the team.  Having to change base at 5pm till 8pm there is also not enough staff so sometimes your the only 8-8 in your team.  It would work much better if there was always 2 staff members working until 8pm and you could remain at your own base where we feel much safer.  We was told we could split our 1 hour break to two 30 mins but we weren't able to take it at a certain time 8pm is far too late to eat tea if I was able to take my break at 5-5: 30 I'd be able to have my tea at a more reasonable time.

We need an ongoing, recruitment programme, incentives to retain staff.  More inhouse training to continue with knowledge/development.  Value staff that have been with organisation for long time.  Time to complete mandatory training,.  Cascading of important information.  Sometimes feels like 'Open University'.  Liaise with AED for opportunities with training which will benefit, WIC'S/AED'S.
We need more staff to cope with increasing demands to services both from increasing number of patients and increase in amount of paperwork needed to be completed.

We urgently need an overhaul of some of the documentation we use.  CPA care plans are a complete waste of time as patients are not interested in them and no health professionals ever refer to them.  All documentation should be simplified and more dynamic in line with our supposed transformation of services.

When band 6's are promoted to clinical lead/ band 7 there does not seem to be overview from other band 7's or band 8's on their way of working or communicating with the rest of the staff.  Meaning - some people let their perceived power go to their heads and treat the rest of the team like they are thick/stupid/useless.

When I experienced bullying/harassment it was in a previous position not the current team I am in now.  That is why I left my previous position.  The team leader witnessed it but nothing was done to prevent it from happening again.

When we were North West Boroughs NHS Trust the occupational health provision was excellent, however, under Mersey Care NHS Trust this is much diminished and limited access to services like we used to such as physio.

Whilst I have the opinion that staff working on the 'shop floor' are happy within their teams and work with pts.  And feel supported by their leadership teams, staff at Operational manager level in our place feel that our wings have been clipped since moving into Mersey Care.  There is a lack of autonomy which is extremely frustrating and slows progress down to a snails pace.  It feels like there are far too many processes in place and hurdles to jump over to make changes.  Simple changes to process can take months to be approved and put into place.

Why is no effort made, none at all, to retain staff?  I have suggested several times to different senior management a study be made from the initial advert for a role, to sorting through suitable candidates to initial interview and possible second interview to commencing the role then uniforms, equipment, training etc the costs from the start to becoming ready to fulfil the role.  After all that they leave before 12 months have passed, yet no one asks why?  What can we do?  What can we put in place for you consider staying and for the staff who do stay, why are we supposed to continue year after year in putting up and coping with being always short staffed, picking up shifts on our days off to cover the team to ensure our patients are seen as scheduled, eventually reaching burn out, Yes its real!  But no one ever wants to sort this problem out, we are continually given lip service, borrowing someone from another team, which then leaves them stretched.  I believe this is a suck it up problem which I may add has been going on for the almost [number removed] years I have been in my role.  Only things which do appear to change is the new roles invented for 'the suits' so top heavy, pity the lower level staff don't have this luxury.  You did ask!

Within my current team, a lot of us have young children.  It is really difficult at times due to management not being flexible, meanwhile our current team leader takes their children to school during working hours and nothing is said.  Also, we are now being told when we can and cant use our annual leave.  Currently under threat that we may not get time of in August summer holidays 2025.  We work really hard all year to then be told we cannot have time off with our children during summer holidays.  We have been told we are only allowed x2 band 5 nurses off at once, when there is 17 of us within our team.  Whilst our manager takes all the half terms off as they apparently is not counted in our numbers.  This is causing a lot of staff members to be considering leaving.

Working for the Children in Care team we require full uniform which I fully respect and appreciate as this is a part of representing our organisation (which I am very proud to work for) however, a lot of Children and young people dislike professionals in uniform and often feel targeted when seeing a professional in uniform and feel "different" from others.  I feel being flexible with uniform would highly appreciated for our children and young people and this will reflect that their voices are behind heard regarding seeing professionals.

Would be good if one B7 is more clinical and the other more managerial in CMHT.  B7 clinical lead - can they carry the most complex cases.  Or add another B7 - for most complex case management.  Duty CMHT why is this largely just nurses?  Why are allied health professionals not on this?  We are an MDT?  AHP are used in FRS.  Why are nurses tasked with Social Circumstance reports- instead of social workers?  Concerns raised are taken personally by line management putting people off raising concerns.  Concerns are received defensively.  People just leave.  RADAR forms glitchy to complete.  No feedback received.  Cover for sick staff.  Frequently told you are a B6 senior nurse- interpreted by many as get on with it- rather than a supportive team approach.  Frequent negative emails- don't do this, don't do that, work from office- feelings of mistrust and being watched in the team.  High staff sickness.  Low morale.
